topics.title: Basics of Security and Traditional Cryptosystems: OSI Security Architecture - Security Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT602 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Security and Traditional Cryptosystems: OSI Security Architecture - Security Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 OSI Security Architecture — Formal Definition

The **OSI Security Architecture** is a globally accepted, standardized framework defined by the **ITU-T Recommendation X.800** (year 1991) and equivalently by **ISO 7498-2**. It is the foundational document that classifies **security attacks**, enumerates **security services**, and specifies **security mechanisms** for open systems interconnection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The OSI Security Architecture is the *entry point* of Module 1. The three pillars of X.800 are: **(i) Security Attacks, (ii) Security Services, and (iii) Security Mechanisms**. This topic only covers **(i) Security Attacks** in depth.

### The Three Pillars of ITU-T X.800

| Pillar | Definition | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Security Attack** | Any action that compromises the security of information owned by an organization | Threat modeling |
| **Security Service** | A processing or communication service that enhances the security of the data and information transfer | Countermeasure design |
| **Security Mechanism** | A mechanism designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack | Implementation primitive |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "House Security" Model

Think of your home as an **information system**:

- **House contents** $\rightarrow$ **Sensitive data** (photos, bank documents)
- **Locks on doors** $\rightarrow$ **Confidentiality** (preventing unauthorized viewing)
- **Solid walls** $\rightarrow$ **Integrity** (preventing tampering)
- **Power backup + electricity** $\rightarrow$ **Availability** (ensuring access when needed)
- **Burglar / spy** $\rightarrow$ **Attacker** (eavesdropper, intruder, impersonator)
- **CCTV + alarm** $\rightarrow$ **Security mechanism** (detection)
- **Insurance claim** $\rightarrow$ **Recovery service**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Security is not a single product — it is a **layered architecture** combining prevention, detection, and recovery. The X.800 standard formalizes this layered thinking for computer networks.

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — Foundation of All Security Goals

Every security attack ultimately targets one of the **three pillars of information security**:

- **Confidentiality (C):** Preserving authorized access restrictions on data disclosure.
- **Integrity (I):** Guarding against improper modification or destruction of data.
- **Availability (A):** Ensuring timely and reliable access to and use of information.

Additionally, two extended goals derived from CIA:

- **Authentication:** Verifying the identity of a principal.
- **Non-repudiation:** Ensuring that neither sender nor receiver can falsely deny having sent/received a message.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Security Attack (X.800):**
> "A security attack is any action that compromises the security of information owned by an organization." In other words, an attack is an *assault on system security* derived from an intelligent threat — i.e., an intelligent act that deliberately attempts to evade security services and violate the **CIA** of the system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Security Attacks (X.800 Taxonomy)

X.800 categorizes all attacks into **two broad families** based on whether they **observe** the traffic or **alter** it.

### 2.1.1 Passive Attacks (Eavesdropping Family)

Passive attacks are **monitoring / eavesdropping attacks** whose goal is to *learn* or *make use of* information from the system **without affecting system resources**. They are very difficult to detect because they do **not alter** the data or system behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A person standing outside your house listening to a phone conversation through a window — they are not changing anything, but they are learning private information.

#### Subtype 1 — Release of Message Contents
- The attacker **reads the plaintext content** of a transmission.
- Example: Sniffing unencrypted HTTP traffic on a Wi-Fi hotspot and reading emails in cleartext.
- Violates: **Confidentiality**.

#### Subtype 2 — Traffic Analysis
- The attacker **cannot read the encrypted payload** (because encryption is in place) but observes the **metadata** — frequency of messages, length of messages, source-destination pairs, time of activity.
- Example: Observing encrypted traffic between two military bases to deduce that an operation is being planned, even if the content is unreadable.
- Violates: **Confidentiality** (partial information leakage).

### 2.1.2 Active Attacks (Modification Family)

Active attacks involve **modification of the data stream** or **creation of a false stream**. They are **easier to detect** than passive attacks but far more damaging. They fall into **four classical categories** based on William Stallings' taxonomy (mirroring the CIA + authenticity quadrants).

#### (a) Masquerade
- An entity **pretends to be a different entity**.
- Example: IP spoofing — attacker sends packets with a forged source IP to impersonate a trusted host.
- Violates: **Authenticity / Authentication**.

#### (b) Replay
- An attacker **passively captures a valid transmission** and **retransmits it later** to produce an unauthorized effect.
- Example: Re-sending a previously captured "transfer \$1000" message to drain a bank account again.
- Violates: **Integrity + Authenticity**.

#### (c) Modification of Messages
- An attacker **alters portions of a legitimate message** in transit.
- Example: Man-in-the-middle changing the destination account number in an online banking transaction.
- Violates: **Integrity**.

#### (d) Denial of Service (DoS)
- An attacker **disables or corrupts networks, systems, or services** to render them unavailable to authorized users.
- Example: SYN flood, UDP flood, ping of death, distributed DoS (DDoS).
- Violates: **Availability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (favourite KTU question):**
> - **Passive attacks** are *eavesdropping* — hard to detect, but *preventable* via encryption.
> - **Active attacks** are *modification/fabrication* — hard to prevent, but *detectable* via authentication, digests, and signatures.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

| Attack Type | Subtype | Target Security Goal | Detection Difficulty | Prevention Difficulty | Standard Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passive | Release of message contents | Confidentiality | High | Low–Medium | **Encryption** (symmetric/asymmetric) |
| Passive | Traffic analysis | Confidentiality (partial) | High | Medium | **Traffic padding**, onion routing |
| Active | Masquerade | Authentication | Medium | Medium | **Authentication, digital signatures** |
| Active | Replay | Authenticity + Integrity | Medium | Medium | **Timestamps, sequence numbers, nonces** |
| Active | Modification of messages | Integrity | Low | Low | **Message authentication codes (MAC), digital signatures** |
| Active | Denial of Service | Availability | Low | High | **Firewalls, rate limiting, redundancy** |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network forensics** uses traffic-analysis patterns to identify anomalies.
- **TLS/SSL** counters release-of-contents attacks via symmetric encryption (AES) and asymmetric key exchange (RSA/ECDHE).
- **Banking systems** use sequence numbers and one-time tokens to defeat replay attacks.
- **Cloud DDoS protection services** (e.g., Cloudflare, AWS Shield) directly counter DoS attacks using scrubbing centers.
- **Firewalls and IDS/IPS** systems implement Stallings' taxonomy in their rule engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** No single mechanism defeats all attacks. Defense must be **defense-in-depth** — multiple layers combining prevention (encryption), detection (IDS), and recovery (backups, redundancy).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **analytical breakdown** of attack types. Since this topic is **conceptual / architectural** (not a closed-form numerical problem), the "derivation" here is the **logical classification hierarchy** and the **CIA-impact reasoning** required to answer KTU essay questions.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: Why X.800 Splits Attacks into Two Families

**Step 1 — Define the goal of an attacker.**
The universal objective of a security attack is to **breach one or more CIA properties** of the target system.

**Step 2 — Determine whether the attacker alters the data path.**
- If the attacker **only observes** $\rightarrow$ *passive family* (read-only).
- If the attacker **alters / injects / disrupts** $\rightarrow$ *active family* (read-write).

**Step 3 — Within each family, identify the *specific mechanism* used.**
- Passive $\rightarrow$ *release of contents* (plaintext) or *traffic analysis* (ciphertext metadata).
- Active $\rightarrow$ four canonical subtypes: *masquerade, replay, modification, DoS*.

**Step 4 — Map each subtype to the violated CIA pillar.**

This gives the analytical matrix:

$$
\text{Attack} = \underbrace{\{\text{Passive, Active}\}}_{\text{Family}} \times \underbrace{\{\text{Contents, Traffic, Masquerade, Replay, Modification, DoS}\}}_{\text{Subtype}} \mapsto \text{CIA pillar}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Impact Matrix (CIA Mapping)

| Attack Subtype | Confidentiality (C) | Integrity (I) | Availability (A) | Authenticity |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Release of message contents | $\bigotimes$ breached | — | — | — |
| Traffic analysis | $\bigtriangleup$ partial | — | — | — |
| Masquerade | — | — | — | $\bigotimes$ breached |
| Replay | — | $\bigotimes$ breached | — | $\bigotimes$ breached |
| Modification of messages | — | $\bigotimes$ breached | — | — |
| Denial of Service | — | — | $\bigotimes$ breached | — |

> **Legend:** $\bigotimes$ = direct breach, $\bigtriangleup$ = partial breach, — = not directly impacted.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — "Replay Attack Lifecycle"

The examiner often asks *"Explain replay attack with an example."* The **step-by-step lifecycle** is:

**Step 1 — Observe (passive phase):**
Attacker Eve places a packet sniffer on the network and **captures a valid authenticated message** $M$ sent by Alice to Bob (e.g., a banking transaction "Transfer ₹50,000 to Account 1234").

**Step 2 — Store:**
Eve stores the entire message — header, body, and authentication tag — verbatim.

**Step 3 — Replay (active phase):**
Eve **re-injects** the captured message $M$ at a later time $t' \gg t$, addressing it to Bob's server.

**Step 4 — Server processing:**
If Bob's server has **no replay protection**, it treats $M$ as a fresh legitimate request and **executes the transaction again**, transferring money to Eve's chosen account.

**Step 5 — Countermeasure (design implication):**
Replay protection requires embedding one of:

$$
\text{Nonce} \in \mathbb{Z}_n \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Sequence Number} \in \mathbb{N} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Timestamp} \in \mathbb{R}^+
$$

such that no message can be **validly reused** after the freshness window expires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical expression of "freshness":**
> A message $M_i$ is **fresh** iff:
> $$ \text{seq}(M_i) > \text{seq}_{\max}^{\text{last accepted}} \quad \text{or} \quad \vert t_{\text{now}} - t_{M_i} \vert < \Delta t_{\text{window}} $$
> Use `\vert` notation in inline LaTeX to avoid markdown table conflicts (per Section II formatting rule).

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Reasoning for "DoS Attack" Effects

**Step 1 — Resource identification:** Attacker identifies a *bottleneck resource* (bandwidth, CPU, memory pool, connection state table).

**Step 2 — Resource exhaustion:** A flood of requests consumes the resource to its saturation limit.

**Step 3 — Legitimate request starvation:** Genuine users' requests are dropped or delayed beyond acceptable timeouts.

**Step 4 — Recovery:** Requires redundant resources, scrubbing centers, and rate-limiting policies.

$$
\text{Service Downtime} = f(\text{attack rate}, \text{legitimate load}, \text{resource headroom})
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Security Attacks (X.800)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Security Attacks (X.800)"] --> B["Passive Attacks"]
    A --> C["Active Attacks"]

    B --> B1["Release of Message Contents"]
    B --> B2["Traffic Analysis"]

    C --> C1["Masquerade"]
    C --> C2["Replay"]
    C --> C3["Modification of Messages"]
    C --> C4["Denial of Service"]

    B1 --> X1["Target: Confidentiality"]
    B2 --> X2["Target: Confidentiality (partial)"]
    C1 --> X3["Target: Authentication"]
    C2 --> X4["Target: Integrity and Authentication"]
    C3 --> X5["Target: Integrity"]
    C4 --> X6["Target: Availability"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    style B2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    style C1 fill:#f4b6b6,stroke:#7a0000,color:#000000
    style C2 fill:#f4b6b6,stroke:#7a0000,color:#000000
    style C3 fill:#f4b6b6,stroke:#7a0000,color:#000000
    style C4 fill:#f4b6b6,stroke:#7a0000,color:#000000
    style X1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style X2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style X3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style X4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style X5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style X6 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
```

> **How to read the diagram:** Blue nodes = passive family, Red nodes = active family, Yellow leaves = violated CIA pillar. This single figure is sufficient to answer 7 marks of a 14-mark KTU essay question.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Replay Attack Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice
    participant Network
    participant Eve as Eve (Attacker)
    participant Bob as Bob (Server)

    Alice->>Network: Step 1 - Send message M with valid auth tag
    Network->>Eve: Step 2 - Eve sniffs and stores M
    Network->>Bob: Step 3 - Bob receives M and processes once
    Note over Bob: Transaction executed once

    Eve->>Network: Step 4 - Re-inject stored M at time t prime
    Network->>Bob: Step 5 - Bob receives identical M
    Note over Bob: Without replay protection - re-executes transaction

    Bob-->>Eve: Step 6 - Server response (fraud success)

    style Eve fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a0000
    style Alice fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f4e79
    style Bob fill:#548235,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2d4a17
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Defense System

This block diagram maps the **functional placement of each X.800 security service / mechanism** within a network stack, showing how attacks are countered at each layer.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph App["Application Layer"]
        APP1["Email / Web / Banking"]
        M1["Digital Signature - counters Modification and Masquerade"]
    end

    subgraph Trans["Transport Layer"]
        T1["TLS / SSL"]
        M2["MAC - counters Modification and Replay"]
    end

    subgraph Net["Network Layer"]
        N1["IPSec"]
        M3["AH and ESP headers - counters Masquerade and Replay"]
    end

    subgraph Link["Data Link Layer"]
        L1["Wired and Wireless LAN"]
        M4["WPA2 Encryption - counters Release of Contents"]
    end

    APP1 --> T1 --> N1 --> L1
    M1 -.protects.-> APP1
    M2 -.protects.-> T1
    M3 -.protects.-> N1
    M4 -.protects.-> L1

    Attacker["Attacker - Passive and Active"] -.targets.-> APP1
    Attacker -.targets.-> T1
    Attacker -.targets.-> N1
    Attacker -.targets.-> L1

    style Attacker fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:3px
    style APP1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#1f4e79
    style T1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#1f4e79
    style N1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#1f4e79
    style L1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#1f4e79
    style M1 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style M2 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style M3 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style M4 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The attacker (red block) attempts to strike every layer. Each X.800 mechanism (green) is *plugged* into the appropriate layer to counter the relevant attack subtype. This figure is also a model answer seed for a 14-mark question on "Explain the OSI Security Architecture with a neat block diagram."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate clearly between passive and active security attacks. Which is harder to detect and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Eavesdropping / monitoring | Modification / fabrication |
| Effect on data | Data is **not altered** | Data is **altered, injected, or destroyed** |
| Detection | Very difficult (no trace) | Easier (visible anomalies) |
| Prevention | Encryption, traffic padding | Authentication, integrity checks, redundancy |
| CIA breach | Confidentiality | Integrity, Availability, Authenticity |
| Example | Sniffing a Wi-Fi conversation | IP spoofing, DoS flood |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Passive attacks are *harder to detect* because they leave no trace, but are *easier to prevent* with encryption. Active attacks are *easier to detect* but *harder to prevent*.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is a "replay attack"? Mention any one method to prevent it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **replay attack** is an active security attack in which the attacker **passively captures a valid data transmission** and subsequently **re-transmits it verbatim** to trick the receiver into performing an unauthorized operation.

- **Example:** Re-sending a previously valid OTP or "Transfer ₹5000" message to a banking server.
- **CIA violation:** Integrity and Authenticity.
- **Prevention method (any one):**
  1. **Sequence numbers** — every message is assigned a strictly increasing integer; the receiver rejects duplicates.
  2. **Timestamps** — each message carries a timestamp; the receiver accepts only messages within a freshness window $\Delta t$.
  3. **Nonces** — sender includes a one-time random number $N$ that the receiver tracks in a cache.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024]**

**(a) Describe the OSI Security Architecture (ITU-T X.800) in detail. List the three key aspects defined by it. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **OSI Security Architecture**, formally known as **ITU-T Recommendation X.800** (1991) and equivalent to **ISO 7498-2**, is the international standard that provides a unified framework for security in open systems. It addresses security concerns for the **OSI reference model** and has been widely adopted for TCP/IP-based networks as well.

**The Three Key Aspects (3 marks):**

1. **Security Attack** — Any action that compromises the security of information.
2. **Security Service** — A service that enhances the security of data transfer and processing. (Examples: confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation, access control, availability.)
3. **Security Mechanism** — A mechanism used to implement one or more security services. (Examples: encipherment, digital signature, access control, data integrity, authentication exchange, traffic padding, routing control, notarization.)

**Purpose of X.800 (2 marks):**
- Provides a **common vocabulary** for security professionals.
- Assists in the **systematic design** of security features for products and organizations.
- Helps in **evaluating** whether a product meets the security requirements.

**Why it is important (2 marks):**
- OSI is the basis for the modern Internet, so X.800's terminology is the universal language of network security.
- Maps **attacks → services → mechanisms** as a clear defense hierarchy.

---

**(b) Classify security attacks and explain each active attack subtype with a real-world example. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

X.800 classifies attacks as **passive** and **active**.

**Passive Attacks (2 marks — brief recap):**
1. **Release of message contents** — reading plaintext. *Example:* Sniffing HTTP traffic at a coffee-shop Wi-Fi.
2. **Traffic analysis** — observing metadata. *Example:* Monitoring encrypted VoIP call durations between two diplomats.

**Active Attacks (5 marks — full explanation):**

1. **Masquerade (1 mark):**
   - One entity pretends to be another.
   - *Example:* IP spoofing — attacker sends packets with forged source IP to impersonate a bank server.
   - Counter: **Authentication, digital certificates (PKI).**

2. **Replay (1 mark + 1 mark):**
   - Attacker captures and re-sends a valid message.
   - *Example:* Re-transmitting a captured OTP or a "transfer funds" request.
   - Counter: **Timestamps, nonces, sequence numbers.**

3. **Modification of Messages (1 mark + 1 mark):**
   - Some portion of a legitimate message is altered.
   - *Example:* Man-in-the-middle modifies the destination account number in an online payment.
   - Counter: **Message Authentication Code (MAC), hash functions, digital signatures.**

4. **Denial of Service (1 mark + 1 mark):**
   - Service is made unavailable to legitimate users.
   - *Example:* SYN flood attack saturating a web server's TCP connection table.
   - Counter: **Firewalls, rate limiting, scrubbing centers, redundant infrastructure.**

**Comparison line (free 0.5 mark):** Active attacks are easier to detect but harder to prevent; passive attacks are harder to detect but easier to prevent.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the OSI Security Architecture with a neat block diagram and discuss the relationship between security attacks, services, and mechanisms. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **OSI Security Architecture (X.800)** is the formal framework that classifies and addresses security concerns in networked open systems. It defines three principal concepts and the relationship between them.

**The Triangular Relationship (4 marks):**

$$
\text{Security Attacks} \xrightarrow{\text{Threat}} \text{System} \xleftarrow{\text{Counter}} \text{Security Services} \xleftarrow{\text{Implement}} \text{Security Mechanisms}
$$

- An **attack** is a violation of security.
- A **service** counteracts the attack by enhancing security.
- A **mechanism** is the technical means to provide the service.

**Block Diagram (in words, 2 marks):**

| Layer | Security Concern | Service Used | Mechanism Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | Data integrity, authenticity | Digital signature service | Asymmetric crypto (RSA) |
| Transport | Session confidentiality | Confidentiality service | TLS (AES, ChaCha20) |
| Network | Packet spoofing | Authentication service | IPSec (AH, ESP) |
| Data Link | Wireless sniffing | Confidentiality service | WPA2 (AES-CCMP) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The architecture emphasizes **defense-in-depth** — multiple mechanisms and services at multiple layers are required to counter multiple attack types.

---

**(b) With a suitable example for each, explain the following: (i) Release of message contents, (ii) Traffic analysis, (iii) Masquerade, (iv) Replay. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Release of Message Contents (1.5 marks):**
The attacker reads the plaintext of a transmission.
*Example:* A student uses Wireshark on a college LAN to capture and read an unencrypted email containing a friend's password.

**(ii) Traffic Analysis (1.5 marks):**
The message content is encrypted and unreadable, but the attacker deduces information from metadata — message size, timing, frequency, source-destination.
*Example:* An adversary monitoring an encrypted link between a head office and a factory infers that a major production launch is imminent because the data rate tripled overnight.

**(iii) Masquerade (2 marks):**
An entity pretends to be a different entity to gain unauthorized access.
*Example:* An attacker sends a TCP SYN packet to a server with a forged source IP address equal to that of the administrator, then hijacks the session.

**(iv) Replay (2 marks):**
An attacker captures a legitimate message and retransmits it later to obtain an unauthorized effect.
*Example:* A malicious user records a valid 4-digit OTP for a ₹10,000 transaction and submits the same OTP again within the validity window to fraudulently authorize a second transaction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing passive and active attack definitions** — passive means *observation only*, NOT *less harmful*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting that X.800 = ITU-T X.800 = ISO 7498-2** — quoting only one source loses a mark; examiners expect *both* references.
> 3. **Writing examples without naming the violated CIA pillar** — every attack example MUST be tagged with "this violates Confidentiality / Integrity / Availability / Authenticity."
> 4. **Not drawing the attack-taxonomy diagram** — a 14-mark essay question on this topic *demands* a labelled diagram of the attack classification. Missing it = -3 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up "detection" and "prevention"** — passive attacks are *hard to detect, easier to prevent*; active attacks are *easier to detect, harder to prevent*. Memorize this line; it scores bonus marks.
> 6. **Skipping countermeasures** — listing attacks without their corresponding security service/mechanism loses 2 marks in Part B answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **OSI Security Architecture** = **ITU-T X.800** = **ISO 7498-2** (year 1991) — memorize all three names.
- The three pillars of X.800 are: **Attacks**, **Services**, **Mechanisms**.
- **CIA Triad** is the universal yardstick: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** (plus Authentication and Non-repudiation as extended goals).
- **Passive attacks** = eavesdropping = *Release of message contents* and *Traffic analysis*; hard to detect, easier to prevent via encryption.
- **Active attacks** = *Masquerade*, *Replay*, *Modification of messages*, *Denial of Service*; easier to detect, harder to prevent.
- **DoS** is the only standard attack subtype in this module that **directly breaches Availability**.
- **Replay** is the only attack that **passively captures and then actively re-injects** — it bridges both worlds.
- **Traffic analysis** is the only attack that succeeds even when encryption is properly applied.
- **Countermeasure key words** to remember: **Encryption** (release of contents), **Traffic padding** (traffic analysis), **Authentication / Digital signature** (masquerade), **Nonce / Timestamp / Sequence number** (replay), **MAC / Hash / Digital signature** (modification), **Firewall / Rate limiting / Redundancy** (DoS).
- **One-line mantra for the exam:** *"Passive = read, Active = write."*
- **Diagram policy:** Always draw a labelled attack-taxonomy diagram in 14-mark answers.
- **Countermeasure policy:** Always pair every attack with a security service and a mechanism.
- **Valuation policy:** Always tag every example with the violated CIA pillar.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT602 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Information Security, a **Security Service** is a processing or communication service that is provided by a system to give a specific kind of protection to the system resources. Security services implement security policies and are implemented using security mechanisms. They are designed to counter security attacks and enforce the security goals of an organization.

According to the **ITU-T X.800 Recommendation (Security Architecture for OSI)** and **RFC 2828**, security services enhance the security of the data processing systems and the information transfers of an organization. These services are intended to counteract security threats, and they make use of one or more security mechanisms to provide the service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 - Services):**
> The X.800 standard defines **FIVE primary security services**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation, and Access Control. A sixth service, **Availability**, is also widely recognized and forms the core of what is often called the **CIA Triad plus Authentication/Non-repudiation**.

## 1.2 The Six Fundamental Security Services

| # | Service | Core Purpose |
|---|---------|--------------|
| 1 | **Data Confidentiality** | Protects data from unauthorized disclosure |
| 2 | **Data Integrity** | Ensures data is not altered in transit or at rest |
| 3 | **Authentication** | Verifies the identity of communicating entities |
| 4 | **Non-Repudiation** | Prevents denial of actions performed |
| 5 | **Access Control** | Regulates who can access what resources |
| 6 | **Availability** | Ensures systems and data are accessible when needed |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The High-Security Bank Vault

Imagine a **bank vault** in a major metropolitan city. The vault itself doesn't just have one lock — it has multiple layers of protection, each designed to prevent a specific type of attack:

- **Confidentiality** → The vault's **steel walls and combination lock** ensure that only authorized people can see what's inside. Nobody can peek in and learn the contents.
- **Integrity** → The **sealed deposit boxes with tamper-evident seals** ensure that once a customer places something inside, it cannot be modified without leaving evidence.
- **Authentication** → The **ID card check + biometric fingerprint scanner at the door** verifies that the person claiming to be a bank employee is actually who they say they are.
- **Non-Repudiation** → The **CCTV camera with timestamped recordings** ensures that if Mr. X withdrew money at 3:15 PM, he cannot later deny it — the camera is proof.
- **Access Control** → The **permission matrix** that defines which employee can access which customer's box (only managers can access safety deposit boxes, only tellers can access cash drawers).
- **Availability** → The **backup power generator, redundant server systems, and 24/7 armed guards** ensure the vault and its services are always operational, even during a power outage or a robbery attempt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Anchor:** Just as a bank doesn't rely on a single security measure, an information system must deploy multiple security services in layers. This is the **Defense-in-Depth** principle — one service alone is never enough.

## 1.4 Why Security Services Matter (The Big Picture)

Every modern digital transaction — whether it's a UPI payment, an Aadhaar authentication, an email exchange, or a video call — relies on these services operating correctly in the background. When a security service fails, the impact can be catastrophic:

- **Failure of Confidentiality** → Data breach (e.g., the 2017 Equifax breach exposing **147 million** personal records)
- **Failure of Integrity** → Manipulation of financial records, election tampering
- **Failure of Authentication** → Identity theft, phishing attacks
- **Failure of Non-Repudiation** → Disputed digital signatures, fraud
- **Failure of Access Control** → Privilege escalation attacks
- **Failure of Availability** → **DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) attacks** shutting down websites

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Information Security is fundamentally a service-oriented discipline. The services listed above are the **deliverables** that a security architect promises to the organization. Every firewall, every encryption algorithm, every authentication protocol is ultimately a *mechanism* used to deliver one or more of these *services*.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Security Service

### 2.1.1 Data Confidentiality

**Definition:** The property that information is not made available or disclosed to unauthorized individuals, entities, or processes.

**Operational Logic:**
- Protects against **snooping, eavesdropping, and unauthorized disclosure**
- Applies to data in three states:
  1. **Data at rest** (stored in databases, hard drives)
  2. **Data in transit** (traveling over networks)
  3. **Data in use** (being processed in memory)

**Techniques used:** Encryption (AES, RSA), access control lists, steganography, traffic padding

**Levels of Confidentiality Protection:**
- **Connection Confidentiality:** Protects all data on a connection
- **Connectionless Confidentiality:** Protects individual messages/PDUs
- **Selective-Field Confidentiality:** Protects only specific fields in a message
- **Traffic-Flow Confidentiality:** Hides the source, destination, frequency, and length of communication

### 2.1.2 Data Integrity

**Definition:** The property that data has not been modified or destroyed in an unauthorized manner.

**Operational Logic:**
- Two sub-categories:
  1. **Connection Integrity with Recovery:** Detects and attempts to recover from integrity violations
  2. **Connection Integrity without Recovery:** Detects but does not recover
  3. **Connectionless Integrity:** Integrity applied to individual messages
  4. **Selective-Field Integrity:** Integrity applied to specific fields

**Techniques used:** Hash functions (SHA-256, MD5), MAC (Message Authentication Codes), digital signatures, CRCs

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):** Integrity protects against **modification, insertion, deletion, and replay** of data — NOT against disclosure. Disclosure is the job of Confidentiality.

### 2.1.3 Authentication

**Definition:** The corroboration that an entity is the one claimed.

**Two Sub-types:**
1. **Peer Entity Authentication:** Verifies the identity of a communicating entity during a connection (e.g., verifying that the user logging into a server is indeed who they claim to be across a multi-packet session)
2. **Data Origin Authentication:** Verifies the source/origin of a specific data unit (e.g., verifying that an email came from the person whose address is in the "From" field)

**Techniques used:** Passwords, biometrics, digital certificates, challenge-response protocols, Kerberos, OAuth tokens

### 2.1.4 Non-Repudiation

**Definition:** Protection against false denial by one of the parties involved in a communication of having participated in all or part of the communication.

**Two Sub-types:**
1. **Non-Repudiation of Origin:** Protects against the sender's false denial of having sent the message
2. **Non-Repudiation of Receipt:** Protects against the receiver's false denial of having received the message

**Techniques used:** Digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA), timestamping, trusted third-party notaries, blockchain receipts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Non-repudiation is what makes digital signatures legally binding under the **IT Act 2000** in India. Without non-repudiation, a digital contract would be unenforceable.

### 2.1.5 Access Control

**Definition:** The prevention of unauthorized use of a resource, including the prevention of use of a resource in an unauthorized manner.

**Operational Logic:**
- Enforces the **principle of least privilege**
- Involves determining **WHO** can do **WHAT** to **WHICH** resources
- Two main operations:
  1. **Authorization:** Determining what an authenticated user is allowed to do
  2. **Access enforcement:** Enforcing those authorization decisions

**Techniques used:** ACLs, RBAC (Role-Based Access Control), ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control), capability lists, firewalls, IAM systems

### 2.1.6 Availability

**Definition:** The property of being accessible and usable on demand by an authorized entity.

**Operational Logic:**
- Protects against:
  - **Denial of Service (DoS)** attacks
  - **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)** attacks
  - Hardware/software failures
  - Natural disasters
- Ensures **timely, reliable access** to resources

**Techniques used:** Redundancy (RAID, failover clusters), backups, UPS, DDoS mitigation services (Cloudflare, Akamai), disaster recovery plans, RAID arrays

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA Triad Context:** Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability are the **three pillars** of the classic CIA Triad. Authentication, Non-Repudiation, and Access Control are the **supporting services** that make CIA achievable in practice.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet: Service vs. Attack vs. Mechanism

| Security Service | Security Threats Countered | Security Mechanisms Used |
|------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Confidentiality** | Snooping, traffic analysis, eavesdropping | Encryption, steganography, access control |
| **Integrity** | Modification, masquerading, replay, insertion, deletion | Hashing, MAC, digital signatures, CRCs |
| **Authentication** | Masquerading, impersonation | Passwords, biometrics, digital certificates |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Repudiation of origin, repudiation of receipt | Digital signatures, notarization, timestamps |
| **Access Control** | Unauthorized use, misuse of resources | ACLs, RBAC, capability lists, firewalls |
| **Availability** | DoS, DDoS, system failure, disasters | Redundancy, backups, RAID, DDoS mitigation, UPS |

## 2.3 X.800 Service Categories — Hierarchical View

The ITU-T X.800 framework organizes the six services into a clear hierarchy:

$$
\text{Security Services} = \begin{cases} \text{Data Confidentiality} \\ \text{Data Integrity} \\ \text{Authentication} \\ \text{Non-Repudiation} \\ \text{Access Control} \\ \text{Availability} \end{cases}
$$

Each service is **independent** — meaning that one service does not automatically provide another. For example, encryption provides confidentiality, but **does not** automatically provide integrity or authentication. To get all three, you need to combine encryption (for confidentiality) with a MAC (for integrity) and a digital signature (for authentication and non-repudiation).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These six services form the **language of security engineering**. When a CTO asks "What is our security posture?", the CISO responds by mapping these services to:
- **Compliance frameworks** (ISO 27001, NIST 800-53, PCI-DSS, GDPR)
- **Risk assessment matrices**
- **Security architecture diagrams**
- **Incident response plans**

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum, every subsequent module — from cryptography (Module 2) to network security (Module 3) — will be a deeper exploration of the **mechanisms** that implement these six **services**. Understanding them thoroughly now is non-negotiable.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Service-Attack Mapping

## 3.1 Exhaustive Service-Attack Coverage Matrix

The following analytical matrix maps every major **security attack** to the **specific service** that counters it, along with the **mechanism** that implements that service. This is a high-yield KTU examination construct.

| Attack Name | Description | Service Defeated | Mechanism Used |
|-------------|-------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Snooping / Eavesdropping** | Passive interception of data | Confidentiality | Encryption (AES, 3DES) |
| **Traffic Analysis** | Inferring info from traffic patterns | Confidentiality (Traffic-Flow) | Traffic padding, onion routing |
| **Modification** | Altering message contents in transit | Integrity | MAC, digital signatures, hashing |
| **Masquerading** | Pretending to be another entity | Authentication + Access Control | Digital certificates, passwords, biometrics |
| **Replay Attack** | Re-sending a captured valid message | Integrity (specifically) | Timestamps, sequence numbers, nonces |
| **Insertion / Deletion** | Adding/removing messages in a stream | Integrity | Sequence numbers, integrity checks |
| **Denial of Service (DoS)** | Flooding a system to make it unavailable | Availability | Rate limiting, filtering, redundancy |
| **DDoS** | Distributed DoS from botnets | Availability | CDN, DDoS scrubbing centers, Anycast |
| **Repudiation of Origin** | Sender denies sending a message | Non-Repudiation of Origin | Digital signatures (with trusted CA) |
| **Repudiation of Receipt** | Receiver denies getting a message | Non-Repudiation of Receipt | Acknowledgment with digital signature |
| **Unauthorized Access** | Accessing resources without permission | Access Control | ACLs, RBAC, capability lists |
| **Elevation of Privilege** | Gaining higher privileges than allowed | Access Control | Principle of least privilege, sandboxing |

## 3.2 Comparative Analysis: Authentication vs. Non-Repudiation

This is a **frequently tested distinction** in KTU exams. Let us derive the difference analytically.

**Step 1 — Define the temporal scope:**

$$
\text{Authentication Scope} = \text{Single Session or Message Verification}
$$

$$
\text{Non-Repudiation Scope} = \text{Permanent, Legally Binding Proof}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the time of validation:**

$$
\text{Authentication Validation} = \text{Real-time (at the moment of connection or message receipt)}
$$

$$
\text{Non-Repudiation Validation} = \text{Anytime in the future (can be verified days/years later)}
$$

**Step 3 — Analyze the cryptographic strength required:**

For **Authentication** alone, symmetric MACs are sufficient:

$$
\text{MAC} = H(K, M) \quad \text{where } H \text{ is a hash function, } K \text{ is the shared secret, } M \text{ is the message}
$$

For **Non-Repudiation**, asymmetric digital signatures are required:

$$
\text{Signature} = S_d(M) = D_{priv}(H(M)) \quad \text{verified by } S_e(V) = E_{pub}(V) = H(M)
$$

where $S_d$ is signing with the private key and $S_e$ is verification with the public key. The key difference is that with a MAC, the **same key** signs and verifies, so the receiver could forge a message. With a digital signature, only the sender has the private key, so the receiver **cannot** forge — hence non-repudiation.

**Step 4 — Conclude the operational difference:**

| Dimension | Authentication | Non-Repudiation |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Who can verify? | Only the intended recipient (with shared secret) | Anyone with the public key |
| Can the recipient forge? | Yes (in symmetric MAC) | No (in asymmetric signature) |
| Legal binding? | No | Yes (under IT Act 2000) |
| Cryptographic primitive | MAC, hash + shared key | Digital signature, hash + asymmetric keypair |
| Replay protection? | Depends on protocol | Implicit (if timestamped) |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Relationship Between CIA Triad and Supporting Services

We will now derive mathematically why the three additional services (Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control) are **necessary extensions** of the CIA Triad.

**Step 1 — Start with the CIA Triad:**

$$
\text{Security}_{\text{core}} = \{C, I, A\}
$$

where $C$ = Confidentiality, $I$ = Integrity, $A$ = Availability.

**Step 2 — Identify the gap:**

Confidentiality and Integrity, by themselves, do not answer the question:

$$
\text{"How do we know the entity we are communicating with is legitimate?"}
$$

This is the **identity gap**. Without identity, confidentiality and integrity can be subverted by a **masquerader** who:
1. Establishes a fake "secure" channel (violating $C$ and $I$)
2. Exfiltrates the data while appearing to be a legitimate user

**Step 3 — Add Authentication to close the identity gap:**

$$
\text{Security}_{\text{extended}} = \{C, I, A, \text{Auth}\}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the accountability gap:**

After authentication, we have:

$$
\text{"How do we prove the entity performed the action, even if they later deny it?"}
$$

This is the **accountability gap**. Without non-repudiation, a sender can claim "I never sent that message" and the receiver cannot prove otherwise in a court of law.

**Step 5 — Add Non-Repudiation to close the accountability gap:**

$$
\text{Security}_{\text{full}} = \{C, I, A, \text{Auth}, NR\}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the authorization gap:**

Finally, even an authenticated, non-repudiable entity should not be able to access **all** resources:

$$
\text{"How do we restrict what an authenticated user can do?"}
$$

This is the **authorization gap**, filled by Access Control.

**Step 7 — Conclude the complete service model:**

$$
\text{Security}_{\text{complete}} = \{C, I, A, \text{Auth}, NR, \text{AccessControl}\}
$$

This six-service model is the **complete information security service framework** that students must memorize for KTU examinations.

## 3.4 Service-to-Layer Mapping in the OSI Model

Each security service operates at a specific layer (or layers) of the OSI model. This is a frequently tested mapping:

| OSI Layer | Security Services Typically Implemented |
|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| **Application (7)** | Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control (e.g., S/MIME, PGP, HTTPS) |
| **Presentation (6)** | Confidentiality (e.g., TLS encryption) |
| **Session (5)** | Authentication (session establishment) |
| **Transport (4)** | Confidentiality, Integrity (e.g., TLS/SSL, SSH) |
| **Network (3)** | Authentication, Access Control (e.g., IPsec, firewalls) |
| **Data Link (2)** | Confidentiality, Integrity (e.g., WPA2, MACsec) |
| **Physical (1)** | Availability (physical security, redundancy) |
