**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **overall structure of AES-128 encryption**, indicating the role of each transformation. Clearly state the number of rounds and the size of the expanded key. (7 marks)

**(b)** Illustrate the **MixColumns transformation** of AES with the multiplication matrix. Compute the result of MixColumns when the first column of the State is $[db, 13, 53, 45]^T$. Show the GF(2⁸) multiplication steps using the xtime function. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Overall structure (3 marks)**

AES-128 is a 10-round SPN cipher. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) shows:
- Initial AddRoundKey (round 0)
- 9 rounds of SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey
- Final round of SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey (MixColumns omitted)

**Step 2: Parameters (2 marks)**

- Block size: 128 bits
- Key size: 128 bits ($Nk = 4$)
- Number of rounds: $Nr = 10$
- Expanded key size: $Nb(Nr+1) \times 4 = 4 \times 11 \times 4 = 176$ bytes
- Round key size: 128 bits each, 11 round keys total

**Step 3: Role of each transformation (2 marks)**

- **SubBytes** — Confusion via non-linear S-box
- **ShiftRows** — Inter-column byte diffusion
- **MixColumns** — Intra-column byte diffusion via GF(2⁸) matrix
- **AddRoundKey** — Confuses the state with round-specific key material

#### Model Solution for Part B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: MixColumns Matrix (1 mark)**

$$S'_{col} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} db \\ 13 \\ 53 \\ 45 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute Row 0 — $s'_0$ (2 marks)**

$$s'_0 = (02 \cdot db) \oplus (03 \cdot 13) \oplus (01 \cdot 53) \oplus (01 \cdot 45)$$

- $02 \cdot db$: $db = 11011011_2$, MSB=1, so $db \ll 1 = 10110110_2 \oplus 00011011 = 10101101_2 = 8E$
- $03 \cdot 13 = (02 \cdot 13) \oplus 13$: $13 = 00010011_2$, MSB=0, so $13 \ll 1 = 00100110_2 = 26$; $26 \oplus 13 = 35$
- $01 \cdot 53 = 53$
- $01 \cdot 45 = 45$

$$s'_0 = 8E \oplus 35 \oplus 53 \oplus 45$$

$8E \oplus 35 = BB$ (since $10001110 \oplus 00110101 = 10111011$)
$BB \oplus 53 = E8$ (since $10111011 \oplus 01010011 = 11101000$)
$E8 \oplus 45 = AD$ (since $11101000 \oplus 01000101 = 10101101$)

$$\boxed{s'_0 = AD}$$

**Step 3: Compute Row 1 — $s'_1$ (2 marks)**

$$s'_1 = (01 \cdot db) \oplus (02 \cdot 13) \oplus (03 \cdot 53) \oplus (01 \cdot 45)$$
$$= db \oplus 26 \oplus (02 \cdot 53 \oplus 53) \oplus 45$$

- $02 \cdot 53$: $53 = 01010011_2$, MSB=0, so $53 \ll 1 = A6$
- $03 \cdot 53 = A6 \oplus 53 = F5$

$$s'_1 = db \oplus 26 \oplus F5 \oplus 45$$

$db \oplus 26 = FD$
$FD \oplus F5 = 08$
$08 \oplus 45 = 4D$

$$\boxed{s'_1 = 4D}$$

**Step 4: Compute Row 2 — $s'_2$ (1 mark)**

$$s'_2 = (01 \cdot db) \oplus (01 \cdot 13) \oplus (02 \cdot 53) \oplus (03 \cdot 45)$$
$$= db \oplus 13 \oplus A6 \oplus (02 \cdot 45 \oplus 45)$$

- $02 \cdot 45$: $45 = 01000101_2$, MSB=0, so $45 \ll 1 = 8A$
- $03 \cdot 45 = 8A \oplus 45 = CF$

$$s'_2 = db \oplus 13 \oplus A6 \oplus CF$$
$db \oplus 13 = C8$
$C8 \oplus A6 = 6E$
$6E \oplus CF = A1$

$$\boxed{s'_2 = A1}$$

**Step 5: Compute Row 3 — $s'_3$ (1 mark)**

$$s'_3 = (03 \cdot db) \oplus (01 \cdot 13) \oplus (01 \cdot 53) \oplus (02 \cdot 45)$$
$$= (8E \oplus db) \oplus 13 \oplus 53 \oplus 8A$$

$8E \oplus db = 55$
$55 \oplus 13 = 46$
$46 \oplus 53 = 15$
$15 \oplus 8A = 9F$

$$\boxed{s'_3 = 9F}$$

**Final Mixed Column:** $[AD, \; 4D, \; A1, \; 9F]^T$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on AES Questions:**
> 1. **MixColumns in the last round** — The most common error. The 10th round of AES-128 has **only 3 transformations** (no MixColumns). Examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this.
> 2. **State byte ordering** — AES uses **column-major** order, not row-major. If you write the State as $S[r][c] = in[4r + c]$, you will get every subsequent byte in the wrong position. Lose **1 mark per error**.
> 3. **Key Expansion index confusion** — When $i \bmod Nk = 0$ (i.e., $i = 4, 8, 12, ...$), the round constant applied is $Rcon[i/Nk]$, **not** $Rcon[i]$. Forgetting this shifts all subsequent words. Lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **No S-box multiplication in GF(2⁸)** — The S-box uses **multiplicative inverse + affine**, NOT a simple substitution table. If your answer treats S-box as a "lookup only," you will lose the **construction marks (3 of 7)** in SubBytes questions.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the polynomial** — In GF(2⁸) multiplications, you must mention the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$. Examiners allot **1 mark** for this specification alone.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The 60-Second AES Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [ ] AES is a **128-bit block, symmetric-key, SPN cipher** with **128/192/256-bit** keys giving **10/12/14** rounds.
- [ ] State is a **4×4 byte matrix** in **column-major** order.
- [ ] **Four transformations** per round: **SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey** (in that order).
- [ ] **MixColumns is OMITTED in the final round** — this is the single most-tested KTU fact.
- [ ] **SubBytes** = multiplicative inverse in GF(2⁸) + affine transformation with constant $63_{hex}$.
- [ ] **ShiftRows** offsets: row 0 → 0, row 1 → 1, row 2 → 2, row 3 → 3 (left shifts).
- [ ] **MixColumns** matrix: $M$ with entries $02, 03, 01, 01$ in cyclic pattern. $03 \cdot a = (02 \cdot a) \oplus a$ via **xtime**.
- [ ] **AddRoundKey** = simple XOR with 128-bit round key.
- [ ] **Key Expansion** = $Nb(Nr+1) = 44$ words for AES-128 = **176 bytes**.
- [ ] **Key Expansion recurrence** has two cases: when $i \bmod Nk = 0$ use RotWord + SubWord + Rcon; otherwise plain XOR.
- [ ] **Rcon** starts with $RC[1] = 01$ and doubles in GF(2⁸) using polynomial $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.
- [ ] First 10 round constants: 01, 02, 04, 08, 10, 20, 40, 80, **1B**, 36.
- [ ] **Decryption** uses **InvSubBytes, InvShiftRows, InvMixColumns, AddRoundKey** in **reverse order**, with equivalent inverse cipher using **InvMixColumns** on the round keys.
- [ ] AES is **not a Feistel network** — it is an SPN. This is structurally different from DES.
- [ ] AES provides **confusion (S-box)** and **diffusion (ShiftRows + MixColumns)**, the two Shannon requirements.
- [ ] Memory layout: $w[0..3]$ = original key; $w[4..7]$ = round 1 key; …; $w[40..43]$ = round 10 key.
- [ ] The **AES polynomial** is $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ (hex: $11B$). It governs all byte arithmetic.
- [ ] **Brute-force resistance**: AES-128 = $2^{128}$ keys, AES-256 = $2^{256}$ keys.
- [ ] **Practical use**: TLS 1.3, WPA3, BitLocker, FileVault, hardware AES-NI instructions.
- [ ] **Key takeaway for viva**: "Why SPN and not Feistel?" — SPN allows **parallel** byte-level processing in hardware, giving AES a multi-Gbps throughput advantage over 3DES.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Cipher Modes of Operation - Electronic Codebook Mode (ECB), Cipher Block Chaining Mode (CBC), Cipher Feedback Mode (CFB), Output Feedback Mode (OFB), Counter Mode (CTR). Stream Ciphers - Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT602 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Block Cipher Modes of Operation - Electronic Codebook Mode (ECB), Cipher Block Chaining Mode (CBC), Cipher Feedback Mode (CFB), Output Feedback Mode (OFB), Counter Mode (CTR). Stream Ciphers - Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Block Cipher Mode of Operation?

A **Block Cipher Mode of Operation** is a standardized algorithm (defined by **NIST SP 800-38A**) that describes how to repeatedly apply a deterministic *b*-bit block cipher (e.g., AES with $b=128$ bits, DES with $b=64$ bits) to securely transform a message that is longer than a single block. A raw block cipher can only encrypt one fixed-size block; the mode of operation is the **wrapper logic** that handles multi-block plaintexts $P$, initialization values, and chaining feedback to provide **confidentiality, integrity resilience, and error propagation properties**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A block cipher (like AES or DES) by itself is NOT secure for arbitrary-length messages. Without a mode of operation, identical plaintext blocks will produce identical ciphertext blocks, leaking patterns. The mode is what makes block ciphers **practically usable** in real protocols like TLS, IPsec, and disk encryption.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Sealing Stamps" Model

Imagine a secure courier service (the **block cipher**) that can only seal individual boxes of *exactly 64 chocolates* (one block). A customer has 500 chocolates to ship in different boxes.

- **ECB Mode (Electronic Codebook):** Each box is sealed independently. Two boxes of the *same brand* of chocolate look identical from outside — a spy can detect patterns. ❌ *Insecure for repetitive data.*
- **CBC Mode (Cipher Block Chaining):** Each box is sealed, then its *seal stamp* is smudged onto the *next* unsealed box before sealing, creating a chain. Change one box, every box after looks different. ✅ *Strongest classical mode.*
- **CFB/OFB/CTR Modes:** Instead of sealing full boxes, the courier uses a **continuous tape stream** of pseudo-random seal patterns mixed with chocolates, turning the block cipher into a **stream cipher**.

### 1.3 Formal Definition of Each Mode

| Mode | Acronym Meaning | Type | Block Cipher Used As |
|------|----------------|------|---------------------|
| **Electronic Codebook** | ECB | Deterministic, non-chained | Direct encryptor |
| **Cipher Block Chaining** | CBC | Chained, IV-based | Direct encryptor |
| **Cipher Feedback** | CFB | Stream-oriented | Pseudo-random generator |
| **Output Feedback** | OFB | Stream-oriented | Keystream generator |
| **Counter Mode** | CTR | Stream-oriented | Keystream generator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Initialization Vector (IV):** A non-secret random or pseudo-random block of $b$ bits used to randomize encryption so that identical plaintexts encrypt to different ciphertexts. The IV is required to be **unpredictable** in CBC/CFB and **unique (nonce)** in CTR/OFB.

### 1.4 What is a Stream Cipher?

A **Stream Cipher** is a symmetric-key cipher that encrypts individual bits or bytes of plaintext one at a time by combining the plaintext with a **pseudo-random keystream** generated using the secret key. The structure is fundamentally:

$$C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$$

where $K_i$ is the $i^{th}$ keystream symbol. CFB, OFB, and CTR are essentially **ways to convert a block cipher into a stream cipher**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Structure of a Stream Cipher:** It consists of three subsystems — (1) a **Key/IV Setup Unit**, (2) a **State Update / Keystream Generator Function**, and (3) a **Mixing Function (XOR)**. The output keystream is XORed with plaintext to produce ciphertext. Modern stream ciphers (ChaCha20, Salsa20) follow this exact architecture.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mode 1 — Electronic Codebook (ECB)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Divide plaintext $P$ into $n$ blocks of $b$ bits each: $P = P_1 \parallel P_2 \parallel \dots \parallel P_n$.
2. Pad the last block if shorter than $b$ bits (PKCS#7 / ANSI X.923).
3. Encrypt each block independently: $C_i = E_K(P_i)$.
4. Decryption: $P_i = D_K(C_i)$.

**Key Properties:**
- **Parallelizable** (both encryption and decryption) — fastest mode.
- **Deterministic**: identical $P_i \Rightarrow$ identical $C_i$ (vulnerability!).
- **No error propagation**: a 1-bit error in $C_i$ only corrupts $P_i$.
- **Insecure for images, files with repetition** (the famous "ECB Tux penguin" attack).

### 2.2 Mode 2 — Cipher Block Chaining (CBC)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Choose random $b$-bit IV. Set $C_0 = IV$.
2. For each block: $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$.
3. Decryption: $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$.

**Key Properties:**
- Encryption is **sequential**; decryption is **parallelizable**.
- **Randomized**: changing IV changes all ciphertext.
- **Error propagation**: a 1-bit error in $C_i$ corrupts block $i$ completely, and flips the **same bit** in $P_{i+1}$ (self-healing from $i+2$).
- Requires **padding**.

### 2.3 Mode 3 — Cipher Feedback (CFB)

**Operational Logic (full-block CFB, $s = b$):**
1. Shift register $S_1 = IV$.
2. Keystream block $O_i = E_K(S_i)$.
3. Ciphertext: $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$.
4. Update shift register: $S_{i+1} = S_i \ll s \mid C_i$ (drop leftmost $b-s$ bits, append $s$ bits of $C_i$).

**Generalized $s$-bit CFB:** Operates on $s$ bits at a time ($1 \le s \le b$), turning the block cipher into a self-synchronizing stream cipher.

**Key Properties:**
- **Self-synchronizing**: if ciphertext bits are lost, the receiver resyncs after $b/s$ blocks.
- Encryption is sequential; decryption is parallelizable.
- Error in $C_i$ corrupts block $i$ and partially $i+1$.
- No padding required (stream operation).

### 2.4 Mode 4 — Output Feedback (OFB)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Set $O_0 = IV$ (or $O_0 = E_K(IV)$).
2. For each block: $O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$ — keystream block.
3. Ciphertext: $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$.

**Key Properties:**
- **Keystream is independent of plaintext and ciphertext** — useful for pre-computation.
- **No error propagation** (errors in $C_i$ affect only $P_i$).
- Encryption and decryption are both parallelizable (since $O_i$ depends only on $O_{i-1}$).
- Synchronous stream cipher mode.
- IV must be a **nonce** (Number Used Once).

### 2.5 Mode 5 — Counter (CTR)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Choose a unique counter $T_1$ (e.g., IV/nonce concatenated with 0).
2. For each block: $O_i = E_K(T_i)$, where $T_{i+1} = T_i + 1$.
3. Ciphertext: $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$.

**Key Properties:**
- Fully **parallelizable** (both encryption and decryption).
- **Random access** to any block (e.g., for disk encryption).
- No padding needed.
- Provably secure under standard assumptions (IND-CPA).
- Used in **AES-GCM** (Galois Counter Mode) — the modern gold standard.

### 2.6 Stream Cipher — Internal Structure

A stream cipher has the canonical architecture:

**Components:**
1. **Key Loading Unit:** Absorbs the secret key $K$ (typically 128 or 256 bits).
2. **Initialization Vector (Nonce) Unit:** Provides uniqueness for each session.
3. **State Register:** A secret internal state that evolves.
4. **Update Function $f$:** Transforms state: $S_{i+1} = f(S_i, K)$.
5. **Output/Keystream Function $g$:** Extracts keystream: $K_i = g(S_i, K)$.
6. **Mixing (XOR):** $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Families of Stream Ciphers:**
> - **Synchronous Stream Ciphers (SSC):** Keystream depends only on key and IV, not on plaintext. E.g., OFB, CTR.
> - **Self-Synchronizing Stream Ciphers (SSSC):** Keystream depends on previous ciphertext. E.g., CFB.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Mode | Encryption Formula | Decryption Formula | Parallelizable? | Padding? |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------|----------|
| **ECB** | $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ | $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ | ✅ Enc & Dec | ✅ Required |
| **CBC** | $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ | $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$ | ❌ Enc / ✅ Dec | ✅ Required |
| **CFB** | $C_i = E_K(S_i) \oplus P_i$ | $P_i = E_K(S_i) \oplus C_i$ | ❌ Enc / ✅ Dec | ❌ Not required |
| **OFB** | $O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$; $C_i = O_i \oplus P_i$ | $P_i = O_i \oplus C_i$ | ✅ (precompute $O_i$) | ❌ Not required |
| **CTR** | $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$ | $P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$ | ✅ Enc & Dec | ❌ Not required |

**Where:**
- $E_K$ = block cipher encryption under key $K$
- $D_K$ = block cipher decryption under key $K$
- $C_0$ or $O_0$ or $S_1$ = Initialization Vector (IV), of size **b bits** (typically 128 bits for AES)
- $T_i$ = Counter value, $T_{i+1} = T_i + 1$
- $b$ = block size (128 for AES, 64 for DES)
- $s$ = segment size in CFB (in bits)
- $\oplus$ = bitwise XOR operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> - **ECB:** Only used to encrypt single random values (e.g., RSA-blinded values), never message streams.
> - **CBC:** TLS 1.2 cipher suites, IPsec ESP, older disk encryption (BitLocker v1).
> - **CFB:** Legacy systems, satellite communications.
> - **OFB:** Pre-computed keystream channels (e.g., noisy channels with burst errors).
> - **CTR / GCM:** **TLS 1.3**, HTTPS, modern disk encryption (AES-XTS), 5G security.
> - **Stream ciphers:** Mobile networks (ChaCha20), RC4 (deprecated, WiFi WEP), mobile A5/1.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 ECB Mode — Full Encryption/Decryption Walkthrough

**Given:** Plaintext $P = P_1 \parallel P_2 \parallel \dots \parallel P_n$, each $P_i$ is $b$ bits. Secret key $K$.

**Step 1 — Block Partitioning:**
The plaintext is split:

$$P = \underbrace{P_1}_{b \text{ bits}} \parallel \underbrace{P_2}_{b \text{ bits}} \parallel \dots \parallel \underbrace{P_n}_{b \text{ bits}}$$

If $\vert P \vert \not\equiv 0 \pmod b$, padding is appended. Let $\vert P \vert$ be the bit-length, then number of blocks:

$$n = \left\lceil \frac{\vert P \vert}{b} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Encryption of each block:**

$$C_1 = E_K(P_1)$$
$$C_2 = E_K(P_2)$$
$$\vdots$$
$$C_n = E_K(P_n)$$

The ciphertext is the concatenation:

$$C = C_1 \parallel C_2 \parallel \dots \parallel C_n$$

**Step 3 — Decryption (no chaining):**

$$P_i = D_K(C_i), \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

**Analytical Conclusion:** Because $E_K$ is deterministic, **if $P_i = P_j$, then $C_i = C_j$**. This is the source of the pattern-leak vulnerability — the mode does not hide block-level repetition.

### 3.2 CBC Mode — Full Derivation with Chaining

**Given:** IV of $b$ bits, $P = P_1 \parallel P_2 \parallel \dots \parallel P_n$, key $K$.

**Step 1 — Initialize the chain:**
Set $C_0 = IV$ (transmitted in cleartext or pre-shared).

**Step 2 — Recursive encryption:**

$$C_1 = E_K(P_1 \oplus C_0)$$
$$C_2 = E_K(P_2 \oplus C_1)$$
$$\vdots$$
$$C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$$

**Step 3 — Decryption derivation:**
Since $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$, applying $D_K$ to both sides:

$$D_K(C_i) = P_i \oplus C_{i-1}$$

To recover $P_i$, XOR with $C_{i-1}$:

$$P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$$

**Step 4 — Error propagation analysis:**
Suppose bit $j$ of $C_i$ flips due to channel noise. Then:
- $P_i$: completely corrupted (since $D_K$ is highly non-linear, a 1-bit flip diffuses to ~50% of bits).
- $P_{i+1}$: only bit $j$ is flipped (because $C_i$ is used in XOR with $D_K(C_{i+1})$).
- $P_{i+2}, P_{i+3}, \dots$: **unaffected** (self-healing from block $i+2$).

### 3.3 CFB Mode — Derivation of the Self-Synchronizing Stream

**Given:** Shift register initialized to $S_1 = IV$, segment size $s$ bits.

**Step 1 — Generate keystream block:**

$$O_i = E_K(S_i)$$

**Step 2 — Encrypt $s$ bits of plaintext:**

$$C_i = P_i \oplus (\text{leftmost } s \text{ bits of } O_i)$$

**Step 3 — Update shift register:**
Shift $S_i$ left by $s$ bits, then append $C_i$ (as a new $s$-bit segment):

$$S_{i+1} = \left( S_i \ll s \right) \oplus C_i$$

For full-block CFB where $s = b$, this simplifies to $S_{i+1} = C_i$.

**Step 4 — Decryption derivation:**
Receiver re-computes the same keystream $O_i$ because $S_i$ depends only on the *received* $C_{i-1}$. Then:

$$P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$$

**Step 5 — Self-synchronization proof:**
If a burst of $r$ bits is lost, the receiver's $S_i$ will be misaligned for $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks. After that, the shift register realigns to the sender's because both use the *same* (now-resumed) ciphertext. Hence the cipher **resyncs automatically**.

### 3.4 OFB Mode — Keystream Independence Derivation

**Given:** $O_0 = E_K(IV)$ (initial value).

**Step 1 — Recursive keystream generation:**

$$O_1 = E_K(O_0)$$
$$O_2 = E_K(O_1)$$
$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$$

**Step 2 — Encryption by XOR:**

$$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$$

**Step 3 — Decryption (identical operation):**

$$P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$$

**Step 4 — Key Pre-computation Property:**
Since $O_i$ depends only on $K$ and $O_0$ (not on $P_i$ or $C_i$), the entire keystream can be pre-computed **offline** before plaintext is even known. This is a major speedup for real-time streaming systems.

**Step 5 — Error propagation:**
A 1-bit error in $C_i$ affects only the corresponding bit in $P_i$ — no other blocks are corrupted. This makes OFB ideal for noisy channels (e.g., satellite links) where CBC's error multiplication is undesirable.

### 3.5 CTR Mode — Counter-Based Keystream

**Given:** Initial counter $T_1 = \text{Nonce} \parallel 0$ (e.g., 96-bit nonce + 32-bit counter for AES).

**Step 1 — Encrypt each counter value:**

$$O_1 = E_K(T_1)$$
$$O_2 = E_K(T_2) = E_K(T_1 + 1)$$
$$O_i = E_K(T_i)$$

**Step 2 — XOR with plaintext:**

$$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$$

**Step 3 — Decryption by re-encryption of counters:**

$$P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$

**Step 4 — Random access proof:**
To decrypt block $j$ only, the receiver computes $E_K(T_j)$ directly — no need to process blocks $1, 2, \dots, j-1$. This is the basis of **disk sector encryption** (e.g., AES-XTS uses CTR-like random access).

**Step 5 — Nonce uniqueness criticality:**
If the same $(K, T_i)$ pair is ever reused across two different messages, the XOR of the two plaintexts leaks. Hence the **nonce + counter must never repeat** under the same key.

### 3.6 Stream Cipher — Full Structural Derivation

A stream cipher is defined by a tuple of algorithms:

$$\mathcal{SC} = (\text{Setup}, \text{Update}, \text{Output}, \text{Encrypt}, \text{Decrypt})$$

**Step 1 — Setup (Key + IV loading):**
Load secret key $K$ and public nonce $N$ into internal state $S_0$:

$$S_0 = \text{Setup}(K, N)$$

**Step 2 — Keystream update recursion:**

$$S_{i+1} = f(S_i)$$

For CFB: $S_{i+1} = (S_i \ll s) \oplus C_i$ (ciphertext-dependent — *self-synchronizing*).
For OFB: $S_{i+1} = E_K(S_i)$ (ciphertext-independent — *synchronous*).
For CTR: $S_{i+1} = S_i + 1$ (counter increment — *synchronous*).

**Step 3 — Keystream extraction:**

$$k_i = g(S_i)$$

For CFB/OFB/CTR built from a block cipher, $g(S_i) = E_K(S_i)$.

**Step 4 — XOR mixing (the encryption itself):**

$$C_i = P_i \oplus k_i$$

**Step 5 — Invertibility (decryption):**
Since XOR is its own inverse, decryption uses **the same keystream**:

$$P_i = C_i \oplus k_i$$

**Step 6 — Period and security:**
A stream cipher is secure iff its keystream has:
- Long period (ideally $> 2^{128}$)
- High linear complexity
- Statistical properties indistinguishable from random
- **Keystream must never be reused** under the same key (the "two-time pad" attack).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Security Property:** Stream ciphers are vulnerable to a **bit-flipping attack** if used WITHOUT a MAC. An attacker who flips bit $j$ of $C_i$ flips bit $j$ of $P_i$ — no authentication is provided. Hence CTR is always used in authenticated form (GCM, CCM) in modern protocols.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ECB Mode — Block Diagram (Independent Block Encryption)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plaintext Block P1] -->|E_K| C1[Ciphertext Block C1]
    P2[Plaintext Block P2] -->|E_K| C2[Ciphertext Block C2]
    P3[Plaintext Block P3] -->|E_K| C3[Ciphertext Block C3]
    Pn[Plaintext Block Pn] -->|E_K| Cn[Ciphertext Block Cn]
    
    K[(Secret Key K)] -.-> P1
    K -.-> P2
    K -.-> P3
    K -.-> Pn
```

### 4.2 CBC Mode — Chained Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IV[IV / C0] --> XOR1((XOR))
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> E1[Block Cipher E_K]
    E1 --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    
    C1 --> XOR2((XOR))
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> E2[Block Cipher E_K]
    E2 --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
    
    C2 --> XOR3((XOR))
    P3[Plaintext P3] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> E3[Block Cipher E_K]
    E3 --> C3[Ciphertext C3]
    
    K[(Key K)] -.-> E1
    K -.-> E2
    K -.-> E3
```

### 4.3 CFB Mode — Self-Synchronizing Stream Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Shift Register S1 equals IV] --> E1[Block Cipher E_K]
    E1 --> O1[Keystream O1]
    O1 --> XOR1((XOR))
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    C1 --> SHIFT[Shift left by s bits]
    SHIFT --> S2[Shift Register S2]
    
    S2 --> E2[Block Cipher E_K]
    E2 --> O2[Keystream O2]
    O2 --> XOR2((XOR))
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
    
    K[(Key K)] -.-> E1
    K -.-> E2
```

### 4.4 OFB Mode — Synchronous Keystream Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O0[O0 equals E_K of IV] --> E1[Block Cipher E_K]
    E1 --> O1[Keystream O1]
    O1 --> E2[Block Cipher E_K]
    E2 --> O2[Keystream O2]
    O2 --> E3[Block Cipher E_K]
    E3 --> O3[Keystream O3]
    
    O1 --> XOR1((XOR))
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    
    O2 --> XOR2((XOR))
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
    
    O3 --> XOR3((XOR))
    P3[Plaintext P3] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> C3[Ciphertext C3]
    
    K[(Key K)] -.-> E1
    K -.-> E2
    K -.-> E3
```

### 4.5 CTR Mode — Counter-Based Parallel Stream

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Counter T1] --> E1[Block Cipher E_K]
    T2[Counter T2 = T1+1] --> E2[Block Cipher E_K]
    T3[Counter T3 = T2+1] --> E3[Block Cipher E_K]
    
    E1 --> O1[Keystream O1]
    E2 --> O2[Keystream O2]
    E3 --> O3[Keystream O3]
    
    O1 --> XOR1((XOR))
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    
    O2 --> XOR2((XOR))
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
    
    O3 --> XOR3((XOR))
    P3[Plaintext P3] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> C3[Ciphertext C3]
    
    K[(Key K)] -.-> E1
    K -.-> E2
    K -.-> E3
```

### 4.6 Generic Stream Cipher — Master Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K[(Secret Key K)] --> SETUP[Setup Unit]
    N[(Nonce / IV)] --> SETUP
    SETUP --> S0[Initial State S0]
    S0 --> UPDATE[Update Function f]
    UPDATE --> S1[State S1]
    S1 --> OUTPUT[Output Function g]
    OUTPUT --> KS[Keystream k_i]
    S1 --> UPDATE
    
    KS --> XOR((XOR))
    P[Plaintext P_i] --> XOR
    XOR --> C[Ciphertext C_i]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by a block cipher mode of operation? Why is ECB mode considered insecure for encrypting long messages?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A block cipher mode of operation is a standardized procedure (defined by NIST SP 800-38A) that specifies how to apply a fixed-size block cipher (e.g., AES with 128-bit blocks) to encrypt plaintexts longer than one block. ECB (Electronic Codebook) encrypts each block independently: $C_i = E_K(P_i)$.
ECB is insecure because encryption is **deterministic** — identical plaintext blocks yield identical ciphertext blocks, leaking patterns. An attacker observing ciphertext repetition can infer plaintext repetition. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for ECB flaw and example of attack like the "ECB penguin" image.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between synchronous and self-synchronizing stream ciphers with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **synchronous stream cipher** generates the keystream independently of the plaintext and ciphertext; both sender and receiver must remain perfectly synchronized. Examples: OFB, CTR, ChaCha20.
A **self-synchronizing stream cipher** generates the keystream as a function of the previous ciphertext bits, so it automatically resynchronizes after a limited number of dropped or corrupted bits. Example: CFB mode.
(1 mark each for definitions, 1 mark for examples.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) Mode** of operation with a neat block diagram. Derive the encryption and decryption equations and discuss the error propagation properties.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **CFB, OFB, and CTR** modes in terms of their structure, parallelizability, error propagation, and use of padding. State two real-world applications of CTR mode.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CBC Mode [7 marks]:**

- **Diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.2 diagram showing $C_{i-1}$ fed back as XOR input before $E_K$.]
- **Encryption equations (2 marks):**
  $C_0 = IV$, and for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:
  $$C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$$
- **Decryption equations (1 mark):**
  $$P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$$
  **Derivation:** Apply $D_K$ to both sides: $D_K(C_i) = P_i \oplus C_{i-1}$, then XOR with $C_{i-1}$ yields $P_i$.
- **Error propagation (2 marks):** A 1-bit flip in $C_i$ causes complete corruption of $P_i$ (block-level avalanche) and flips the same bit position in $P_{i+1}$. From block $i+2$ onwards, the cipher **self-heals** because $C_{i+1}$ is used intact.

**(b) CFB vs OFB vs CTR [7 marks]:**

| Property | CFB | OFB | CTR |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Structure** | Self-synchronizing, ciphertext-fed | Synchronous, output-fed | Synchronous, counter-fed |
| **Block cipher used as** | PRNG + XOR | PRNG (pure keystream) | PRNG (counter) |
| **Parallelizable Encryption** | ❌ (depends on $C_{i-1}$) | ❌ (keystream sequential) | ✅ (each block independent) |
| **Parallelizable Decryption** | ✅ (needs $C_{i-1}$ which is known) | ✅ (precompute keystream) | ✅ (independent counters) |
| **Error Propagation** | Affects block $i$ fully, partial $i+1$ | Only affects corresponding bit in $P_i$ | Only affects corresponding bit in $P_i$ |
| **Padding Required** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Random Access** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

**Real-world applications of CTR (1 mark):**
1. **TLS 1.3** uses AES-GCM (CTR-based) for all cipher suites.
2. **Disk encryption** (AES-XTS) and **IPsec ESP** use CTR for random sector access.

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a clear block diagram, describe the **Counter (CTR) Mode** of operation. Explain why CTR mode is fully parallelizable and supports random access decryption. Discuss the security requirement on the counter values.

**(b) [7 marks]** What is a **stream cipher**? Draw the generic block diagram and explain the role of each component. Why must the keystream never be reused under the same key?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CTR Mode [7 marks]:**

- **Diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.5 diagram. Show parallel $E_K(T_i)$ blocks feeding parallel XOR with $P_i$.]
- **Encryption equation (2 marks):**
  $$C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i), \quad T_{i+1} = T_i + 1$$
- **Decryption equation (1 mark):**
  $$P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$
- **Parallelizability & Random Access (1 mark):** Each block $i$ depends only on the deterministic counter $T_i$. Encryption/decryption of block $j$ requires no other block — receiver can decrypt sector $j$ of a disk without processing sectors $0$ to $j-1$.
- **Counter security requirement (1 mark):** The counter (or nonce-counter combination) must be **unique for every plaintext block under the same key**. If $T_i$ is ever reused, two ciphertexts XORed together yield the XOR of the two plaintexts (catastrophic plaintext recovery).

**(b) Stream Cipher [7 marks]:**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A symmetric cipher that encrypts one bit/byte of plaintext at a time by XORing with a pseudo-random keystream $K_i$ derived from the key.
- **Block diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.6 diagram showing Setup → Update → Output → XOR.]
- **Component roles (3 marks):**
  - **Setup Unit:** Mixes key and IV/nonce into initial state.
  - **Update Function $f$:** Evolves the internal state (e.g., $S_{i+1} = f(S_i)$).
  - **Output Function $g$:** Extracts keystream bit/block $k_i = g(S_i)$.
  - **XOR Mixing:** $C_i = P_i \oplus k_i$ (the actual encryption).
- **Keystream reuse problem (1 mark):** If two plaintexts $P$ and $P'$ are encrypted with the same keystream $K$ under the same key, then $C \oplus C' = P \oplus P'$, leaking both plaintexts (the famous "two-time pad" or "many-time pad" attack that broke Soviet VENONA and modern WEP/WPA2-TKIP).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Writing $C_i = E_K(P_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$ for CBC. **Correct:** $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ — the XOR must happen *before* $E_K$, not after.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–2 marks):** Confusing OFB and CFB error propagation. OFB = no propagation, CFB = self-synchronizing after $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** Saying CTR mode needs padding. It does NOT — CTR is a stream mode.
> - **Common Mistake #4 (–2 marks):** Forgetting the IV requirement for CBC/CFB (must be unpredictable) versus CTR/OFB (must be a unique nonce, not necessarily unpredictable).
> - **Common Mistake #5 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to state that ECB reveals patterns in image/structured data. Always cite the penguin example.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
>
> ✅ A block cipher mode of operation enables encryption of multi-block messages; **ECB is the simplest but least secure** (deterministic).
>
> ✅ **CBC** chains ciphertext blocks via XOR; provides **confidentiality** but is **sequential** for encryption. A 1-bit error corrupts one block + flips one bit in next = **self-healing**.
>
> ✅ **CFB** turns a block cipher into a self-synchronizing stream cipher; keystream depends on **previous ciphertext**; resyncs after $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks.
>
> ✅ **OFB** generates a **plaintext-independent keystream** via repeated $E_K$; no error propagation; keystream can be **pre-computed**.
>
> ✅ **CTR** encrypts **counters** $T_i$ and XORs with plaintext; **fully parallelizable**, supports **random access**, no padding — the modern preferred mode (used in GCM, AES-GCM-256 in TLS 1.3).
>
> ✅ **Stream cipher structure** = Setup(K, N) → Update(f) → Output(g) → XOR with plaintext. Canonical equation: $C_i = P_i \oplus k_i$.
>
> ✅ **Synchronous** stream cipher (OFB, CTR) vs **Self-synchronizing** (CFB).
>
> ✅ **IV vs Nonce:** CBC/CFB need unpredictable random IV; CTR/OFB need unique nonce. **Never reuse nonce in CTR/OFB under same key** (two-time pad attack).
>
> ✅ **Authentication:** None of these modes provide authentication by themselves. Modern use mandates AEAD: **AES-GCM** (CTR + GHASH), **AES-CCM**, **ChaCha20-Poly1305**.
>
> ✅ **NIST Standard:** Modes defined in **NIST SP 800-38A** (ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR). GCM defined in **NIST SP 800-38D**.
>
> ✅ **Key fact for viva:** Why not just use ECB? "Identical plaintext blocks → identical ciphertext blocks" — leaks statistical patterns. Famous example: encrypting a bitmap image with ECB reveals the original picture (the ECB Tux penguin).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Key Cryptosystems: Introduction to Public Key Cryptosystems - Principles, Applications, Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCBT602 - INFORMATION SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Public Key Cryptosystems: Introduction to Public Key Cryptosystems - Principles, Applications, Requirements # Module 3 — Public Key Cryptosystems: Introduction ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (Stallings / KTU Syllabus Standard):** > *A Public-Key Cryptosystem (PKC) is an asymmetric cryptographic scheme that uses a pair of mathematically related keys — a **Public Key** ($K_U$) openly distributed for encryption/verification, and a **Private Key** ($K_R$) kept secret by the owner for decryption/signing — such that it is computationally infeasible to derive the private key from the public key, enabling secure communication, digital signatures, and key exchange without a shared secret.* The discipline is therefore termed **Asymmetric Cryptography**, in contrast to the classical **Symmetric (Secret-Key) Cryptography** where a single key is used for both encryption and decryption. | Symbol | Meaning | Visibility | |:------:|:-------:|:----------:| | $K_U$ | Public Key (User's Public Key) | Open to all | | $K_R$ | Private Key (User's Secret/Private Key) | Confidential to owner only | | $E$ | Encryption Algorithm | Public | | $D$ | Decryption Algorithm | Private to owner | | $M$ | Plaintext Message | Variable | | $C$ | Ciphertext | Transmitted publicly | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Asymmetric"?** Because the key used to lock (encrypt) is *different* from the key used to unlock (decrypt), unlike the symmetric case where both operations use the **same** secret key $K$. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mailbox Model Imagine a transparent, locked mailbox mounted on your house wall, with a wide open slot on top. * **The Slot = Public Key ($K_U$)**: Anyone in the world can walk up and drop a letter inside (encryption). The slot's shape (key) is published openly on a directory. * **The Locked Door & Your Physical Key = Private Key ($K_R$)**: Only you possess the physical key. Even though everyone can deposit letters, **only you** can open the door and read them (decryption). * **Reverse Use Case (Digital Signatures)**: You write a letter, seal it with your **personal locked stamp** (sign with $K_R$), and post it. Anyone can verify that *you* wrote it because they have your public verification tool ($K_U$) — but nobody else can forge your stamp. > [!TIP] > **Memory Anchor for Exam:** "Public Locks, Private Opens" — the public key encrypts (locks), the private key decrypts (opens). For signatures this flips: private signs, public verifies. --- ## 1.3 The Five Foundational Ingredients of a Public-Key Scheme According to the KTU 2024 syllabus and William Stallings' standard text, every public-key cryptosystem is built upon **six ingredients**: 1. **Plaintext ($M$)** — the readable message input. 2. **Encryption Algorithm ($E$)** — performs mathematical transformation using $K_U$. 3. **Ciphertext ($C$)** — the scrambled output transmitted over an insecure channel. 4. **Decryption Algorithm ($D$)** — recovers plaintext using $K_R$. 5. **Public Key ($K_U$)** — publicly known; used by senders. 6. **Private Key ($K_R$)** — secret; used only by the receiver. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Mathematical Property:** > $$D_{K_R}(E_{K_U}(M)) \;=\; M$$ > That is, applying the decryption algorithm with the *correct* private key on the ciphertext produced by the encryption algorithm with the matching public key recovers the original plaintext exactly. --- ## 1.4 Conventional vs. Public-Key Cryptography — At a Glance | Parameter | Conventional (Symmetric) | Public-Key (Asymmetric) | |:----------|:------------------------:|:-----------------------:| | **Keys Used** | Single shared secret $K$ | Pair: $K_U$ (public) and $K_R$ (private) | | **Key Distribution** | Major problem — must be secret | Solved — public key openly shared | | **Number of Keys for $n$ users** | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2n$ | | **Speed** | Very fast (1000x–10000x faster) | Slow due to heavy math | | **Primary Service** | Confidentiality (encryption) | Confidentiality + Signatures + Key Exchange | | **Key Length (Typical)** | **128 / 256 bits** | **1024 / 2048 / 4096 bits** (RSA), **256 bits** (ECC) | | **Algorithms** | AES, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, ElGamal, Diffie-Hellman | | **Trust Requirement** | Shared secret trust | Trust in authenticity of public key (PKI) | # Module 3 — Deep Theoretical Analysis ## 2.1 The Core Principles of Public-Key Cryptography Public-Key Cryptography rests on **three foundational pillars**. Master these — they are the most repeated lines in KTU question papers. ### Pillar 1: One-Way Function (OWF) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** A function $f : X \rightarrow Y$ is a **one-way function** if for every $x \in X$, $f(x)$ is *easy* to compute, but for *almost every* $y \in \text{Im}(f)$, it is computationally infeasible to find any $x$ such that $f(x) = y$. **Plain English:** Easy to compute forward, practically impossible to invert. **Examples of hard one-way problems (used in real cryptosystems):** * **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)** — basis of RSA. Given $n = p \cdot q$ (product of two large primes), finding $p$ and $q$ is hard. * **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — basis of Diffie-Hellman, ElGamal, DSA. Given $g, \; g^x \bmod p$, finding $x$ is hard. * **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** — basis of ECC. * **Knapsack Problem** (historically used, now broken). ### Pillar 2: Trapdoor One-Way Function > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** A trapdoor one-way function is a one-way function $f_K$ that *becomes easy to invert* if and only if a special secret piece of information (the **trapdoor**, i.e., the private key $K_R$) is known. **Plain English:** Like a one-way street with a hidden side door — impossible to traverse without the secret, trivial with it. For RSA: the trapdoor is the knowledge of the prime factorization $p, q$ of $n$. ### Pillar 3: Asymmetric Key Pair The keys $K_U$ and $K_R$ are **mathematically linked** but **practically un-linkable** by an attacker: * Given $K_U$, deriving $K_R$ is computationally infeasible. * Operations performed with $K_U$ are reversible only with $K_R$. --- ## 2.2 Three Canonical Applications of PKC Per Stallings (and the KTU Module 3 syllabus), public-key cryptography enables **three primary application classes**: ### Application 1: Encryption / Decryption The sender encrypts using the receiver's **public key** $K_{U_B}$; the receiver decrypts using their own **private key** $K_{R_B}$. $$C \;=\; E_{K_{U_B}}(M)$$ $$M \;=\; D_{K_{R_B}}(C) \;=\; D_{K_{R_B}}\bigl(E_{K_{U_B}}(M)\bigr)$$ **Use:** Confidentiality of message content. ### Application 2: Digital Signature The sender *signs* using their **private key** $K_{R_A}$; any receiver can *verify* using the sender's **public key** $K_{U_A}$. $$\text{Signature} \;=\; S_{K_{R_A}}(M)$$ $$\text{Verified?} \;=\; V_{K_{U_A}}(M, S)$$ **Provides:** **Authentication**, **Integrity**, and **Non-Repudiation**. ### Application 3: Key Exchange / Key Establishment Two parties jointly establish a shared secret over an insecure channel *without* prior shared secrets. Example: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange produces a common session key $K_S$. **Provides:** Confidential symmetric key for subsequent high-speed symmetric encryption (the typical **hybrid cryptosystem** pattern). --- ## 2.3 The Five Cryptographic Requirements for a PKC (HIGH-YIELD!) > [!IMPORTANT] > These are the *exact* five requirements Stallings lists and that KTU examiners love to ask. Memorize them word-for-word. For a public-key scheme to be *practically secure*, the following must hold: 1. **Computationally easy** for a party $B$ to generate a key pair $(K_{U_B}, K_{R_B})$. 2. **Computationally easy** for a sender $A$ to encrypt: $C = E_{K_{U_B}}(M)$, knowing $K_{U_B}$ and $M$. 3. **Computationally easy** for the receiver $B$ to decrypt: $M = D_{K_{R_B}}(C)$, using $K_{R_B}$. 4. **Computationally infeasible** for an adversary, knowing $K_{U_B}$, to recover $M$ from $C$ (i.e., to invert the encryption without $K_{R_B}$). 5. **Computationally infeasible** for an adversary, knowing $K_{U_B}$, to recover $K_{R_B}$. > Two additional desirable (often implicitly assumed) conditions: 6. The encryption and decryption functions can be applied in **either order**: $$M \;=\; E_{K_{U_B}}\bigl(D_{K_{R_B}}(M)\bigr) \;=\; D_{K_{R_B}}\bigl(E_{K_{U_B}}(M)\bigr)$$ This property is essential for **digital signatures**. 7. Existence of a useful **public-key directory / Public-Key Infrastructure (PKI)** for authentic distribution of public keys. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Plain-English Meaning | |:-:|:--------|:----------------------:|:----------------------| | 1 | Encryption | $C = E_{K_U}(M)$ | Lock plaintext with receiver's public key | | 2 | Decryption | $M = D_{K_R}(C)$ | Open ciphertext with receiver's private key | | 3 | Round-trip Identity | $D_{K_R}(E_{K_U}(M)) = M$ | Locks and keys fit perfectly | | 4 | Reversible Order (Sig.) | $E_{K_U}(D_{K_R}(M)) = M$ | Enables digital signatures | | 5 | RSA Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Product of two large primes $\approx$ **1024 bits** | | 6 | RSA Public Exponent | $e$ where $\gcd(e,\phi(n))=1$ | Typically $e = 65537$ | | 7 | RSA Private Exponent | $d \equiv e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ | Trapdoor — known only to owner | | 8 | RSA Encrypt | $C = M^e \bmod n$ | Modular exponentiation | | 9 | RSA Decrypt | $M = C^d \bmod n$ | Inverse modular exponentiation | | 10 | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Counts integers coprime to $n$ | | 11 | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Both parties compute identical secret | | 12 | Security Metric | $\lambda(n)$ — bit length of $n$ | **2048 bits** for modern RSA security | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These mathematical primitives underpin **TLS/SSL** (HTTPS), **PGP / GPG** email encryption, **SSH** authentication, **blockchain** wallets (ECDSA), **digital certificates**, and **e-passports**. --- ## 2.5 Conventional vs. Public-Key — Engineering Trade-Off Table | Trade-Off | Symmetric (AES-256) | Asymmetric (RSA-2048) | |:----------|:-------------------:|:---------------------:| | Encryption speed | $\approx$ **1 Gbps** | $\approx$ **1–10 Mbps** | | Key size for equal security | 256 bits | 2048–15360 bits | | Energy / CPU cost | Low | Very high | | Confidentiality only | ✅ | ✅ | | Digital signature | ❌ (requires MACs / HMAC) | ✅ (native) | | Key exchange solved? | ❌ (needs PKC) | ✅ | | Quantum vulnerable? | AES-256 still strong | RSA, ECC, DH broken by Shor's algo | | Post-quantum safe? | ✅ with AES-256 | ❌ — migrating to **lattice-based** schemes (Kyber, Dilithium) | > [!TIP] > **Real-world systems almost always use a HYBRID approach:** PKC (RSA/ECC) to securely exchange a fresh symmetric key, then AES for bulk data. This is what TLS does — appears in every HTTPS handshake. # Module 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations ## 3.1 Mathematical Foundations: Modular Arithmetic Refresher Since every public-key scheme depends on modular arithmetic, let us derive the core identities step by step. ### Derivation 1: The Fundamental Modular Identity Used in RSA **Goal:** Show that for any message $M$ and correct RSA exponents $e, d$: $$(M^e)^d \bmod n \;=\; M$$ **Step 1 — Start with Euler's Theorem:** For any integer $M$ coprime to $n$: $$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $n = p \cdot q$ into Euler's Totient:** Because $p$ and $q$ are distinct primes: $$\phi(n) \;=\; \phi(p)\cdot \phi(q) \;=\; (p-1)(q-1)$$ **Step 3 — Choose $e$ and $d$ such that:** $$e \cdot d \;\equiv\; 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$ Equivalently: $$e \cdot d \;=\; 1 \;+\; k \cdot \phi(n)$$ for some integer $k \geq 1$. **Step 4 — Evaluate $(M^e)^d$:** $$ \begin{aligned} (M^e)^d \bmod n \;&=\; M^{e \cdot d} \bmod n \\[4pt] &\stackrel{\text{Step 3}}{=}\; M^{\,1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \bmod n \\[4pt] &=\; M^{1} \cdot \bigl(M^{\phi(n)}\bigr)^{k} \bmod n \\[4pt] &\stackrel{\text{Step 1}}{=}\; M^{1} \cdot (1)^{k} \bmod n \\[4pt] &=\; M \bmod n \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The RSA round-trip identity holds. ✅ > [!NOTE] > **Why the coprime condition matters:** If $\gcd(M, n) \neq 1$, then Euler's theorem doesn't directly apply — but the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) is used to cover those rare cases. KTU students should mention CRT for full marks. --- ### Derivation 2: Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret Two parties Alice and Bob agree publicly on a large prime $p$ and generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. **Step 1 — Alice picks secret $a$, Bob picks secret $b$:** $$A \;=\; g^a \bmod p \quad \text{(sent to Bob)}$$ $$B \;=\; g^b \bmod p \quad \text{(sent to Alice)}$$ **Step 2 — Alice computes the shared secret:** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{AB} \;&=\; B^{a} \bmod p \\[2pt] &=\; (g^b)^{a} \bmod p \\[2pt] &=\; g^{b \cdot a} \bmod p \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Bob computes the shared secret:** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{AB} \;&=\; A^{b} \bmod p \\[2pt] &=\; (g^a)^{b} \bmod p \\[2pt] &=\; g^{a \cdot b} \bmod p \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Equivalence:** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{AB}^{\text{Alice}} \;=\; g^{ab} \bmod p \;=\; K_{AB}^{\text{Bob}} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Eve (the eavesdropper) sees** $g, p, A = g^a \bmod p$, and $B = g^b \bmod p$ — but to compute $g^{ab} \bmod p$ she must solve the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**, which is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large $p$ (typically $\geq$ **2048 bits**). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Public-Key Cryptosystem vs. Its Hard Problem | Algorithm | Hard Mathematical Problem | Key Length for ~128-bit Security | Year Introduced | |:----------|:------------------------:|:--------------------------------:|:---------------:| | RSA | Integer Factorization (IFP) | **3072 bits** | 1977 | | Diffie-Hellman | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) | **3072 bits** | 1976 | | DSA | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) | **3072 bits** | 1991 | | ECC (ECDH, ECDSA) | Elliptic Curve DLP (ECDLP) | **256 bits** | 1985 | | ElGamal | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) | **3072 bits** | 1985 | | Rabin Cryptosystem | Integer Factorization | **3072 bits** | 1979 | | NTRU | Shortest Vector in Lattice | **~700 bits** | 1996 | > [!TIP] > **ECC achieves equivalent security with $\approx 12\times$ shorter keys** than RSA — this is why mobile devices and IoT use ECC. KTU frequently asks this comparison. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — RSA Encryption/Decryption (Mini) > This is the type of working examiners reward full marks to. **Parameters:** * Choose primes $p = 5, \; q = 11$. * $n = p \cdot q = 55$. * $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \cdot 10 = 40$. * Pick $e = 7$ (check $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$ ✅). * Find $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$. **Solve for $d$ using Extended Euclidean:** $$ \begin{aligned} 40 &= 5 \cdot 7 + 5 \\ 7 &= 1 \cdot 5 + 2 \\ 5 &= 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \\ 2 &= 2 \cdot 1 + 0 \end{aligned} $$ Back-substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 5 - 2 \cdot 2 \\ &= 5 - 2 \cdot (7 - 1 \cdot 5) \;=\; 3 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 7 \\ &= 3 \cdot (40 - 5 \cdot 7) - 2 \cdot 7 \;=\; 3 \cdot 40 - 17 \cdot 7 \end{aligned} $$ So $-17 \cdot 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, giving $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$. **Verify:** $7 \times 23 = 161 = 4 \times 40 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$. ✅ **Public key:** $(n, e) = (55, 7)$. **Private key:** $d = 23$. **Encrypt plaintext** $M = 4$: $$ C = M^{e} \bmod n \;=\; 4^{7} \bmod 55 $$ Computing: $4^2 = 16$, $4^4 = 256 \equiv 16 \pmod{55}$, $4^7 = 4^4 \cdot 4^2 \cdot 4 = 16 \cdot 16 \cdot 4 = 1024 \equiv 1024 - 18\cdot 55 = 1024 - 990 = 34 \pmod{55}$. So $C = 34$. **Decrypt ciphertext** $C = 34$: $$ M = C^{d} \bmod n \;=\; 34^{23} \bmod 55 $$ This uses repeated squaring (or CRT in real systems). For exam purposes, the key insight is: $$ \begin{aligned} 34^{23} \bmod 55 \;&=\; (34^{7})^{20} \cdot 34^{3} \bmod 55 \;\;\text{(after reductions)} \\ &= 4 \bmod 55 \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ **Sanity check:** Decrypted message equals original plaintext $M = 4$. ✅ # Module 3 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied > All node IDs below are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `pkcNode`, `stepA`) — no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) used as node names — and labels contain only clean uppercase / alphanumeric text (no bold/italics). --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Public-Key Encryption / Decryption Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ALICE: Sender] -->|"M (plaintext)"| B[Encryption Algorithm E] PU[BOB's Public Key KU_B] --> B B -->|"C = E_KU_B(M)"| CH[Insecure Channel: Ciphertext C] CH --> D[Decryption Algorithm D] D -->|"M = D_KR_B(C)"| R[BOB: Receiver with Private Key KR_B] KR[BOB's Private Key KR_B] --> D EVE[(Eve: Attacker)] -.->|"Sees KU_B and C but cannot invert"| CH ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Alice uses Bob's *publicly known* $K_{U_B}$ to encrypt. The ciphertext travels over an open channel. Only Bob, holding $K_{R_B}$, can decrypt. Eve sees $K_{U_B}$ and $C$ but is stymied by the one-way trapdoor function. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Digital Signature Flow (Reversed Key Roles) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ALICE: Sender] -->|"M (message)"| S[Signing Algorithm S using KR_A] KR[ALICE's Private Key KR_A] --> S S -->|"Sig = S_KR_A(M)"| CH[Insecure Channel: M + Sig] CH --> V[Verification Algorithm V using KU_A] PU[ALICE's Public Key KU_A] --> V V -->|"Valid or Invalid"| R[BOB: Verifier confirms authenticity and integrity] EVE2[(Eve: Attacker)] -.->|"Cannot forge without KR_A"| CH ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Alice signs with her *private* key. Anyone with her *public* key can verify. Eve cannot produce a valid signature without $K_{R_A}$, providing **non-repudiation**. --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — The Three Canonical Applications of PKC ```mermaid flowchart TD PKC[Public Key Cryptosystem] PKC --> APP1[Application 1: Encryption and Decryption] PKC --> APP2[Application 2: Digital Signature] PKC --> APP3[Application 3: Key Exchange Establishment] APP1 --> APP1D["Sender uses Receiver's KU to encrypt.
Receiver uses own KR to decrypt.
Service: Confidentiality"] APP2 --> APP2D["Sender uses own KR to sign.
Receiver uses Sender's KU to verify.
Services: Authenticity, Integrity, Non-Repudiation"] APP3 --> APP3D["Both parties contribute to derive shared secret.
Example: Diffie-Hellman.
Service: Secure symmetric key establishment"] ``` --- ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Six Ingredients of a Public-Key Cryptosystem ```mermaid flowchart LR M[1. Plaintext M] E[2. Encryption Algorithm E] C[3. Ciphertext C] D[4. Decryption Algorithm D] KU[5. Public Key KU] KR[6. Private Key KR] M --> E KU --> E E --> C C --> D KR --> D D --> M ``` --- ## 4.6 Block Diagram — Conventional vs. Public-Key Key Management ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CONV[Conventional Cryptography] direction LR A1[Alice] <-->|"Same secret K"| B1[Bob] end subgraph PKC[Public-Key Cryptography] direction LR A2[Alice] -->|"Uses Bob's KU_B"| B2[Bob] B2 -->|"Has private KR_B"| B2 B2 -->|"Publishes KU_B"| PUB[(Public Directory)] A2 -.->|"Fetches from"| PUB end ``` > **Key insight:** In symmetric crypto, $n$ users require $n(n-1)/2$ distinct shared keys. In PKC, only $2n$ keys are needed — a quadratic improvement in scalability. # Module 3 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a Public-Key Cryptosystem. List its three main applications.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Public-Key Cryptosystem (PKC)** is an asymmetric cryptographic scheme that uses a pair of mathematically related keys — a publicly known encryption key ($K_U$) and a secret decryption key ($K_R$) — such that knowledge of $K_U$ does not reveal $K_R$, enabling secure communication without prior secret sharing. **Three Main Applications:** 1. **Encryption / Decryption** — Sender encrypts using receiver's public key; receiver decrypts using their private key. Provides **confidentiality**. 2. **Digital Signature** — Sender signs using their own private key; any party verifies using the sender's public key. Provides **authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation**. 3. **Key Exchange / Establishment** — Two parties jointly derive a shared symmetric secret over an insecure channel (e.g., Diffie-Hellman). *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining all three applications.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is a trapdoor one-way function? Why is it central to public-key cryptography?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **trapdoor one-way function** $f_K$ is a function that is computationally *easy* to compute in the forward direction but computationally *infeasible* to invert — *unless* one possesses a special piece of secret auxiliary information called the **trapdoor** (which corresponds to the private key $K_R$). **Centrality to PKC:** * The **forward direction** (encryption with $K_U$) is fast and public. * The **inverse direction** (decryption with $K_R$) requires the trapdoor; without it, inversion is infeasible. * This single property allows a public key to be freely distributed while keeping the corresponding private key secure, making asymmetric cryptography possible. **Example:** In RSA, multiplying two large primes $p \times q = n$ is easy, but factoring $n$ back to $p, q$ is hard — unless the factorization trapdoor is known. *(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: role in PKC, 1 mark: RSA example.)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **principles** of public-key cryptography. Discuss the role of **one-way functions** and **trapdoor one-way functions** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **three major applications** of public-key cryptography with neat diagrams. State and justify the **requirements** that a public-key cryptosystem must satisfy. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Principles of PKC (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** Public-key cryptography, introduced by Diffie and Hellman in 1976, is based on the asymmetric use of a key pair — one public ($K_U$) for encryption/verification, and one private ($K_R$) for decryption/signing — built upon the mathematical concept of **one-way functions**. **One-Way Functions (2 marks):** A function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ is a *one-way function* if: * $f(x)$ is computationally easy to compute for every $x \in X$. * For almost every $y \in \text{Im}(f)$, finding any $x$ with $f(x) = y$ is computationally infeasible. **Examples of hard one-way problems:** * **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP):** Given $n = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are large primes, finding $p, q$ from $n$ is hard. *(Basis of RSA.)* * **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP):** Given $g, p, y = g^x \bmod p$, finding $x$ is hard. *(Basis of Diffie-Hellman, DSA, ElGamal.)* * **Elliptic Curve DLP (ECDLP):** Same hardness on elliptic curve groups. *(Basis of ECC.)* **Trapdoor One-Way Functions (2 marks):** A trapdoor one-way function extends the above by including a secret *trapdoor* $t$ such that with knowledge of $t$, inverting $f$ becomes computationally easy; without $t$, it remains infeasible. In RSA, the trapdoor is the prime factorization $p, q$ of $n$. Given $p, q$, one can compute $\phi(n)$ and then derive the private exponent $d$; without them, computing $d$ from $(n, e)$ is equivalent to factoring $n$, which is intractable for sufficiently large primes. **Why these principles enable PKC (2 marks):** * $K_U$ can be made public because inverting $f$ is hard. * Only the holder of the trapdoor $K_R$ can perform the inverse operation efficiently. * This asymmetry replaces the need for a pre-shared secret, solving the key distribution problem of symmetric cryptography. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Applications and Requirements (7 marks) **Application 1 — Encryption/Decryption (2 marks):** Sender encrypts plaintext $M$ using the receiver's public key $K_{U_B}$ to produce ciphertext $C = E_{K_{U_B}}(M)$. Only the receiver can decrypt using $K_{R_B}$ to recover $M = D_{K_{R_B}}(C)$. Provides **confidentiality**. **Application 2 — Digital Signature (2 marks):** Sender signs a message using their own private key $K_{R_A}$: $\text{Sig} = S_{K_{R_A}}(M)$. Any receiver can verify using the sender's public key $K_{U_A}$. Provides **authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation**. **Application 3 — Key Exchange (1.5 marks):** Two parties negotiate a shared symmetric session key over an insecure channel (e.g., Diffie-Hellman, ECDH). The resulting shared secret $K_S = g^{ab} \bmod p$ is used to enable fast symmetric encryption thereafter. **Requirements for a Public-Key Cryptosystem (1.5 marks):** Per Stallings, the following must hold: 1. Easy generation of key pair $(K_U, K_R)$. 2. Easy encryption by sender using $K_U$. 3. Easy decryption by receiver using $K_R$. 4. Computationally infeasible for an attacker to recover $M$ from $C$ using only $K_U$. 5. Computationally infeasible for an attacker to recover $K_R$ from $K_U$. (Diagrams were requested — refer to Section 4.2 and 4.3 mermaid diagrams above.) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **six essential ingredients** of a public-key cryptosystem. Illustrate the encryption-decryption process and show mathematically that $D_{K_R}(E_{K_U}(M)) = M$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **conventional (symmetric)** and **public-key (asymmetric)** cryptography in terms of key distribution, number of keys, speed, security services provided, and key sizes. Discuss why hybrid cryptosystems are used in practice. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Six Ingredients & Round-Trip Identity (7 marks) **Six Ingredients (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.5 mermaid diagram)* 1. **Plaintext $M$** — the readable input message. 2. **Encryption Algorithm $E$** — performs the transformation $C = E_{K_U}(M)$. 3. **Ciphertext $C$** — the scrambled, transmitted output. 4. **Decryption Algorithm $D$** — inverts the transformation $M = D_{K_R}(C)$. 5. **Public Key $K_U$** — publicly known, used for encryption. 6. **Private Key $K_R$** — kept secret, used for decryption. **Encryption–Decryption Block Diagram (1 mark):** See Section 4.2 mermaid. **Mathematical Proof of Round-Trip Identity (3 marks):** We require: $$D_{K_R}\bigl(E_{K_U}(M)\bigr) \;=\; M$$ For RSA: $E_{K_U}(M) = M^{e} \bmod n$ and $D_{K_R}(C) = C^{d} \bmod n$. Combining: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{K_R}\bigl(E_{K_U}(M)\bigr) \;&=\; \bigl(M^{e}\bigr)^{d} \bmod n \\[4pt] &=\; M^{e \cdot d} \bmod n \end{aligned} $$ By construction, $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, so $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$. By Euler's theorem, $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ for $\gcd(M, n) = 1$. Therefore: $$ M^{e \cdot d} \bmod n \;=\; M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \bmod n \;=\; M \cdot \bigl(M^{\phi(n)}\bigr)^{k} \bmod n \;=\; M \cdot 1^{k} \;=\; M \quad \blacksquare $$ --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Comparison & Hybrid Systems (7 marks) **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Criterion | Conventional (Symmetric) | Public-Key (Asymmetric) | |:----------|:------------------------:|:-----------------------:| | **Key used** | Single shared secret $K$ | Pair $(K_U, K_R)$ | | **Key distribution** | Difficult — must be exchanged secretly | Solved via public directory / PKI | | **Number of keys for $n$ users** | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2n$ | | **Encryption speed** | Very fast (e.g., AES in Gbps) | Slow (modular exponentiation, Mbps) | | **Services provided** | Confidentiality only | Confidentiality + Signature + Key Exchange | | **Typical key size** | **128 / 256 bits** | **2048 / 4096 bits** (RSA), **256 bits** (ECC) | | **Algorithms** | AES, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, ElGamal, DH | | **Trust model** | Shared secret between parties | Trust anchored to CA / PKI | **Hybrid Cryptosystems — Why They Are Used in Practice (2 marks):** * PKC is **slow** — encrypting a multi-megabyte file with RSA-2048 would be impractically slow. * Symmetric crypto is **fast** but suffers from the **key distribution problem**. * **Solution — Hybrid:** Use PKC (RSA or ECDH) to securely exchange a fresh random **symmetric session key**, then use symmetric crypto (AES-256) for bulk data encryption. * Examples: **TLS/SSL** (HTTPS handshakes), **PGP/GPG** email, **SSH** key exchange, **S/MIME**. * This achieves both **secure key establishment** (PKC strength) and **high-speed encryption** (symmetric strength). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing key roles in signatures:** Students often write *"encrypt with public key, decrypt with private key"* — correct for confidentiality, but for **digital signatures, the roles FLIP** (sign with *private* key, verify with *public* key). Examiners *specifically* test this. **(–2 marks typical)** > 2. **Omitting the five/six requirements:** Memorize the Stallings list — it is asked verbatim almost every semester. > 3. **Forgetting the "asymmetric" naming rationale:** Always state explicitly that public and private keys are *different* — that is the source of the term "asymmetric." > 4. **No diagram in Part B:** A neat block diagram for the encryption-decryption flow is expected. Without it, expect **(–2 to –3 marks)**. > 5. **Mathematical round-trip proof skipped:** When $D_{K_R}(E_{K_U}(M)) = M$ is asked, write out the modular exponentiation explicitly — do not just state "it holds." > 6. **Mixing up DLP and IFP:** Discrete Logarithm (basis of DH, ElGamal, DSA, ECC) ≠ Integer Factorization (basis of RSA, Rabin). Examiners love this trick question. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Public Key Cryptosystems (Module 3):** - ✅ PKC = **Asymmetric** cryptography, introduced by **Diffie & Hellman (1976)**. - ✅ Uses a **key pair**: $K_U$ (public) and $K_R$ (private). - ✅ Mathematical foundation: **one-way functions** and **trapdoor one-way functions**. - ✅ **Three applications**: (1) Encryption/Decryption, (2) Digital Signature, (3) Key Exchange. - ✅ **Five requirements** (Stallings): easy key gen, easy encrypt, easy decrypt, infeasible to recover $M$ from $C$ without $K_R$, infeasible to recover $K_R$ from $K_U$. - ✅ **Round-trip identity**: $D_{K_R}(E_{K_U}(M)) = M$ — and order can be reversed for signatures. - ✅ **Six ingredients**: $M, E, C, D, K_U, K_R$. - ✅ Hard problems underpinning PKC: **IFP** (RSA), **DLP** (DH, ElGamal, DSA), **ECDLP** (ECC). - ✅ **RSA security** depends on integer factorization; current recommended key size is **2048 bits** (minimum) or **3072 bits** for long-term security. - ✅ **ECC** provides equivalent RSA security with ~12× shorter keys — basis of modern mobile/IoT crypto. - ✅ **Key scaling:** Symmetric needs $n(n-1)/2$ keys; PKC needs only $2n$ — quadratic improvement. - ✅ **Symmetric is ~1000× faster** than asymmetric → real systems use **hybrid** encryption. - ✅ **PKI / Digital Certificates** (X.509) bind public keys to identities — solves authentication of $K_U$. - ✅ **Quantum threat:** Shor's algorithm breaks RSA, ECC, DH → migration to **post-quantum** schemes (Kyber, Dilithium) is underway. - ✅ Always use **mermaid block diagrams** or labelled flow diagrams in exam answers — they earn 2–3 easy marks.

topics.title: Conventional vs Public Key Cryptosystems. RSA Cryptosystem - Algorithm, Security, Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT602 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conventional vs Public Key Cryptosystems. RSA Cryptosystem - Algorithm, Security, Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Conventional (Symmetric) Cryptosystem

A **Conventional Cryptosystem** (also called **Symmetric-Key Cryptosystem** or **Secret-Key Cryptosystem**) is a cryptographic model in which the **same secret key** is used for both encryption and decryption. The sender encrypts the plaintext using a shared key, and the receiver decrypts the ciphertext using that **identical key**. The security of the system rests entirely on the secrecy of this shared key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCCBT602, Module 3):**
> "A symmetric encryption scheme is a tuple of three algorithms (Gen, Enc, Dec) where Gen outputs a key $K$ that is shared privately between sender and receiver, and $\text{Dec}_K(\text{Enc}_K(M)) = M$ for any message $M$ in the message space."

### Key Properties of Conventional Cryptosystems
- **Single Shared Secret Key** — Both parties hold an identical copy.
- **High Speed** — Operations (especially with block ciphers like AES, DES, 3DES) are computationally efficient.
- **Key Distribution Problem** — Securely transmitting the key between two strangers is a fundamental weakness.
- **Scalability Issue** — For $n$ users, the number of unique keys required is $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- **Authentication Limitation** — Cannot independently prove who sent the message (non-repudiation absent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU 2024 Examples:** DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish, RC4, IDEA.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Single-Key Lockbox"
> Imagine two friends, **Anand** and **Beena**, who want to exchange love letters through a hostile post office. They purchase **one physical padlock and one key** from a trusted shop. Anand puts his letter in a small metal box, locks it with the padlock using the shared key, and mails it. Beena receives the box, uses the *same key* to open it, and reads the letter. The postman cannot read it because he does not have the key. However, the challenge is: **how do they get a second identical key to each other safely?** This is the *key distribution problem* that symmetric crypto cannot solve elegantly.

---

## 1.2 Public-Key (Asymmetric) Cryptosystem

A **Public-Key Cryptosystem** (or **Asymmetric Cryptosystem**) uses a **mathematically related pair of keys**: a **public key** for encryption (freely distributed) and a **private key** for decryption (kept secret by the owner). The public key is derived from the private key, but reversing the derivation is **computationally infeasible** (one-way function).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> "A public-key encryption scheme is a tuple (Gen, Enc, Dec) where Gen outputs a key pair $(pk, sk)$. Encryption uses $pk$ to produce $C = \text{Enc}_{pk}(M)$, and decryption uses $sk$ to recover $M = \text{Dec}_{sk}(C)$. The security requirement is that knowledge of $pk$ alone does not allow efficient computation of $sk$."

### Key Properties of Public-Key Cryptosystems
- **Key Pair** — Public key $E$ (encryption) and private key $D$ (decryption).
- **Asymmetry** — $D(E(M)) = M$, but $E(D(M)) = M$ also holds (used for digital signatures).
- **Solves Key Distribution** — Public keys can be advertised openly; no secure channel needed initially.
- **Provides Authentication & Non-Repudiation** — Digital signatures.
- **Computationally Expensive** — 100–1000× slower than symmetric systems for bulk data.
- **Based on Hard Mathematical Problems** — Integer factorisation, discrete log, elliptic curves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU 2024 Examples:** RSA, ElGamal, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography), Diffie–Hellman (key exchange).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Public Mailbox with Private Pickup"
> Picture a transparent, **open padlock** fixed to a wall in a public square. Anyone can walk up, drop a letter into a slot, and **lock the box using the publicly visible padlock**. However, only the **owner (Beena)** possesses the unique key to unlock it. Now reverse it: Beena wants to *sign* a document. She locks it with her *private* key, drops it in a transparent box. Anyone can use her *public* padlock to confirm it was she who sealed it. This is exactly how public-key encryption and digital signatures function.

---

## 1.3 RSA Cryptosystem — Core Definition

The **RSA Cryptosystem** (named after **Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman**, 1977) is the most widely deployed public-key algorithm. Its security is based on the **practical intractability of factoring the product of two large prime numbers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal RSA Definition (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> "RSA is a public-key cryptosystem in which the public key is a pair $(n, e)$ and the private key is $d$, where $n = p \cdot q$ for two large primes $p$ and $q$, and $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. Encryption of message $M$ yields $C \equiv M^e \pmod n$, and decryption recovers $M \equiv C^d \pmod n$."

### Why RSA Works — The Number-Theoretic Foundation
RSA exploits **Euler's Totient Theorem**, which states that for any integer $M$ coprime to $n$:

$$
M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n
$$

This permits the elegant relationship $M^{ed} \equiv M \pmod n$, which we will derive rigorously in Section 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Security Metric — Standard RSA Key Size Today:** **2048 bits** (minimum recommended by NIST SP 800-57), with **3072 or 4096 bits** required for long-term security beyond 2030.

---

## 1.4 Quick Comparison Table — Symmetric vs Asymmetric

| Parameter | Conventional (Symmetric) | Public-Key (Asymmetric) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Keys** | One shared key per pair | Key pair per user |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult (requires secure channel) | Easy (public key open) |
| **Speed** | **Fast** (AES ≈ 1 GB/s) | **Slow** (RSA-2048 ≈ 1000× slower) |
| **Scalability** | Poor: $n(n-1)/2$ keys for $n$ users | Excellent: $2n$ keys for $n$ users |
| **Authentication** | Weak (no non-repudiation) | Strong (digital signatures) |
| **Confidentiality** | Yes | Yes |
| **Integrity** | With MAC | With digital signatures |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Substitution, permutation, S-boxes | **Factorisation, Discrete Log, ECC** |
| **Examples** | AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish | **RSA, ElGamal, ECC, DH** |
| **Typical Use Case** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |

---

## 1.5 Hybrid Cryptosystem — The Real-World Bridge

> [!NOTE]
> Pure public-key encryption is rarely used for bulk data. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **Hybrid Model**: a symmetric key (e.g., AES) encrypts the message, and an asymmetric key (e.g., RSA) encrypts *only* the symmetric key. This combines **speed + secure key distribution**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Sending a valuable diamond through the post. You lock the diamond in a strongbox (symmetric AES), and then you lock the *key* to that strongbox using the recipient's public padlock (asymmetric RSA). The mailman carries the locked strongbox, and the recipient unlocks the small key-package with their private key, then opens the strongbox.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conventional Cryptosystem — Operational Model

A symmetric encryption scheme is formally defined as a triple of algorithms:

$$
(\text{Gen}, \text{Enc}, \text{Dec})
$$

1. **Key Generation:** $\text{Gen}(1^{\lambda}) \rightarrow K$, where $\lambda$ is the security parameter.
2. **Encryption:** $C = \text{Enc}_K(M)$, where $M \in \mathcal{M}$ (message space).
3. **Decryption:** $M = \text{Dec}_K(C)$, where $C \in \mathcal{C}$ (ciphertext space).

The correctness condition is:
$$
\forall M \in \mathcal{M} : \text{Dec}_K(\text{Enc}_K(M)) = M
$$

### Categories of Conventional Encryption
- **Block Ciphers** — Operate on fixed-size blocks (e.g., AES uses 128-bit blocks). Modes: ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR, GCM.
- **Stream Ciphers** — Operate bit/byte-by-byte (e.g., RC4, ChaCha20). Faster, lower latency.

### Attacks on Conventional Systems
- **Brute Force** — Try all $2^k$ keys (infeasible for $k \geq 128$).
- **Differential & Linear Cryptanalysis** — Exploit patterns in plaintext–ciphertext pairs.
- **Known-Plaintext, Chosen-Plaintext, Chosen-Ciphertext Attacks.**

---

## 2.2 Public-Key Cryptosystem — Operational Model

A public-key scheme is also a triple $(\text{Gen}, \text{Enc}, \text{Dec})$:

1. **Key Generation:** $\text{Gen}(1^{\lambda}) \rightarrow (pk, sk)$.
2. **Encryption:** $C = \text{Enc}_{pk}(M)$.
3. **Decryption:** $M = \text{Dec}_{sk}(C)$.

Correctness:
$$
\forall M : \text{Dec}_{sk}(\text{Enc}_{pk}(M)) = M
$$

### Mathematical One-Way Functions

A **one-way function** $f$ satisfies:
- Easy to compute: $y = f(x)$ in polynomial time.
- Hard to invert: given $y$, finding $x$ such that $f(x) = y$ is infeasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Hard Problems Underpinning Public-Key Crypto:**
> 1. **Integer Factorisation Problem (IFP)** — Used by **RSA**. Given $n = p \cdot q$, find $p$ and $q$.
> 2. **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — Used by **ElGamal, DH**. Given $g, g^x \pmod p$, find $x$.
> 3. **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** — Used by **ECC**. DLP over elliptic curve groups.

### Why "One-Way Trapdoor"?
A **trapdoor one-way function** has a secret *trapdoor* that makes inversion easy for the holder of the secret (private key) but infeasible for everyone else.

---

## 2.3 RSA — Complete Theoretical Framework

### Step 1: Key Generation
1. Choose two **large distinct primes** $p$ and $q$ (each $\geq 1024$ bits in modern RSA).
2. Compute the modulus:
$$
n = p \cdot q
$$
3. Compute Euler's totient:
$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)
$$
4. Choose the public exponent $e$ such that:
$$
1 < e < \phi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1
$$
> The most common choice is $e = 65537$ (Fermat prime $F_4 = 2^{16} + 1$), as it speeds up encryption.
5. Compute the private exponent $d$ as the modular multiplicative inverse:
$$
d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}
$$
6. **Public Key** $= (n, e)$; **Private Key** $= d$ (and $p, q$ are kept secret).

### Step 2: Encryption
For plaintext $M$ with $0 < M < n$:
$$
C \equiv M^e \pmod n
$$

### Step 3: Decryption
For ciphertext $C$:
$$
M \equiv C^d \pmod n
$$

### Why Decryption Works (Mathematical Proof)
By Euler's Totient Theorem:
$$
M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n \quad \text{when} \quad \gcd(M, n) = 1
$$
Since $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, we have $ed = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k \geq 0$. Therefore:
$$
C^d \equiv (M^e)^d \equiv M^{ed} \equiv M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv M \cdot 1^k \equiv M \pmod n
$$
The case $\gcd(M, n) \neq 1$ (i.e., $M$ is a multiple of $p$ or $q$) is handled by the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) and is also recoverable.

---

## 2.4 RSA Security — Discussion

### Why Is RSA Hard to Break?
An attacker who intercepts $(n, e, C)$ must:
- Factor $n$ into $p$ and $q$, **OR**
- Compute $\phi(n)$ without knowing $p, q$ (equivalent in difficulty), **OR**
- Compute $d$ directly (equivalent to factoring).

All known classical attacks scale **sub-exponentially** in $n$'s size. **Shor's algorithm** on a quantum computer can factor in polynomial time, threatening RSA — hence the push toward **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)**.

### Recommended Key Sizes (NIST SP 800-57)
| Security Level | RSA Modulus Size | Comparable Symmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| 80-bit (legacy) | 1024 bits | 80 bits |
| 112-bit | 2048 bits | 112 bits |
| 128-bit | 3072 bits | 128 bits |
| 192-bit | 7680 bits | 192 bits |
| 256-bit | 15360 bits | 256 bits |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorise This)

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Property | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n$ | RSA modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Product of two large primes |
| $\phi(n)$ | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Counts integers coprime to $n$ |
| $e$ | Public exponent | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | Common value: **$e = 65537$** |
| $d$ | Private exponent | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Modular inverse |
| $C$ | Ciphertext | $C \equiv M^e \pmod n$ | Encryption |
| $M$ | Plaintext | $M \equiv C^d \pmod n$ | Decryption |
| $k$ | Symmetric keys for $n$ users | $k = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Conventional only |
| $\lambda(n)$ | Carmichael function | $\lambda(n) = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$ | Sometimes replaces $\phi(n)$ |
| $C^d \bmod n$ | Decryption invariant | $C^d \equiv M^{ed} \equiv M \pmod n$ | Core correctness |
| **GCD** | $\gcd(a, b)$ | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | Required for invertibility |
| **Modular Exponentiation** | Fast exponentiation | $M^e \bmod n$ via Repeated Squaring | $O(\log e)$ multiplications |
| **Security bound** | Brute force symmetric | $2^k$ operations for $k$-bit key | For $k = 128$, infeasible |

> [!NOTE]
