# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Discretionary Access Control (DAC). Explain how Unix file access control implements DAC. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Discretionary Access Control (DAC)** is an access control model in which the **owner of a resource has the discretion (freedom) to decide who can access it and how**. The control is "discretionary" because the owner can grant or revoke permissions at will.

**Unix implementation (2 marks):**
- Every file has an **owner UID** and a **group GID**, stored in its inode.
- Permissions are encoded as a **9-bit mode**: three bits each for owner, group, and others (r=4, w=2, x=1).
- The owner sets these bits using `chmod`, e.g., `chmod 750 file.txt`.
- Decision ladder: kernel first checks owner UID, then group GID, then falls through to others.
- Because the **owner can change permissions anytime**, this is the textbook example of DAC.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip (1 mark reserved):** Always explicitly state that the **owner retains control** — this is the defining property distinguishing DAC from Mandatory Access Control (MAC).

---

### Question 2: List and briefly explain the four components of the NIST RBAC reference model. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ANSI/INCITS 359-2012** NIST RBAC standard defines four components:

1. **Core RBAC (1 mark):** Defines the basic elements — Users, Roles, Permissions (Operations on Objects), and Sessions. Establishes the fundamental user-role and role-permission assignments.

2. **Hierarchical RBAC (1 mark):** Introduces role hierarchies (partial order RH) so that senior roles inherit permissions from junior roles (e.g., `Senior_Engineer ⊇ Engineer`).

3. **Constrained RBAC (0.5 mark):** Adds **Separation of Duties (SoD)** constraints — Static SoD (SSD) and Dynamic SoD (DSD) — to enforce conflict-of-interest policies.

4. **Symmetric RBAC (0.5 mark):** Requires that user-permission relations be derivable symmetrically from role assignments, supporting policy review and certification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner tip:** Students often omit Symmetric RBAC. Mentioning it explicitly scores the full 3 marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Unix File Access Control + RBAC `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice A]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the Unix file access control mechanism in detail, including the 9-bit permission mask, octal notation, and the kernel's permission-checking algorithm. Illustrate with an example. Also discuss the special permissions SUID, SGID, and Sticky bit with one real-world use case for each.

**Model Answer:**

**(1) The 9-bit Permission Mask (2 marks):**
Each file in Unix has a permission mask consisting of 3 classes × 3 bits = 9 bits:
- **Owner (User) class:** r (read), w (write), x (execute)
- **Group class:** r, w, x
- **Other (World) class:** r, w, x

These are encoded in **octal notation**, where r=4, w=2, x=1. The octal digit for a class is the sum of the set bits. Example: `rwx = 4+2+1 = 7`, `r-x = 4+0+1 = 5`, `r-- = 4+0+0 = 4`. The mode `0750` therefore means: owner = rwx, group = r-x, others = none.

**(2) The Kernel's Permission-Checking Algorithm (2 marks):**
When a process P with UID=*u* and GID=*g* requests operation *op* on file F (with owner UID=*u<sub>F</sub>*, group GID=*g<sub>F</sub>*, mode M), the kernel follows this ladder:

1. **If *u* == 0 (root):** Grant (subject to capability checks; DAC is bypassed).
2. **Else if *u* == *u<sub>F</sub>*:** Apply Owner class bits from M.
3. **Else if *g* == *g<sub>F</sub>*** OR ***g*** is in F's supplementary group list: Apply Group class bits.
4. **Else:** Apply Other class bits.

**Critical KTU point:** Owner class is checked *first*; even if a user is in the file's group, they are evaluated by the Owner class if their UID matches. This is a frequent exam question.

**(3) Special Permissions (2 marks):**

| Bit | Octal | Name | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| SUID | 4xxx | Set User ID | `/usr/bin/passwd` runs with root's UID so it can update `/etc/shadow` |
| SGID | 2xxx | Set Group ID | Shared project directory `/home/proj` where all new files inherit the group |
| Sticky | 1xxx | Sticky bit | `/tmp` — users can create files but only the file owner (or root) can delete them |

**(4) Example (1 mark):** `chmod 4755 /usr/bin/mybinary` — sets SUID (4), owner=rwx (7), group=r-x (5), others=r-x (5). When run, the process executes with the file owner's UID.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** With a neat diagram, describe the **NIST RBAC reference model**. Explain User Assignment (UA), Permission Assignment (PA), and Separation of Duties (SSD and DSD) with examples. State how RBAC differs from DAC.

**Model Answer:**

**(1) NIST RBAC Model with Diagram (2.5 marks):**

The NIST model has four layered components (refer to Section 4.3 mermaid diagram):
- **Core RBAC** establishes USERS, ROLES, PERMISSIONS, SESSIONS.
- **Hierarchical RBAC** introduces role inheritance via a partial order.
- **Constrained RBAC** adds SSD and DSD.
- **Symmetric RBAC** ensures UA/PA symmetry for review.

**(2) User Assignment (UA) — 1 mark:**
UA is a many-to-many relation `UA ⊆ USERS × ROLES`. Example: User "Ravi" is assigned to roles `Doctor` and `Researcher`. Ravi can be assigned to multiple roles, and a role can have multiple users.

**(3) Permission Assignment (PA) — 1 mark:**
PA is a many-to-many relation `PA ⊆ PERMISSIONS × ROLES` (or equivalently from roles to permissions). Example: The `Doctor` role is assigned the permission `read(Patient_Record)`. A permission is an (operation, object) pair.

**(4) Separation of Duties (1.5 marks):**
- **Static Separation of Duties (SSD):** A constraint checked at *assignment time*. Example: A user cannot be assigned to both `Cashier` and `Auditor` roles. Formally: $\forall (r_1, r_2) \in SSD : \neg \exists u : (u,r_1) \in UA \land (u,r_2) \in UA$.
- **Dynamic Separation of Duties (DSD):** A constraint checked at *session activation time*. A user may hold both `Cashier` and `Auditor` roles in the IAM, but cannot have *both* active in the same session. Formally: $\forall (r_1, r_2) \in DSD$, the active role set of any single session cannot contain both.

**(5) RBAC vs. DAC (1 mark):**
- **DAC:** Owner of a *resource* controls access. Permissions are tied to objects.
- **RBAC:** *Roles* (organizational functions) control access. Permissions are tied to roles, not resources. RBAC is a "neutral" model — neither discretionary nor mandatory.

---

### Question B: ABAC + Comparative Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Choice B]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain **Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)** in detail. Describe the ABAC tuple, the XACML reference architecture, and one combining algorithm. Illustrate with a real-world cloud-IAM example.

**Model Answer:**

**(1) ABAC Overview (1 mark):**
ABAC is a **fine-grained, policy-driven** access control model that evaluates Boolean rules over attributes of the Subject (S), Object (O), Action (A), and Environment (E). Access decisions are made at *request time*, not at policy-creation time. It is the most flexible model and subsumes DAC, MAC, and RBAC.

**(2) The ABAC Tuple (1.5 marks):**
A request is a 4-tuple:

$$
Q = \langle S, O, A, E \rangle
$$

- **S** (Subject attributes): `S.role`, `S.department`, `S.clearanceLevel`
- **O** (Object attributes): `O.owner`, `O.classification`, `O.tags`
- **A** (Action attribute): `A.type` ∈ {read, write, delete, ...}
- **E** (Environment attributes): `E.time`, `E.ip`, `E.device`

A policy is a Boolean function $P(S, O, A, E) \to \{\text{True}, \text{False}\}$.

**(3) XACML Reference Architecture (2.5 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.4 mermaid diagram)
- **PEP (Policy Enforcement Point):** Sits in the application, intercepts every request, and forwards it as an XACML `<Request>` to the PDP.
- **PDP (Policy Decision Point):** The brain — fetches applicable policies from the PRP, fetches attribute values from the PIP, evaluates each rule, applies the combining algorithm, and returns Permit / Deny / NotApplicable / Indeterminate.
- **PAP (Policy Administration Point):** Where policy authors create, edit, and publish policies.
- **PIP (Policy Information Point):** Connects to attribute sources (LDAP, DB, REST APIs) to retrieve live attribute values.
- **PRP (Policy Retrieval Point):** The policy store (filesystem, DB).

**(4) Combining Algorithm — Deny-Overrides (1 mark):**
The algorithm:

$$
\text{Final} = \begin{cases}
\text{Deny} & \text{if } \exists i : \text{Evaluate}(P_i, Q) = \text{Deny} \\
\text{Permit} & \text{else if } \exists i : \text{Evaluate}(P_i, Q) = \text{Permit} \\
\text{NotApplicable} & \text{else if } \forall i : \text{Evaluate}(P_i, Q) = \text{NotApplicable} \\
\text{Indeterminate} & \text{otherwise (errors)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Default behavior:** If no rule matches, the system returns `NotApplicable`, which is then mapped to `Deny` by the PEP (fail-closed posture).

**(5) Cloud-IAM Example (1 mark):**
An AWS IAM policy statement implementing ABAC:

```
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": "s3:GetObject",
  "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::company-bucket/*",
  "Condition": {
    "StringEquals": {
      "aws:PrincipalTag/department": "s3:ResourceTag/data-owner"
    },
    "IpAddress": {
      "aws:SourceIp": "10.0.0.0/8"
    },
  }
}
```

This policy allows an S3 read **only if** the requester's `department` tag matches the S3 object's `data-owner` tag **and** the request comes from the corporate IP range. This is a textbook ABAC policy — it uses Subject attribute (PrincipalTag), Object attribute (ResourceTag), and Environment attribute (SourceIp) in a single decision.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Compare and contrast **DAC, RBAC, and ABAC** in a tabular form. State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of each model. Which model is best suited for a **Zero-Trust cloud environment** and why?

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Comparison Table (3 marks):** (See Section 3.5 Analytical Matrix for the full table — reproduce in answer.)

Key columns: Decision basis, Granularity, Context-awareness, Standard, Example system.

**(2) Advantages & Limitations (3 marks):**

**Unix DAC:**
- ✅ Advantages: Simple, fast kernel check, no central DB needed, perfect for file systems.
- ❌ Limitations: Coarse-grained, no context-awareness, vulnerable to trojans (a malicious program can use the owner's permissions).

**RBAC:**
- ✅ Advantages: Easy to align with org structure, scalable user onboarding, well-defined role hierarchies support SoD.
- ❌ Limitations: **Role explosion** (hundreds of roles in large orgs), static — cannot express "permit only between 9 AM and 5 PM" without a custom role for every time window.

**ABAC:**
- ✅ Advantages: Extremely fine-grained, context-aware (time, IP, risk), single policy covers many scenarios, supports Zero-Trust.
- ❌ Limitations: Complex to author, requires live attribute sources (PIPs), PDP is a performance critical-path, harder to audit than role lists.

**(3) Best Model for Zero-Trust Cloud (1 mark):**
**ABAC** is the canonical model for Zero-Trust environments (per NIST SP 800-207). Reason: Zero-Trust requires **per-request, context-aware** decisions — "never trust, always verify." RBAC only checks *who* you are; ABAC also checks *what* you are accessing, *from where*, *at what time*, *with what device*, and *with what risk score*. Cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) all expose ABAC primitives (tag-based conditions) for this reason.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unix DAC ladder error:** Forgetting that **Owner class is checked BEFORE Group class**. A user who is BOTH the owner and a group member is evaluated by Owner class only — *not* by the union. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Octal weight confusion:** Writing `r=2, w=1, x=4` instead of the correct `r=4, w=2, x=1`. Marks lost: 1.
> 3. **RBAC vs. RBAC-with-hierarchy confusion:** Forgetting that role inheritance requires the **Hierarchical RBAC component** — Core RBAC alone does not include inheritance. Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **ABAC default behavior:** Stating the default is "Permit when no rule matches" — it is actually **Deny** (whitelist philosophy). Marks lost: 1.
> 5. **XACML combining algorithm:** Writing the Deny-Overrides rule as "first Deny wins" — correct, but forgetting the **Indeterminate → Deny** fail-closed mapping loses 1 mark.
> 6. **SSD vs. DSD confusion:** Mixing up static (assignment-time) and dynamic (activation-time) separation of duties. Marks lost: 1.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 (UFAC + RBAC + ABAC)**

### 🔑 Unix File Access Control
- ✅ The 9-bit mode: **owner (3) + group (3) + other (3)**, octal weights **4, 2, 1**.
- ✅ Special bits: **SUID = 4xxx, SGID = 2xxx, Sticky = 1xxx**.
- ✅ Decision ladder order: **root → owner UID → group GID → other**.
- ✅ POSIX ACLs add named entries; **mask limits non-owner entries only**.
- ✅ `chmod 4755 file` = SUID + rwxr-xr-x.
- ✅ Real-world SUID example: `/usr/bin/passwd` runs as root to update `/etc/shadow`.

### 🔑 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
- ✅ NIST model has **Core, Hierarchical, Constrained, Symmetric** layers.
- ✅ **UA** = users to roles; **PA** = permissions to roles; both are many-to-many.
- ✅ **Hierarchy**: senior role inherits all permissions of junior roles (transitive).
- ✅ **SSD** = assignment-time conflict prevention; **DSD** = session-time conflict prevention.
- ✅ RBAC is **neutral** — not DAC, not MAC.
- ✅ Limitation: **role explosion** in large enterprises.

### 🔑 Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)
- ✅ Tuple: **Q = ⟨S, O, A, E⟩** (Subject, Object, Action, Environment).
- ✅ **XACML 3.0** is the OASIS standard architecture.
- ✅ Five components: **PEP, PDP, PAP, PIP, PRP**.
- ✅ Four decision outcomes: **Permit, Deny, NotApplicable, Indeterminate**.
- ✅ Combining algorithms: **Deny-overrides (default)**, Permit-overrides, First-applicable.
- ✅ **Default behavior = Deny** (whitelist / fail-closed).
- ✅ Foundation of **Zero-Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)**.
- ✅ ABAC **subsumes** DAC, MAC, and RBAC as special cases.

### 🎯 KTU Frequently Tested Points
- ✅ Convert octal → symbolic mode and vice versa (e.g., `0754 → rwxr-xr--`).
- ✅ Sketch the NIST RBAC diagram and label UA, PA, sessions.
- ✅ Write a sample XACML policy combining 2–3 attributes.
- ✅ State the Deny-Overrides algorithm in 3–4 lines.
- ✅ Compare DAC/RBAC/ABAC on at least 4 criteria.
- ✅ State one real-world example for each model (file system, SAP, AWS IAM).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Security: Intruders, Intrusion Detection Techniques, Password Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT602 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Security: Intruders, Intrusion Detection Techniques, Password Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Intruders — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **System Security**, an **Intruder** (also referred to as a *hacker* or *adversary*) is defined as an entity (human or automated software agent) that attempts to gain unauthorized access to a computing system, bypass its access-control mechanisms, or perform malicious actions such as denial-of-service, data exfiltration, privilege escalation, or sabotage.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCCBT602 syllabus**, intruders are formally classified by **Anderson's Threat Model (1980)** into three principal classes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anderson's Intruder Classification (Mandatory KTU Terminology)**
> 1. **Masquerader** — An external individual who penetrates a system's access controls to exploit a legitimate user's account.
> 2. **Misfeasor** — A legitimate authenticated insider who misuses his/her granted privileges.
> 3. **Clandestine User** — An individual who seizes supervisory (root/admin) control of the system, evading access controls and audit logs.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Building Security Viewpoint

Imagine a large corporate office building:

- A **Masquerader** is like a thief who **steals an employee's ID badge** and walks into the building pretending to be a real employee. He has *no* legitimate access — he just *faked* it.
- A **Misfeasor** is like a *real* janitor (legitimate user) who, after hours, sneaks into the CEO's office to read confidential files. He *does* have a key, but to *wrong* doors.
- A **Clandestine User** is like a person who has **broken into the security control room**, disabled the CCTV cameras, and is now operating invisibly — he controls the *system itself*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:** The **three classical security goals** that intruders threaten are **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)**. Loss of any one is a *security breach*.

---

## 1.2 Intrusion Detection — Formal Definition

**Intrusion Detection** is the process of monitoring and analyzing the events occurring in a computer system or network for signs of security violations — i.e., unauthorized access, misuse, or malicious activity — as defined by **Stallings' Information Security textbook (canonical KTU reference)**.

The software/hardware component that performs this is called an **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The CCTV + Burglar Alarm

Think of an IDS like a **smart CCTV + alarm system** installed in a bank:

- The **cameras** *record* everything (the *sensors* collect system/network events).
- The **intelligent software** *analyzes* the footage to detect abnormal behavior (e.g., a person at 3 AM, wearing a mask) — this is the *detection engine*.
- When a threat is detected, the **alarm rings** — the IDS raises an *alert* (and may log evidence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IDS vs IPS (Examiner-Favorite Distinction):**
> - **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)** — *passive*: detects and *alerts*; does **NOT** block traffic.
> - **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** — *active*: detects and *automatically blocks* the offending traffic.
> KTU questions *frequently* test this distinction.

---

## 1.3 Password Management — Formal Definition

**Password Management** encompasses the policies, mechanisms, and cryptographic procedures used to **generate, store, transmit, validate, and revoke** user credentials used for authentication in a computing system. A robust password management scheme must protect passwords against **guessing attacks, dictionary attacks, brute-force attacks, rainbow-table attacks, and insider threats**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Locker Key with Pepper

Think of a bank locker key as a password:
- The **key's shape** is the *password* (something you know).
- A **safe with the key inside another safe** is like **hashing** the password.
- **Sprinkling pepper dust** on the key's impression is like **salting** — even identical keys leave different impressions.
- A **trial-and-error locksmith** is a **brute-force attacker**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **NIST SP 800-63B** recommends a minimum password length of **8 characters** (12+ for privileged accounts) and discourages mandatory periodic resets (only on compromise indication).
> - **Storage:** Passwords must **NEVER** be stored in plaintext — only as **salted, cryptographically-hashed** values.

---

## 1.4 Topic Significance in KTU 2024 Evaluation

This topic is a **high-weightage** module because it combines:
- Cryptographic concepts (hashing, salting) — already tested in Modules 1–3.
- System-level security architecture (HIDS/NIDS).
- Probabilistic detection (anomaly detection, Bayesian analysis).

It typically accounts for **14–18% of ESE marks** (Module 4 is one of the two highest-weighted modules in the syllabus).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Intrusion Techniques — A Structured Threat Model

### A. Masquerader Attack Techniques

1. **Password Guessing** — Brute force, dictionary attack, hybrid attack.
2. **Social Engineering** — Phishing, pretexting, baiting.
3. **Credential Stuffing** — Replaying leaked username/password pairs.
4. **Session Hijacking** — Stealing a valid session token (e.g., via XSS).
5. **Exploiting Software Vulnerabilities** — Buffer overflow, SQLi, RCE.

### B. Misfeasor (Insider) Techniques

1. **Privilege Abuse** — Using granted access beyond the intended scope.
2. **Data Exfiltration** — Copying sensitive data to removable media or cloud.
3. **Logical Bombs** — Malicious code triggered by specific conditions.
4. **Backdoor Installation** — Creating alternate access paths for future use.

### C. Clandestine User Techniques

1. **Rootkit Installation** — Replacing OS binaries to hide presence.
2. **Log Tampering** — Modifying audit trails to remove traces.
3. **Kernel-Level Hooks** — Subverting OS-level monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Insight (KTU):** Clandestine users are the *hardest to detect* because they control the *very system* that records evidence. Defense requires **secure logging** (e.g., write-once logs sent to a remote, hardened audit server).

---

## 2.2 Intrusion Detection Techniques — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 Anomaly Detection (Behavior-Based)

**Principle:** Establish a *baseline* of normal system/network behavior. Flag significant deviations.

**Statistical Methods Used:**
- **Threshold Detection** — Count of events exceeds a defined limit (e.g., > 5 failed logins in 60 s).
- **Statistical Moments** — Mean, variance, multivariate distributions.
- **Markov Process Models** — State transition probabilities.
- **Clustering & Outlier Detection** — e.g., k-means, DBSCAN.
- **Neural Networks & Deep Learning** — Autoencoders, LSTMs.

**Advantage:** Can detect **zero-day attacks** (novel, never-seen-before).
**Disadvantage:** Higher **false positive rate (FPR)**.

### 2.2.2 Signature (Misuse) Detection

**Principle:** Maintain a database of known attack *signatures* (patterns). Match incoming events against the signature set.

**Signature Types:**
- **String pattern** — e.g., `/bin/sh` in HTTP payload.
- **Port signature** — connection to TCP/31337.
- **Byte sequence** — NOP sled in exploit code.
- **Heuristic signature** — behavioral rules.

**Advantage:** **Low false positive rate**, highly efficient for known attacks.
**Disadvantage:** **Cannot detect zero-day attacks**; signatures must be continuously updated.

### 2.2.3 Stateful Protocol Analysis

**Principle:** Track the *state* of network protocols (e.g., TCP handshakes) and flag deviations from the protocol specification (e.g., a TCP packet with both SYN and FIN flags set).

### 2.2.4 Hybrid Detection

Combines signature + anomaly → **best of both worlds**. Example: Suricata, Snort with preprocessors.

---

## 2.3 Architecture: HIDS vs NIDS

| Property | HIDS (Host-Based) | NIDS (Network-Based) |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Deployment Location** | On individual host | At network perimeter / choke point |
| **Data Source** | System call traces, log files, file integrity | Network packets, flow records |
| **Visibility** | Local: host events | Network-wide: traffic flows |
| **Encrypted Traffic** | Can analyze (post-decryption) | Cannot analyze (payload encrypted) |
| **Performance Impact** | Yes (host CPU) | No (in-line or passive tap) |
| **Examples** | OSSEC, Tripwire, AIDE | Snort, Suricata, Zeek |
| **Evasion Resistance** | High (local view) | Vulnerable to fragmentation, encryption |

---

## 2.4 Password Management — Protection Strategies

### A. Password Selection Strategies (User-Side)

1. **User Education** — Teach strong-password discipline (weak against human nature).
2. **Computer-Generated Passwords** — High entropy, but hard to remember.
3. **Reactive Password Checking** — Periodic auditing of stored passwords against a dictionary.
4. **Proactive Password Checking** — At creation time, reject weak passwords.

### B. Password Storage Strategies (System-Side)

1. **Plaintext Storage** — ❌ **NEVER** acceptable.
2. **Hashed Storage** — Store H(pwd) using a cryptographic hash (SHA-256, SHA-3).
3. **Salted Hash Storage** — Store H(salt ∥ pwd); the salt is per-user random.
4. **Peppered Hashing** — Server-side secret pepper combined with salt.
5. **Adaptive/Iterated Hashing** — bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2 — slow on purpose.
6. **Password-Based Key Derivation Functions (PBKDF2)** — NIST-recommended.

### C. Password Protection Strategies (Defensive Mechanisms)

1. **Password Aging / Expiration** — Periodic forced change (NIST 800-63B now *discourages* this).
2. **Account Lockout** — Lock account after N failed attempts (e.g., 3 strikes).
3. **Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)** — Something you *know* + something you *have/are*.
4. **CAPTCHA** — Thwart automated attacks.
5. **Password Hash Cracking Countermeasures** — Slow KDFs, large salts, secret peppers.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most-tested set of equations** for Module 4. Memorize every formula.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Password Entropy (Shannon)** | $$E = L \cdot \log_2(N)$$ | $L$ = password length, $N$ = character pool size | Measure password strength in *bits* |
| 2 | **Total Search Space** | $$S = N^{L}$$ | All possible passwords | Brute-force complexity |
| 3 | **Avg. Time to Crack** | $$T = \frac{S}{2R} = \frac{N^{L}}{2R}$$ | $R$ = guesses/sec | Brute-force feasibility |
| 4 | **Information Gain (ID3)** | $$\text{Gain}(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v\vert}{\vert S\vert} H(S_v)$$ | $H$ = entropy, $S_v$ = subsets | Anomaly detection feature selection |
| 5 | **Shannon Entropy** | $$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$$ | $p_i$ = probability of event $i$ | Detecting randomness (e.g., encrypted payloads) |
| 6 | **True Positive Rate (Recall)** | $$\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$$ | Sensitivity / Detection rate | IDS effectiveness |
| 7 | **False Positive Rate** | $$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$$ | False alarm rate | IDS nuisance metric |
| 8 | **Precision** | $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$$ | Positive predictive value | Detection quality |
| 9 | **F1 Score** | $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$ | Harmonic mean of P & R | Balanced detection metric |
| 10 | **Accuracy** | $$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$$ | Overall correctness | Overall IDS quality |
| 11 | **Bayes' Posterior (Anomaly)** | $$P(I \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert I)\,P(I)}{P(E)}$$ | Intrusion probability given event $E$ | Bayesian IDS inference |
| 12 | **Salted Hash Verification** | $$\text{Verify}(p) := H(\text{salt} \Vert p) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{stored\_hash}$$ | Equality test on rehash | Login authentication |

### Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Cybersecurity Operations Centers (SOCs):** Use F1 and ROC-AUC to tune IDS alert thresholds.
- **Identity Providers (Okta, Azure AD):** Use Argon2 with salt+pepper for password storage.
- **Penetration Testing:** Use $T = N^L / 2R$ to estimate feasibility of brute-force attacks.
- **Machine-Learning IDS:** Use Information Gain to rank which log features are most discriminative.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Password Entropy & Brute-Force Time

### Given
A system requires an **8-character password** drawn from a pool of **94 printable ASCII characters** (uppercase, lowercase, digits, symbols). An attacker uses a GPU rig capable of **$R = 10^{10}$** guesses per second.

### Step 1 — Identify the parameters

$$L = 8, \quad N = 94, \quad R = 10^{10}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Password Entropy

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= L \cdot \log_2(N) \\
  &= 8 \cdot \log_2(94) \\
  &= 8 \cdot 6.5546 \\
  &= 52.437 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each character adds $\log_2(94)$ bits because $94$ equally likely choices encode $\log_2(94)$ bits of information. Eight such independent characters contribute linearly.

### Step 3 — Compute Total Search Space

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= N^L = 94^8 \\
  &= 6.0957 \times 10^{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each of the 8 positions can independently take any of 94 values, giving a Cartesian product of size $94^8$.

### Step 4 — Compute Average Time to Crack (on average, half the space is searched)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{S}{2R} = \frac{6.0957 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
  &= \frac{6.0957 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
  &= 3.0479 \times 10^{5} \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to days for engineering intuition

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{days}} &= \frac{3.0479 \times 10^{5}}{86400} \\
                &\approx 3.528 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation (KTU Examiner's Standard):** The password survives brute force for only **~3.5 days** against a $10^{10}$ guess/sec adversary. To exceed a **1-year** security margin, the entropy must be raised to **$E \geq \log_2(2R \cdot T \cdot 86400)$** bits.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Information Gain for Anomaly Detection Feature Selection

### Given
An IDS dataset $S$ has **10,000** network sessions, of which **100** are intrusions. A candidate feature $A$ (e.g., "unusual port used") splits $S$ into two subsets:
- $S_1$ (feature present): 200 sessions, 80 intrusions.
- $S_2$ (feature absent): 9800 sessions, 20 intrusions.

We need to compute the **Information Gain** of feature $A$.

### Step 1 — Compute the original entropy $H(S)$

Intrusion proportion in $S$: $p = 100 / 10000 = 0.01$; normal proportion: $q = 1 - p = 0.99$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -p \log_2 p - q \log_2 q \\
     &= -0.01 \log_2(0.01) - 0.99 \log_2(0.99) \\
     &= -0.01 \cdot (-6.6438) - 0.99 \cdot (-0.0145) \\
     &= 0.06644 + 0.01436 \\
     &= 0.0808 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the conditional entropy of each subset

**Subset $S_1$:** Intrusion rate $p_1 = 80/200 = 0.4$; normal rate $q_1 = 0.6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_1) &= -0.4 \log_2(0.4) - 0.6 \log_2(0.6) \\
       &= -0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) - 0.6 \cdot (-0.7370) \\
       &= 0.5288 + 0.4422 \\
       &= 0.9710 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subset $S_2$:** Intrusion rate $p_2 = 20/9800 \approx 0.00204$; normal rate $q_2 \approx 0.99796$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_2) &= -0.00204 \log_2(0.00204) - 0.99796 \log_2(0.99796) \\
       &\approx 0.00204 \cdot 8.9377 + 0.99796 \cdot 0.01465 \\
       &\approx 0.01823 + 0.01462 \\
       &\approx 0.03285 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the weighted average post-split entropy

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid A) &= \frac{\vert S_1\vert}{\vert S\vert} H(S_1) + \frac{\vert S_2\vert}{\vert S\vert} H(S_2) \\
            &= \frac{200}{10000}(0.9710) + \frac{9800}{10000}(0.03285) \\
            &= 0.02 \cdot 0.9710 + 0.98 \cdot 0.03285 \\
            &= 0.01942 + 0.03219 \\
            &= 0.05161 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Information Gain

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gain}(S, A) &= H(S) - H(S \mid A) \\
                 &= 0.0808 - 0.05161 \\
                 &= 0.0292 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Feature $A$ ("unusual port used") reduces entropy by **0.0292 bits** — a *modest but real* gain. A feature whose gain is near 0 is *useless*; a feature with high gain is *highly discriminative* and should be prioritized in the IDS detection rule set.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayesian Intrusion Posterior

### Given
- Prior probability of any event being an intrusion: $P(I) = 0.01$.
- Prior probability of a benign event: $P(\overline{I}) = 0.99$.
- Likelihood that a *port-scan* signature fires given an intrusion: $P(E \mid I) = 0.95$.
- Likelihood that a *port-scan* signature fires given a benign event: $P(E \mid \overline{I}) = 0.05$.

### Compute $P(I \mid E)$ using Bayes' Theorem

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(I \mid E) &= \frac{P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I)}{P(E)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

First, compute the total probability of $E$ using the **Law of Total Probability**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(E) &= P(E \mid I) \cdot P(I) + P(E \mid \overline{I}) \cdot P(\overline{I}) \\
     &= (0.95)(0.01) + (0.05)(0.99) \\
     &= 0.0095 + 0.0495 \\
     &= 0.0590
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(I \mid E) &= \frac{0.95 \times 0.01}{0.0590} \\
            &= \frac{0.0095}{0.0590} \\
            &\approx 0.1610
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **posterior probability** of intrusion given the port-scan signature is **~16.1%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The low prior (1%) dilutes the strong likelihood (0.95). The posterior is still small, but **~16× the prior**. Combining *multiple* Bayesian evidence (e.g., port-scan + failed-login) raises the posterior rapidly toward 1.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Salted Hash Verification Flow

### Given
A user registers with password $p$ = `"Hello@2024"`. The system generates a **16-byte random salt** $s$. The system uses SHA-256 (output = 32 bytes, 256 bits).

### Step 1 — Generate random salt
$$s \leftarrow \text{Random}(16)$$

### Step 2 — Concatenate and hash
$$h = \text{SHA-256}(s \, \| \, p)$$

### Step 3 — Store $(s, h)$ in the user database

The system **never** stores $p$ itself.

### Step 4 — At login, user submits $p'$

The system *retrieves* the stored salt $s$ and *rehashes*:

$$
h' = \text{SHA-256}(s \, \| \, p')
$$

### Step 5 — Constant-time comparison
$$
\text{Authenticate} \iff h' = h
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Never use a regular `==` comparison — it is *not* constant-time and is vulnerable to **timing attacks**. Use a constant-time comparison function (e.g., Python's `hmac.compare_digest`, C's `CRYPTO_memcmp`).

### Engineering Value of Salt

- Two users with the *same* password $p$ produce *different* hashes $h_1 \neq h_2$ because $s_1 \neq s_2$.
- A precomputed **rainbow table** (which assumes unsalted hashes) is **defeated** because the attacker must recompute a new table for *each* user.

---

## 3.5 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Detection Method | Detection Basis | False Positive | Zero-Day Detection | Resource Cost |
|------------------|-----------------|----------------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Anomaly (Statistical)** | Behavioral baseline | High | ✅ Yes | Moderate |
| **Signature (Misuse)** | Known attack pattern | Low | ❌ No | Low |
| **Stateful Protocol** | Protocol RFC compliance | Medium | ⚠ Partial | Moderate |
| **Hybrid** | Combined | Low | ✅ Yes | High |
| **Bayesian Network** | Conditional probabilities | Medium | ✅ Yes | High |
| **Neural/Deep Learning** | Learned features | Variable | ✅ Yes | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All diagrams use **safe, alphanumeric Mermaid node IDs** and **raw uppercase labels** (no markdown or reserved keywords) per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard protocol.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Anderson's Classification of Intruders

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions are modeled on the **actual KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for PCCBT602 — Information Security (Module 4). Every question is tagged with a simulated past-year identifier and includes a **complete model answer** structured for the KTU evaluator's 7+7 = 14 mark sub-part scheme.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Differentiate between a Masquerader and a Misfeasor with one real-world example for each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

A **Masquerader** is an *external* attacker who has no legitimate system access but obtains and abuses a valid user's credentials. Example: A phishing email tricking a user into revealing their banking password, followed by the attacker logging in as that user.

A **Misfeasor** is a *legitimate, authenticated insider* who misuses the privileges granted to them. Example: A hospital clerk authorized to view patient records selling them to a third party.

**Key Difference:** Masquerader = *no* legitimate identity; Misfeasor = legitimate identity used for illegitimate purposes.

*(1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for difference/example.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Honeypot? Mention its role in intrusion detection.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

A **Honeypot** is a *decoy system* deliberately deployed to attract attackers, observe their behavior, and gather intelligence about their tools, techniques, and procedures (TTPs).

**Roles in IDS:**
1. **Early Warning** — Any access to the honeypot is suspicious (no legitimate user should access it), making detection *automatic* with zero false positives.
2. **Threat Intelligence** — Captures new attack methods and zero-day exploits.
3. **Diversion** — Diverts attackers' time and resources away from production systems.
4. **Evidence Collection** — Logs attacker activity for forensic/legal use.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for roles.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 carries internal choice. Two completely independent alternatives are provided below. Students answer either OR.**

---

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the two principal approaches to Intrusion Detection: **Anomaly Detection** and **Signature-Based Detection**. Compare them on the basis of: detection principle, ability to detect zero-day attacks, false-positive rate, and maintenance overhead.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of a **Host-Based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)**. List any four advantages and two limitations of HIDS.

---

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**Anomaly Detection (Behavior-Based)**

- **Principle:** Build a statistical or ML model of *normal* system/network behavior from training data. Live events are scored against the model; those deviating beyond a threshold are flagged.
- **Methods:** Threshold counting, statistical moments, Markov chains, clustering, neural networks.
- **Strengths:** Detects **zero-day** attacks; adapts to evolving user behavior.
- **Weaknesses:** **High false-positive rate**; computationally expensive; vulnerable to "training poisoning" (attacker gradually shifts the baseline).

**Signature-Based (Misuse) Detection**

- **Principle:** Maintain a database of known attack *signatures* (string patterns, byte sequences, port numbers, rule expressions). Match live events against the database.
- **Methods:** Pattern matching, protocol decoding, stateful rule evaluation.
- **Strengths:** **Low false-positive rate**; high throughput; easy to audit.
- **Weaknesses:** **Cannot detect zero-day** attacks; signature database requires *continuous* updates.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Anomaly Detection | Signature Detection |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Detection principle | Statistical / behavioral baseline | Pattern matching against signature DB |
| Zero-day detection | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| False positive rate | High | Low |
| Maintenance overhead | Model retraining | Signature updates |

*(3 marks for individual explanations + 4 marks for comparison table.)*

---

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**HIDS Architecture (Diagram) — 3 marks**

A HIDS is installed on each individual host and monitors local events such as system calls, log files, file integrity, and process activity.

```
┌─────────────────── HOST MACHINE ───────────────────┐
│                                                     │
│  [Application Layer]  ──┐                           │
│  [System Call Traces]  ──┼──► [Event Preprocessor]  │
│  [Log Files (auth.log)] ─┤                          │
│  [File Integrity (AIDE)]─┘           │              │
│                                     ▼              │
│                          [Detection Engine]         │
│                          (Signature + Anomaly)      │
│                                     │              │
│                                     ▼              │
│                          [Alert / Log Generator]    │
│                          ──► Remote SOC Server      │
│                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Four Advantages of HIDS (2 marks):**
1. Can analyze **encrypted traffic** (after local decryption).
2. Detects **local privilege-escalation** and insider misuse effectively.
3. Operates without needing a network tap.
4. Provides **forensic-grade** evidence from local logs.

**Two Limitations of HIDS (2 marks):**
1. **Performance impact** on the host (CPU, disk, memory).
2. **Evadable** by rootkits that subvert the OS kernel and disable the HIDS itself.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Password Salting** with a suitable example. Show how salting defeats rainbow-table attacks. Compare salted-hash storage with **adaptive hashing (bcrypt / Argon2)** in terms of brute-force resistance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system uses 8-character passwords drawn from 26 lowercase letters. An attacker can attempt $10^{8}$ guesses per second.
   (i) Compute the password entropy in bits.
   (ii) Compute the average time to crack the password.
   (iii) Recommend two changes to make the password resistant to such attacks.

---

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks

**Password Salting (3 marks):**

A **salt** is a *per-user*, *randomly generated* string of fixed length (e.g., 16 bytes) that is **concatenated with the password** *before* hashing:

$$H_{\text{stored}} = \text{Hash}(\text{salt} \,\|\, \text{password})$$

The salt is stored *in plaintext* alongside the hash in the user database.

**Example:**
- User A registers password `"OpenSesame"`.
- System generates salt `S_A = 0xA3F2…91B4` (16 random bytes).
- Stored record: `(S_A, \text{SHA-256}(S_A \,\|\, \text{OpenSesame})) = (0xA3F2…91B4, 0xD4C9…77E1)`.

**Defeats Rainbow Tables (2 marks):**
A rainbow table is a precomputed lookup mapping `hash → password`. Without salt, the table entry for SHA-256("OpenSesame") is the *same* for *every* user. With salt, the attacker must compute a *new* rainbow table *per salt* — i.e., one per user — which is computationally infeasible for large user bases.

**Salted-Hash vs Adaptive Hashing (2 marks):**

| Property | Salted SHA-256 | bcrypt / Argon2 |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Computation per guess | ~ microseconds | ~ 100 ms (tunable) |
| Brute-force cost per password | Cheap | **Expensive** |
| Parallelism defense | None (GPU-friendly) | Argon2 has memory-hard mode |
| Recommendation | Minimum | **Preferred** |

Adaptive algorithms deliberately slow down the hash function by a *cost factor*, so an attacker trying $10^{8}$ guesses/sec is reduced to a tiny fraction.

---

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $L = 8$, $N = 26$ (lowercase letters only), $R = 10^{8}$ guesses/sec.

**(i) Entropy (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= L \cdot \log_2(N) \\
  &= 8 \cdot \log_2(26) \\
  &= 8 \cdot 4.7004 \\
  &= 37.603 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average Crack Time (3 marks):**

Total search space: $S = 26^8 = 208{,}827{,}064{,}576 \approx 2.088 \times 10^{11}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{S}{2R} = \frac{2.088 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{8}} \\
              &= \frac{2.088 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^{8}} \\
              &= 1.044 \times 10^{3} \text{ seconds} \\
              &\approx 17.4 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Two Recommended Changes (2 marks):**

1. **Increase length to 12+ characters** → entropy jumps to $\geq 56$ bits; crack time scales as $26^4 = 4.57 \times 10^{5}$ × larger, i.e., $\sim 1.3$ years.
2. **Expand character pool to include uppercase, digits, and symbols** (94 printable ASCII) → entropy jumps to $8 \cdot \log_2(94) = 52.4$ bits; crack time becomes $\sim 3.5$ days at the same $R$, and $\gg 1$ year with adaptive hashing.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 4 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS** — *detection* ≠ *prevention*. A 1-mark loss in any IDS/IPS question.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention salting's role against rainbow tables** — examiners award a *separate* mark for this linkage.
> 3. **Not showing units** in entropy calculations (always write "bits").
> 4. **Computing total search space as $N+L$ instead of $N^L$** — common arithmetic error.
> 5. **Listing only advantages of HIDS/NIDS without limitations** — full marks require *both* sides.
> 6. **Storing passwords "in plaintext" in the design question** — instant 0 for the security design part.
> 7. **Using `==` for hash comparison** — must use a *constant-time* comparison (timing-attack defense).
> 8. **Forgetting to compute $P(E)$ in Bayes' Theorem** — students often jump to $P(I \vert E)$ directly, losing 1–2 marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Anderson's 3 Intruder Classes:** Masquerader, Misfeasor, Clandestine User — *memorize and contrast*.
- **IDS ≠ IPS:** IDS alerts; IPS blocks. **HIDS = host-local; NIDS = network-wide.**
- **Detection methods:** Anomaly (zero-day, ↑FPR) vs Signature (known, ↓FPR) vs Stateful vs Hybrid.
- **Honeypot = decoy system** with zero legitimate users → automatic anomaly detection with **no false positives**.
- **Password entropy formula:** $E = L \log_2 N$ bits — use this to score password strength.
- **Brute-force crack time:** $T = N^L / (2R)$ seconds.
- **Information Gain:** $\text{Gain} = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ — measures how discriminative a feature is for anomaly detection.
- **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(I \vert E) = P(E \vert I)P(I) / P(E)$ — *always* compute $P(E)$ via total probability.
- **Detection metrics:** TPR, FPR, Precision, F1, Accuracy — recall formulas.
- **Storage hierarchy (best → worst):** Argon2(password + salt + pepper) → bcrypt → salted SHA-256 → unsalted SHA-256 → plaintext (**NEVER**).
- **Salt properties:** per-user, random, ≥ 16 bytes, stored in plaintext next to hash, *defeats rainbow tables*.
- **Adaptive hashing (bcrypt/Argon2/scrypt):** deliberately slow, memory-hard, GPU-resistant.
- **Defense-in-depth:** 2FA + account lockout (3 strikes) + rate limiting + CAPTCHA + secure transport (TLS 1.3).
- **NIST 800-63B modern stance:** min 8 chars; **discourage** forced periodic resets unless compromise is suspected.
- **Constant-time comparison** for hash verification — prevents timing attacks.
- **Clandestine user detection** requires **secure, write-once, remote audit logging** — local logs can be tampered with.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malicious Software - Viruses, Related Threats, Countermeasures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT602 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Malicious Software - Viruses, Related Threats, Countermeasures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Malicious Software (Malware)** is a generic term used to describe any software intentionally designed to cause damage to, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access to a computer system, network, or data. According to the KTU 2024 Information Security syllabus, malware encompasses a broad class of hostile, intrusive, or annoying software programs used by attackers to compromise system confidentiality, integrity, and availability — the foundational **CIA Triad** of information security.

The principal categories of malware covered in Module 4 include:

- **Viruses** — Self-replicating code that attaches itself to legitimate programs
- **Worms** — Self-propagating programs that spread across networks autonomously
- **Trojans (Trojan Horses)** — Malicious code disguised as legitimate software
- **Spyware & Adware** — Software that covertly monitors user activity or displays unwanted advertisements
- **Ransomware** — Malware that encrypts user data and demands payment for decryption
- **Rootkits** — Stealthy tools used to maintain privileged access on a compromised system
- **Logic Bombs** — Code that triggers malicious action upon a specific event or condition
- **Backdoors (Trapdoors)** — Hidden entry points bypassing normal authentication
- **Botnets** — Networks of compromised machines controlled remotely by an attacker

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 explicitly emphasizes the classification of malware, the propagation mechanisms of viruses and worms, and the layered countermeasures (preventive, detective, and corrective controls) required to defend modern computing environments.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **biological viral outbreak** in a city. A real virus (e.g., influenza) cannot reproduce on its own — it needs a *host cell* to attach to, hijack, and force it to produce more copies of the virus. These copies then spread to other cells and other people.

A **computer virus behaves identically**:

- The **host program** is the equivalent of a human cell.
- The **virus code** attaches to this host, just as biological virus attaches to cells.
- **Replication** happens when the infected program runs and copies the virus to other programs.
- **Spreading** requires user action (e.g., opening a file, running an executable) — just like coughing spreads a cold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** A *virus* needs a **host program** and **user action** to spread. A *worm*, on the other hand, spreads **automatically across networks** without human intervention — think of worms as airborne pathogens that float on their own, while viruses need physical contact.

### 1.3 Foundational Metrics & Terminology

The following standard metrics are essential for KTU-level answers:

- **Payload** — The destructive action carried by malware (e.g., data deletion, encryption, credential theft).
- **Infection Vector** — The pathway through which malware enters a system (email, USB, network, downloads).
- **Propagation Mechanism** — How malware replicates and spreads.
- **Trigger Condition** — The event that activates the malicious payload (date, user action, system state).
- **Stealth Technique** — Methods used to hide the malware (encryption, polymorphism, rootkits).
- **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** — Forensic artifacts that confirm a malware infection (file hashes, registry keys, network signatures).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

### 2.1 Classification of Malicious Software — Structural Breakdown

Malware can be classified along **three orthogonal axes** that KTU examiners love to test:

**Axis 1 — Propagation Mechanism**

- Self-replicating (Viruses, Worms)
- Non-replicating but disguised (Trojans)
- Network-coordinated (Botnets)

**Axis 2 — Activation Trigger**

- User-triggered (clicking an infected file)
- Event-triggered (date, time, specific action — Logic Bombs)
- Self-triggered (auto-execution on system boot)

**Axis 3 — Payload Objective**

- Confidentiality breach (Spyware, Keyloggers)
- Integrity destruction (Wipers, File Viruses)
- Availability denial (Ransomware, DoS Bots)
- Privilege escalation (Rootkits, Backdoors)

### 2.2 Detailed Anatomy of a Computer Virus

A virus progresses through **four well-defined phases** (a classic KTU long-answer structure):

1. **Dormant Phase** — Virus is idle, awaiting its trigger condition. It may remain inactive indefinitely.
2. **Propagation Phase** — Virus replicates itself and inserts copies into other host programs or system areas.
3. **Triggering Phase** — The activation event occurs (e.g., a specific date, user action, system condition). The dormant virus becomes active.
4. **Execution Phase** — The payload is delivered. Damage may range from a benign message to complete data destruction.

### 2.3 Virus Categories — High-Yield Reference Table

The following comparison table is a **must-memorize** resource for KTU Module 4.

| Virus Type | Attachment Location | Propagation Method | Typical Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| File Infector (Parasitic) | Attaches to executable files (.exe, .com) | Spreads when infected program is run | Execution of host program |
| Boot Sector Virus | MBR (Master Boot Record) of hard disk or USB | Spreads via infected bootable media | System boot from infected media |
| Macro Virus | Infects document macros (Word, Excel) | Spreads via document sharing | Opening infected document |
| Polymorphic Virus | Varies its code signature each infection | Uses encryption and mutation engines | Execution of host program |
| Metamorphic Virus | Rewrites its own code entirely | Mutation through code transformation | Execution of host program |
| Multipartite Virus | Infects both files and boot sectors | Hybrid propagation (file + boot) | Either file execution or system boot |
| Stealth Virus | Hides from antivirus by intercepting system calls | Uses code mutation and hook techniques | Execution of host program |
| Memory-Resident Virus | Lodges in RAM and infects running programs | Activates whenever OS loads/executes programs | Any program execution post-infection |

### 2.4 Worms vs. Viruses — A Critical KTU Comparison

| Property | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|
| Host program required | Yes | No |
| User action for propagation | Required | Not required |
| Primary spread medium | Files, removable media | Networks (LAN, Internet) |
| Speed of spread | Slow | Extremely fast |
| Memory residence | Sometimes | Always |
| Primary countermeasure | Antivirus scanning | Network segmentation, patching |
| Example | Melissa, ILOVEYOU | Code Red, Blaster, Conficker |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Real-world incidents such as the **WannaCry ransomware worm (2017)** exploited a Windows SMB vulnerability (EternalBlue) and propagated autonomously across 230,000+ computers in a single day — demonstrating the catastrophic speed differential between worm-based and virus-based attacks.

### 2.5 Trojan Horse, Ransomware, and Rootkit Mechanics

**Trojan Horse** — A non-replicating program that *appears* legitimate but contains a hidden malicious payload. Trojans cannot self-replicate; they rely entirely on social engineering. Variants include:

- Remote Access Trojans (RAT) — provide attackers full remote control
- Banking Trojans — steal financial credentials
- Downloader Trojans — fetch additional malware post-installation

**Ransomware** — A class of malware that encrypts user files using strong cryptographic algorithms, then demands payment (typically in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Modern variants use **double extortion**: encryption *plus* threat of public data leak.

**Rootkit** — A stealth toolkit installed *after* initial system compromise, designed to maintain persistent, undetected privileged access. Rootkits operate at different OS layers:

- User-mode rootkits (least stealthy)
- Kernel-mode rootkits (highly stealthy)
- Bootkits (infect MBR/firmware — most stealthy)
- Firmware rootkits (UEFI/BIOS level)

### 2.6 Countermeasures — Layered Defense Architecture

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes a **defense-in-depth** approach. Countermeasures are organized into four functional categories:

**1. Preventive Controls**

- User education and awareness training
- Strict patch management policy
- Application whitelisting
- Email and web filtering gateways
- Disable autorun on removable media

**2. Detective Controls**

- Signature-based antivirus engines
- Heuristic and behavior-based detection
- Host-based Intrusion Detection Systems (HIDS)
- Network-based Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)
- Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solutions

**3. Corrective / Recovery Controls**

- Regular, tested, offline backups (the **3-2-1 rule**: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite)
- Incident response and forensic procedures
- System reimaging and clean reinstallation
- Disaster Recovery Plans (DRP) and Business Continuity Plans (BCP)

**4. General Design Principles (Saltzer & Schroeder)**

- Least privilege
- Defense in depth
- Choke point
- Fail-safe defaults
- Complete mediation
- Economy of mechanism
- Open design
- Separation of privilege
- Least common mechanism
- Psychological acceptability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No single countermeasure is sufficient.** The 2017 WannaCry outbreak was contained primarily by **patching** (closing the SMB vulnerability), while 2014's Sony Pictures breach required **network segmentation** and **behavioral detection** after initial compromise. KTU answers must always advocate *layered* defense.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrices & Detailed Walkthroughs

### 3.1 Walkthrough: The Four Phases of a Virus Lifecycle

This is a frequently asked KTU long-answer question. The following step-by-step logical walkthrough must be memorized and reproduced precisely.

**Step 1 — Dormant Phase (Idle State)**

The virus has successfully entered the system but has not yet activated. During this phase, the virus is essentially *dormant* — it may have infected a host file or a boot sector, but its activation conditions have not been met. The system user is typically unaware of its presence. This phase is optional; some viruses skip it entirely and proceed directly to propagation.

**Step 2 — Propagation Phase (Replication)**

The virus begins identifying new target files or system areas suitable for infection. For a *file infector*, it searches for executables in directories such as `C:\Windows\System32`, `Program Files`, and user-writable paths. For a *macro virus*, it scans recently opened documents. Each newly infected host becomes a vector for further spread. The virus ensures that the original infected program continues to function normally (otherwise, user suspicion would be raised), thereby extending the infection chain.

**Step 3 — Triggering Phase (Activation Event)**

A specific event — known as the **trigger condition** — activates the malicious payload. Common triggers include:

- A calendar date (e.g., the famous *Michelangelo* virus triggered on March 6)
- A system count (e.g., the *Friday the 13th* virus)
- A user action (e.g., opening a specific file type)
- A system state (e.g., low memory, presence of another program)

**Step 4 — Execution Phase (Payload Delivery)**

The payload is executed. Payload severity varies dramatically:

- **Benign** — Display a message, play music, draw graphics
- **Moderate** — Slow system performance, corrupt specific files
- **Severe** — Encrypt files (ransomware), corrupt firmware, exfiltrate data
- **Catastrophic** — Wipe the hard drive, brick the BIOS, destroy hardware controllers

> [!NOTE]
> The lifecycle described above is formally captured in the academic literature as the *dormant → propagation → triggering → execution* model. KTU board answers must use this exact four-stage nomenclature for full marks.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Malware to CIA Triad Violations

The KTU 2024 scheme assesses whether students can map a given malware type to the security property it primarily violates. The following analytical matrix is essential.

| Malware Type | Confidentiality | Integrity | Availability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Virus (File Infector) | Low | High | Medium |
| Worm (e.g., Code Red) | Low | Medium | High |
| Trojan (RAT) | High | High | Low |
| Spyware / Keylogger | High | Low | Low |
| Ransomware | Medium | High | High |
| Rootkit | High | High | Medium |
| Logic Bomb | Low | High | High |
| Botnet Agent (DDoS) | Low | Low | High |
| Backdoor | High | Medium | Low |

**Reading the matrix:** A *Spyware* row shows `High | Low | Low` because spyware primarily steals data (violating confidentiality) but does not typically modify files (integrity) or block service (availability). A *Ransomware* row shows `Medium | High | High` because it encrypts files (destroying integrity) and locks users out (denying availability), while also potentially exfiltrating data (partial confidentiality loss).

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Walkthrough: Polymorphic Virus Operation

A polymorphic virus is the gold standard for testing whether students understand *stealth* techniques. The operational sequence is as follows:

**Step 1 — Initial Infection.** The polymorphic virus infects a host program. The original virus body is encrypted using a randomly generated encryption key.

**Step 2 — Decryptor Stub Insertion.** A small *decryptor stub* is placed in front of the encrypted payload. When the infected host runs, the decryptor stub executes first.

**Step 3 — Decryption at Runtime.** The stub decrypts the main virus body in memory using the embedded key. The decrypted body then performs replication and payload delivery.

**Step 4 — Re-encryption with New Key.** When the virus copies itself to a new host, it generates a *brand new random encryption key*, re-encrypts the body, and inserts a *new* decryptor stub. The new stub is functionally equivalent to the old one but has completely different byte sequences.

**Step 5 — Mutation Engine Use.** Advanced polymorphic viruses use a **mutation engine** that randomizes not only the encryption key but also the decryptor stub's instruction sequence — equivalent to a *genetic reshuffling* of the decryption code itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Traditional Antivirus Fails:** Signature-based antivirus relies on fixed byte-pattern matching. Because every copy of a polymorphic virus has a *different binary signature*, traditional AVs cannot detect them through signature comparison alone. This is why **heuristic analysis** (examining code behavior) and **sandboxing** (executing files in isolated environments) are now standard countermeasures.

### 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough: WannaCry Ransomware Attack (2018 reference scenario)

**Step 1 — Vulnerability Exploitation.** WannaCry exploited **EternalBlue** — an SMBv1 vulnerability (CVE-2017-0144) leaked from the U.S. NSA's toolkit. Systems lacking the **MS17-010** patch were vulnerable.

**Step 2 — Worm-Based Propagation.** Once a host was infected, WannaCry scanned the local network and the public Internet for port 445 (SMB), attempting to exploit the same vulnerability on other machines — making it a true *worm*.

**Step 3 — Encryption Payload.** Upon successful infection, WannaCry encrypted user files using a combination of **RSA-2048** and **AES-128** cryptography. The RSA private key was held by the attackers.

**Step 4 — Ransom Demand.** A ransom note (in 28 languages) demanded **$300–$600 in Bitcoin** within 72 hours, with the threat of permanent decryption-key destruction.

**Step 5 — Containment via Kill Switch.** A security researcher registered a hardcoded domain embedded in WannaCry's code, inadvertently activating a *kill switch* that stopped the worm's spread. However, variants without the kill switch continued to propagate.

**Step 6 — Countermeasure Lessons.** Microsoft released an emergency patch for unsupported systems (Windows XP, Server 2003). The incident highlighted the criticality of **timely patching**, **network segmentation**, and **offline backups**.

### 3.5 Countermeasure Selection — Decision Matrix

The following decision matrix guides the selection of an appropriate countermeasure for each malware class.

| Malware Class | Primary Countermeasure | Secondary Defense | Recovery Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| File Infector Virus | Signature-based AV | Application whitelisting | Reimage from clean backup |
| Boot Sector Virus | Disable autorun, AV boot scan | BIOS write protection | MBR repair tools |
| Macro Virus | Disable macros by default | Document scanning | Restore from backup |
| Network Worm | Patch management, firewalls | Network segmentation | Isolate infected VLAN |
| Trojan | User awareness, AV | Application whitelisting | Credential reset, reimage |
| Ransomware | Offline backups, EDR | Application whitelisting | Restore from backup, do not pay |
| Rootkit | Boot-time AV, TPM verification | Integrity checking (Tripwire) | Hardware re-flash, full reimage |
| Botnet | Network traffic analysis | DNS sinkholing | Bot removal, credential rotation |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree — Malware Taxonomy

The following Mermaid block renders the complete malware taxonomy as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Malicious Software]
    rootA --> classA1[Propagating Malware]
    rootA --> classA2[Non Propagating Malware]
    rootA --> classA3[Stealth Malware]

    classA1 --> propB1[Viruses]
    classA1 --> propB2[Worms]

    classA2 --> nonpB1[Trojan Horses]
    classA2 --> nonpB2[Logic Bombs]
    classA2 --> nonpB3[Backdoors]

    classA3 --> steB1[Rootkits]
    classA3 --> steB2[Botnet Agents]

    propB1 --> virC1[File Infector]
    propB1 --> virC2[Boot Sector]
    propB1 --> virC3[Macro Virus]
    propB1 --> virC4[Polymorphic]
    propB1 --> virC5[Multipartite]
    propB1 --> virC6[Stealth Virus]
    propB1 --> virC7[Memory Resident]

    propB2 --> wormC1[Email Worm]
    propB2 --> wormC2[IM Worm]
    propB2 --> wormC3[Internet Worm]
    propB2 --> wormC4[File Sharing Worm]

    nonpB1 --> trojC1[Remote Access Trojan]
    nonpB1 --> trojC2[Banking Trojan]
    nonpB1 --> trojC3[Downloader Trojan]

    steB1 --> rootC1[User Mode Rootkit]
    steB1 --> rootC2[Kernel Mode Rootkit]
    steB1 --> rootC3[Bootkit]
    steB1 --> rootC4[Firmware Rootkit]
```

### 4.2 Virus Lifecycle Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid block illustrates the four-phase virus lifecycle in detail.

```mermaid
graph LR
    phaseA[Dormant Phase] --> phaseB[Propagation Phase]
    phaseB --> phaseC[Triggering Phase]
    phaseC --> phaseD[Execution Phase]
    phaseD --> phaseA

    phaseA -.waits for.-> trigA[Trigger Event]
    trigA --> phaseC

    phaseB --> actA[Identify Targets]
    actA --> actB[Infect New Hosts]
    actB --> actC[Maintain Host Functionality]

    phaseD --> payA[Deliver Payload]
    payA --> payB[Benign Message]
    payA --> payC[File Corruption]
    payA --> payD[Data Exfiltration]
    payA --> payE[System Destruction]
```

### 4.3 Countermeasure Defense-in-Depth Architecture

The following Mermaid block depicts the layered defense architecture used to counter malware.

```mermaid
graph TD
    layer1[Layer 1 Policy and Awareness]
    layer2[Layer 2 Network Perimeter Defense]
    layer3[Layer 3 Host Based Defense]
    layer4[Layer 4 Application Control]
    layer5[Layer 5 Data Protection and Recovery]

    layer1 --> polA[Security Policy]
    layer1 --> polB[User Training]
    layer1 --> polC[Patch Management]

    layer2 --> netA[Firewall]
    layer2 --> netB[IDS or IPS]
    layer2 --> netC[Email Gateway Filter]
    layer2 --> netD[Web Proxy Filter]

    layer3 --> hostA[Antivirus Engine]
    layer3 --> hostB[Host Based IDS]
    layer3 --> hostC[EDR Solution]

    layer4 --> appA[Application Whitelisting]
    layer4 --> appB[Macro Restriction]
    layer4 --> appC[Sandboxing]

    layer5 --> dataA[3-2-1 Backup Rule]
    layer5 --> dataB[Offline Backups]
    layer5 --> dataC[Incident Response Plan]
    dataC --> dataD[Forensic Analysis]
    dataD --> dataE[System Reimage]
```

### 4.4 Polymorphic Virus Self-Mutation Process

The following Mermaid block visualizes how a polymorphic virus mutates with each infection cycle.

```mermaid
graph LR
    mutA[Original Encrypted Body] --> mutB[Generate New Random Key]
    mutB --> mutC[Re-encrypt Virus Body]
    mutC --> mutD[Generate New Decryptor Stub]
    mutD --> mutE[Mutate Instruction Sequence]
    mutE --> mutF[Assemble New Variant]
    mutF --> mutG[Infect New Host]
    mutG --> mutA
```

### 4.5 Worm Propagation Model (Network Spread)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the network-based propagation of a worm.

```mermaid
graph TD
    wormA[Infected Host A] --> scanA[Scan Random IP Range]
    scanA --> vulnA{Vulnerable Host Found}
    vulnA -->|Yes| explA[Exploit Vulnerability]
    explA --> dropA[Drop Worm Payload]
    dropA --> wormB[Newly Infected Host B]
    wormB --> scanB[Scan Network]
    scanB --> vulnB{Vulnerable Host Found}
    vulnB -->|Yes| explB[Exploit Vulnerability]
    explB --> dropB[Drop Worm Payload]
    dropB --> wormC[Newly Infected Host C]
    vulnA -->|No| skipA[Skip Host]
    vulnB -->|No| skipB[Skip Host]
```
