## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm with respect to propagation mechanism and host dependency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **virus** is a malicious program that requires a *host program* (such as an executable file or document) to attach itself to, and it needs *user action* — like running the infected program — to replicate and spread. In contrast, a **worm** is a self-contained, self-replicating malicious program that propagates *autonomously* across computer networks *without* requiring a host file or user intervention. Worms typically exploit software vulnerabilities to spread, while viruses depend on human-mediated file sharing or media exchange. (3 Marks — Awarded for: (1) Virus needs host + user action, (1) Worm is autonomous, (1) Example or propagation contrast.)

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**What is a Rootkit? Explain the different layers at which a rootkit can operate.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **rootkit** is a collection of malicious software tools installed by an attacker *after* gaining initial system access, designed to maintain persistent, undetected privileged (root/admin) access while hiding its presence and other malicious activity. Rootkits can operate at four layers: (i) **User-mode rootkits** — modify user-level OS APIs and are easiest to detect; (ii) **Kernel-mode rootkits** — modify the OS kernel and are highly stealthy; (iii) **Bootkits** — infect the Master Boot Record or boot sector, surviving OS reinstalls; (iv) **Firmware rootkits** — embed in device firmware (UEFI/BIOS/network cards) and are the most difficult to detect or remove. (3 Marks — Awarded for: (1) Definition, (1) Listing layers, (1) Layer-wise stealth justification.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four phases of a computer virus lifecycle with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the various countermeasures that can be adopted to defend against malware attacks. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Phases of a Computer Virus Lifecycle (7 Marks):**

The lifecycle of a typical computer virus progresses through four well-defined phases. This model is essential to understanding both the timing of damage and the window of opportunity for detection.

**Phase 1 — Dormant Phase:** In this initial stage, the virus has successfully entered the system but remains idle. It has not yet activated because its trigger condition has not been met. Some viruses have no dormant phase at all and proceed directly to propagation. During dormancy, the virus may reside in an infected file, a boot sector, or system memory, lying in wait. (1.5 Marks)

**Phase 2 — Propagation Phase:** The virus begins replicating itself. It identifies potential target files or system areas — for a *file infector*, this means scanning directories for executables; for a *macro virus*, it scans documents; for a *boot sector virus*, it targets the MBR of connected storage devices. The virus inserts a copy of itself into each new target. Critically, it must preserve the *original functionality* of the infected host — otherwise, the user would notice corruption and cease using the program, limiting the spread. (1.5 Marks)

**Phase 3 — Triggering Phase:** A *trigger condition* activates the malicious payload. Triggers can be a specific date (e.g., the *Michelangelo* virus, March 6), a system count, a user action, or a system state. The triggering event is a logical or temporal gate separating the dormant propagation phase from the destructive execution phase. (2 Marks)

**Phase 4 — Execution Phase:** The payload is finally delivered. Payload severity ranges from benign (a message on screen) to catastrophic (deletion of files, encryption of the disk, firmware corruption). The execution phase is the *only* phase in which the user directly observes malicious effects — earlier phases are typically silent. (2 Marks)

> **Diagram Required:** Students should include the four-phase cycle diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes, showing the flow *Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution*, with the possibility of returning to dormant state if no new triggers occur.

---

**(b) Countermeasures Against Malware (7 Marks):**

A robust defense against malware requires a **defense-in-depth** strategy — multiple overlapping layers of control, since no single countermeasure is sufficient. The four functional categories of countermeasures are:

**1. Preventive Controls (1.75 Marks):** These aim to *stop malware from entering* the system. Key measures include:
- User education and awareness training to recognize phishing and social engineering
- Timely patch management to close exploited vulnerabilities
- Application whitelisting (default-deny execution policy)
- Email and web filtering gateways to block malicious attachments and URLs
- Disabling autorun on removable media to block boot sector viruses
- Principle of least privilege to limit malware's blast radius

**2. Detective Controls (1.75 Marks):** These aim to *identify malware presence* as early as possible:
- Signature-based antivirus engines for known threats
- Heuristic analysis to detect previously unseen variants
- Behavior-based detection monitoring for suspicious runtime patterns
- Host-based Intrusion Detection Systems (HIDS)
- Network-based Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)
- Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) for continuous monitoring
- Honeypots to lure and study malware

**3. Corrective / Recovery Controls (1.75 Marks):** These aim to *restore systems* after an incident:
- The **3-2-1 backup rule** — 3 copies, on 2 different media, with 1 offsite (and preferably offline/air-gapped)
- Tested, documented incident response procedures
- System reimaging from known-good baselines
- Forensic analysis to identify the attack vector and scope
- Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Plans (BCP/DRP)

**4. General Security Design Principles (1.75 Marks):** As articulated by Saltzer and Schroeder, these include least privilege, defense in depth, complete mediation, fail-safe defaults, economy of mechanism, open design, separation of privilege, least common mechanism, and psychological acceptability. These design principles ensure that *even if a single control fails*, the overall system remains resilient.

**Conclusion:** Modern malware defense is not a single product but a coordinated, layered, continuously-evolving program involving technology, processes, and people. The WannaCry outbreak of 2017 demonstrated that *patching* alone could have prevented the attack — but *network segmentation* and *offline backups* were equally critical for organizations to recover. (Marks may be awarded for a relevant concluding synthesis.)

---

**Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Classify computer viruses into different types based on their infection targets, with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of polymorphic and metamorphic viruses. Why are they difficult to detect using traditional signature-based antivirus? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Computer Viruses (7 Marks):**

Computer viruses can be classified based on the targets they infect and the techniques they employ.

**1. File Infector (Parasitic) Virus** — Attaches itself to executable files such as `.exe` or `.com`. When the infected program runs, the virus executes and spreads. *Example: Jerusalem Virus.* (1 Mark)

**2. Boot Sector Virus** — Infects the Master Boot Record (MBR) of hard disks or USB drives. The virus activates when the system boots from infected media. *Example: Michelangelo, Stoned.* (1 Mark)

**3. Macro Virus** — Targets the macro programming language embedded in office productivity applications (Microsoft Word, Excel). Spreads through document sharing. *Example: Melissa, Concept.* (1 Mark)

**4. Polymorphic Virus** — Encrypts its payload with a different key on each infection, making the binary signature unique per copy. Uses a *mutation engine* to alter the decryptor stub. (1 Mark)

**5. Metamorphic Virus** — More advanced than polymorphic viruses. Instead of merely encrypting the payload, it *rewrites its own code* with each infection, producing an entirely different — but functionally equivalent — binary. *Example: Zmist.* (1 Mark)

**6. Multipartite Virus** — A hybrid virus that infects both files and boot sectors, combining the propagation methods of file infectors and boot sector viruses. (1 Mark)

**7. Stealth Virus** — Employs techniques to actively hide from antivirus software by intercepting OS system calls and returning false information about infected files. *Example: Brain virus.* (0.75 Mark)

**8. Memory-Resident Virus** — Once executed, it lodges itself in the computer's RAM and infects any program subsequently run on the system. (0.25 Mark)

---

**(b) Polymorphic and Metamorphic Viruses — Working & Detection Challenges (7 Marks):**

**Polymorphic Virus Operation (3.5 Marks):**

A polymorphic virus uses **encryption** plus a **mutation engine** to change its binary representation with each infection. The operational steps are:

1. The original virus body is encrypted using a randomly generated symmetric key.
2. A small *decryptor stub* is prepended to the encrypted body. This stub contains the decryption algorithm and the embedded key.
3. When the infected program runs, the stub executes first, decrypts the virus body in memory, and then transfers control to the decrypted code.
4. When infecting a new host, the virus generates a *new random key*, re-encrypts its body, and constructs a *new* decryptor stub. The mutation engine may also randomize the instruction sequence of the stub (e.g., inserting NOP instructions, reordering equivalent instructions) while preserving its semantic function.

The result: every copy of the virus has a *different byte signature*, defeating naive pattern-matching antivirus engines.

**Metamorphic Virus Operation (3.5 Marks):**

A metamorphic virus goes one step further than a polymorphic virus — it does *not rely on encryption*. Instead, it **rewrites its own code** with each infection. Techniques used include:

- *Instruction substitution* — replacing an instruction with semantically equivalent alternatives (e.g., `ADD` vs. `SUB` with sign-inversion).
- *Register reassignment* — permuting register usage throughout the code.
- *Code permutation* — reordering independent instructions.
- *Dead code insertion* — adding instructions that have no effect.
- *Code shrinking and expansion* — altering the structure while preserving logic.

The result is that successive copies of a metamorphic virus share *no common byte sequences whatsoever* — they are functionally identical but textually different programs. Zmist is a famous example that inserts itself into other programs and completely regenerates its code.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often confuse **polymorphic** with **metamorphic** viruses. *Polymorphic* viruses use **encryption + mutation of decryptor stub**; *metamorphic* viruses use **complete code rewriting**. This distinction is worth 1–2 marks and is frequently tested.
> 2. Do not state that polymorphic viruses are "undetectable." Modern antivirus products use **emulation-based detection** (running the file in a sandboxed VM) to allow the virus to decrypt itself, then scan the decrypted body. Detection is *difficult*, not *impossible*.
> 3. Students sometimes omit the role of the *mutation engine* in polymorphic viruses. Always mention the mutation engine explicitly for full credit.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Malware** is the umbrella term for all intentionally harmful software. Its taxonomy is built on *propagation*, *trigger*, and *payload*.
- A **Virus** requires a *host program* and *user action*. A **Worm** is *autonomous* and *network-based*.
- The four virus lifecycle phases are **Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution** — memorize in this exact order.
- **Boot sector viruses** infect the MBR and spread via removable media. Disable autorun to defend.
- **Macro viruses** propagate through office documents. Disable macros by default.
- **Polymorphic viruses** use encryption + mutation engine; **Metamorphic viruses** use full code rewriting. Both defeat signature-based AV.
- **Trojans** are non-replicating, disguised as legitimate software. Variants include RATs, banking, and downloader Trojans.
- **Ransomware** encrypts files and demands payment. The best defense is *offline, tested backups* and the principle *do not pay*.
- **Rootkits** maintain privileged stealthy access. They operate at user-mode, kernel-mode, boot, and firmware layers. Detection requires boot-time AV and integrity checking.
- **Logic Bombs** trigger on events; **Backdoors** bypass authentication; **Botnets** are coordinated networks of compromised hosts.
- **Countermeasures** must follow **defense-in-depth**: preventive + detective + corrective + design principles.
- The **3-2-1 backup rule** is a recurring KTU answer point: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite/offline.
- The **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — is the evaluation lens. Every malware answer can be mapped to one or more of these properties.
- **WannaCry (2017)** is a favorite KTU case study: worm + ransomware hybrid, exploited EternalBlue, contained by a kill switch.
- Saltzer & Schroeder's **10 design principles** (least privilege, defense in depth, fail-safe defaults, etc.) are standard KTU answer content for the *countermeasures* question.
- For full marks on long answers, always include a **diagram**, **example malware names**, and **explicit countermeasure mappings**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attacks - Types, Countermeasures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBT602 - INFORMATION SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attacks - Types, Countermeasures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attack** is a coordinated, malicious attempt to disrupt the normal traffic of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming the target or its surrounding infrastructure with a flood of internet traffic originating from multiple distributed sources simultaneously. Unlike a traditional Denial of Service (DoS) attack which uses a single attacker, a DDoS leverages a **botnet** — a network of compromised machines (zombies) — to generate massive volumetric or logical load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> A DDoS attack aims to make a machine or network resource unavailable to its intended users by temporarily or indefinitely disrupting services of the host connected to the internet. The distributed nature makes it exponentially harder to mitigate because blocking a single source does not stop the attack.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small bakery that normally serves **50 customers per hour**. Now imagine **50,000 people** simultaneously walking into the bakery, lining up at the counter, asking pointless questions, and blocking the entrance. The bakery's staff cannot serve legitimate customers, the phone lines jam, and the kitchen grinds to a halt.

- The **bakery** = Target web server (e.g., bank, e-commerce site)
- The **50,000 strangers** = Compromised machines in a botnet (often unaware victims)
- The **pointless questions and crowding** = Malicious packets (SYN, UDP, HTTP GET, etc.)
- The **legitimate customers** = Real users who get denied service

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (DoS vs DDoS):**
> - **DoS** = Single attacker → Single target (easy to filter by IP)
> - **DDoS** = Many attackers (botnet) → Single target (hard to filter because traffic looks distributed and legitimate)

### 1.3 Standard DDoS Metrics (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Metric | Symbol/Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth Saturation** | **Gbps (Gigabits per second)** | Volume-based attack measure |
| **Packet Rate** | **Mpps (Million packets per second)** | Protocol attack measure |
| **Requests per Second** | **RPS / QPS** | Application layer measure |
| **Attack Vector** | Categorical | L3/L4/L7 attack type |
| **Botnet Size** | Number of zombies | Magnitude amplification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 DDoS Threat Model: The Operational Lifecycle

A typical DDoS attack follows a structured lifecycle, which is a high-yield concept for KTU theory questions:

1. **Recruitment Phase** — Attacker scans the internet for vulnerable machines, infects them with malware to form a **botnet**.
2. **Command-and-Control (C&C) Phase** — Attacker communicates with bots via IRC channels, HTTP, or P2P networks.
3. **Attack Phase** — Attacker triggers bots to flood the target with malicious packets.
4. **Cover/Retreat Phase** — Bots retreat or rotate IP addresses to avoid detection and forensics.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of DDoS Attacks (KTU Mandatory Diagram)

DDoS attacks are categorized by the **OSI/TCP-IP layer** they target:

#### **Category A: Volume-Based Attacks (Layer 3/4)**
Goal: Saturate the **bandwidth** of the target network.

- **UDP Flood** — Sends massive UDP packets to random ports; target replies with ICMP "Destination Unreachable," exhausting bandwidth.
- **ICMP Flood (Ping Flood)** — Sends ICMP Echo Request packets as fast as possible without waiting for replies.
- **Amplification Attacks** — Exploits vulnerable servers (DNS, NTP, SSDP, Memcached) to reflect and amplify traffic toward the victim. Amplification factor ranges from **×30 (DNS)** to **×51,000 (Memcached)**.

#### **Category B: Protocol Attacks (Layer 3/4)**
Goal: Exhaust **server resources** (connection tables, state memory).

- **SYN Flood** — Exploits TCP 3-way handshake. Attacker sends SYN but never sends final ACK, leaving half-open connections filling the server's connection backlog.
- **Ping of Death** — Sends oversized/fragmented ICMP packets (>65,535 bytes) causing buffer overflow.
- **Smurf Attack** — Sends ICMP Echo Request with spoofed victim IP to a broadcast address; all hosts reply to the victim.
- **Fragmentation Attack** — Sends fragmented packets that cannot be reassembled, exhausting memory.

#### **Category C: Application Layer Attacks (Layer 7)**
Goal: Crash the **web server** or exhaust application-level resources (DB, CPU).

- **HTTP Flood** — Massive legitimate-looking GET/POST requests.
- **Slowloris** — Opens many connections and slowly sends HTTP headers, never completing the request, exhausting connection pool.
- **DNS Query Flood** — Floods DNS resolver with random subdomain queries.

### 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Attack Comparison Matrix

| Attack Type | OSI Layer | Resource Targeted | Detection Difficulty | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| UDP Flood | L3/L4 | Bandwidth | Low | LOIC, UDP Unicorn |
| ICMP Flood | L3/L4 | Bandwidth | Low | Ping of Death scripts |
| DNS Amplification | L3/L4 | Bandwidth (×30 to ×100) | Medium | dnsAmp |
| SYN Flood | L4 | Connection Table | Medium | hping3, Mirai |
| Ping of Death | L3 | Buffer Memory | Low | Custom scripts |
| Smurf Attack | L3 | Bandwidth | Low | smurf.c |
| HTTP Flood | L7 | App Resources | High | Slowloris, LOIC |
| Slowloris | L7 | Connection Pool | Very High | Slowloris |
| DNS Query Flood | L7 | DNS Resolver | High | dnsquery |

### 2.4 Countermeasures Framework (Defense-in-Depth)

The **DDoS countermeasures** are organized into four functional classes per RFC 4732 / KTU syllabus:

#### **A. Prevention (Proactive)**
- **Ingress/Egress Filtering** — Block spoofed source IP addresses at the network edge using BCP 38 / RFC 2827.
- **Rate Limiting** — Cap per-IP request rate at routers/firewalls.
- **Patch Management** — Close amplifiers (open DNS resolvers, NTP monlist).
- **Network Architecture Hardening** — Use Anycast routing to distribute traffic geographically.

#### **B. Detection (Reactive)**
- **Anomaly-Based Detection** — Statistical analysis of traffic baselines; flags deviations (e.g., sudden spike in SYN packets).
- **Signature-Based Detection** — Matches known attack patterns using IDS/IPS (Snort, Suricata).
- **Honeypots** — Decoy servers to study attack patterns.

#### **C. Response (Mitigation)**
- **Blackholing / Sinkholing** — Route all attack traffic to a null route (`null0`). Downside: takes legitimate users offline too.
- **Traffic Scrubbing** — Forward traffic to a DDoS cleaning center (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai) that filters malicious packets.
- **Upstream Filtering** — Coordinate with ISP to filter at the source.

#### **D. Recovery & Forensics**
- **Post-attack traffic analysis** to identify botnet C&C domains.
- **Incident response documentation** for regulatory compliance.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DDoS defense is a cornerstone of:
- **Cloud Service Providers (AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection)**
- **CDN Providers (Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly)**
- **Banking & Financial Networks** (PCI-DSS compliant infrastructure)
- **IoT Security** (Mirai botnet used ~145,000 IoT cameras in 2016 Dyn attack)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In 14-mark theory questions, examiners expect you to: (1) classify the attack by layer, (2) explain the mechanism with a diagram, and (3) propose layered countermeasures. Memorizing the attack-tool mapping gives easy retrieval marks.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Focus:** Since DDoS attacks are a systems/networking problem, the analytical content here focuses on **quantitative attack modeling**, **botnet math**, and **defense capacity calculations** — the kind of numerical reasoning KTU examiners test.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: SYN Flood — Capacity Exhaustion Analysis

**Problem:** A web server has a TCP connection backlog buffer of size **B = 1024 entries**. Each half-open SYN connection consumes **H = 304 bytes** of memory. The SYN cookie defense mechanism uses **S = 64 bytes** per cookie. An attacker controls a botnet generating **R = 500 SYNs per second**, and legitimate traffic timeout is **T = 60 seconds**.

**Step 1 — Model the half-open connection accumulation rate.**

Each SYN occupies a slot for at most **T** seconds before timeout. At equilibrium, the number of half-open slots in use is:

$$N_{half\text{-}open} = R \times T$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$N_{half\text{-}open} = 500 \times 60 = 30{,}000 \text{ slots}$$

**Step 3 — Compare against buffer capacity.**

The server buffer can hold only:

$$B = 1024 \text{ slots}$$

Since:

$$N_{half\text{-}open} = 30{,}000 \gg B = 1024$$

The buffer overflows in time:

$$t_{overflow} = \frac{B}{R} = \frac{1024}{500} = 2.048 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a memory calculation (KTU variation).**

Total memory required by half-open connections without SYN cookies:

$$M_{attack} = N_{half\text{-}open} \times H = 30{,}000 \times 304 = 9{,}120{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 9.12 \text{ MB}$$

Memory required with SYN cookies enabled:

$$M_{cookie} = R \times T \times S = 30{,}000 \times 64 = 1{,}920{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.92 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

The SYN cookie defense reduces memory footprint by a factor of:

$$\text{Reduction Factor} = \frac{H}{S} = \frac{304}{64} = 4.75\times$$

This shows why SYN cookies are an effective countermeasure for SYN flood attacks.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Amplification Factor Calculation

**Problem:** A DNS amplification attack uses 60-byte DNS queries that trigger 400-byte responses. An attacker controls a botnet of **N = 10,000** bots, each capable of sending **q = 50 queries per second**.

**Step 1 — Compute the Amplification Factor (AF).**

$$\text{AF} = \frac{\text{Response Size}}{\text{Request Size}} = \frac{400}{60} \approx 6.67\times$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total attack bandwidth directed at victim.**

Total attack packets per second:

$$P_{total} = N \times q = 10{,}000 \times 50 = 500{,}000 \text{ packets/second}$$

Assuming each response packet is 400 bytes (3,200 bits):

$$B_{attack} = P_{total} \times \text{Response Size in bits}$$

$$B_{attack} = 500{,}000 \times 3{,}200 \text{ bits/sec}$$

$$B_{attack} = 1{,}600{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} = 1.6 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the attacker's actual upstream bandwidth.**

$$B_{attacker} = P_{total} \times \text{Request Size in bits}$$

$$B_{attacker} = 500{,}000 \times 480 \text{ bits/sec} = 240{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps} = 240 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bandwidth amplification ratio.**

$$\text{Bandwidth Amplification} = \frac{B_{attack}}{B_{attacker}} = \frac{1.6 \text{ Gbps}}{240 \text{ Mbps}} \approx 6.67\times$$

This confirms the amplification factor directly translates to bandwidth amplification.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Botnet Infection Growth Model

**Problem:** A botnet starts with **B₀ = 1,000** infected machines and infects new machines at an exponential growth rate of **r = 15% per hour**.

**Step 1 — Define the exponential growth model.**

$$B(t) = B_0 \cdot e^{rt}$$

**Step 2 — Compute size after t = 24 hours.**

$$B(24) = 1{,}000 \cdot e^{0.15 \times 24} = 1{,}000 \cdot e^{3.6}$$

$$B(24) \approx 1{,}000 \cdot 36.598 \approx 36{,}598 \text{ bots}$$

**Step 3 — Compute time to reach 1 million bots.**

$$1{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}000 \cdot e^{0.15t}$$

$$e^{0.15t} = 1{,}000$$

$$0.15t = \ln(1{,}000) = 6.9078$$

$$t = \frac{6.9078}{0.15} \approx 46.05 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

Botnets can scale to attack-magnitude within 2 days — a key reason DDoS defense must be **automated and pre-deployed**.

    S->>A: SYN-ACK (Seq=y2)

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack. How does it differ from a traditional DoS attack?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack is a malicious attempt to disrupt the normal functioning of a target server, service, or network by overwhelming it with a massive flood of internet traffic originating from multiple compromised machines (a botnet) distributed across the internet.

**Key Differences from DoS:**

| Aspect | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| Source count | Single attacker | Many (botnet) |
| Detection | Easy (block 1 IP) | Hard (many sources) |
| Bandwidth | Limited | Massive amplification |
| Mitigation | Simple filtering | Requires scrubbing/CDN |
| Forensic trace | Straightforward | Complex (proxies/reflection) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three types of DDoS attacks and the OSI layer they target.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **UDP Flood** — Targets OSI Layer 3/4 (Network/Transport); exhausts bandwidth by sending UDP packets to random ports.
2. **SYN Flood** — Targets OSI Layer 4 (Transport); exhausts the server's TCP connection backlog by leaving half-open connections.
3. **HTTP Flood / Slowloris** — Targets OSI Layer 7 (Application); exhausts web server resources with legitimate-looking HTTP requests.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Module-end 14-mark questions typically have **part (a) for 7 marks** (descriptive/explanatory) and **part (b) for 7 marks** (analytical/comparison). Internal choice: either OR.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a DDoS attack with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of botnets, command-and-control servers, and reflectors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare volume-based, protocol-based, and application-layer DDoS attacks with suitable examples. For each, propose one specific countermeasure. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Architecture of DDoS Attack:**

A DDoS attack has a hierarchical architecture consisting of four key components:

1. **Attacker (Botmaster)** — The mastermind who initiates and orchestrates the attack. The botmaster does not directly attack the target; instead, it issues commands.

2. **Command-and-Control (C&C) Server** — Acts as the communication relay between the botmaster and the botnet. Common C&C channels include IRC, HTTP, HTTPS, or P2P networks. The botmaster sends an attack command (e.g., "flood 192.168.1.10 on port 80"), and the C&C relays it to all bots.

3. **Botnet (Zombies / Agents)** — A network of compromised machines (PCs, IoT devices, servers) infected with malware. Each bot runs the attack without the owner's knowledge. Modern botnets (e.g., **Mirai**) primarily target vulnerable IoT devices using default credentials.

4. **Reflectors / Amplifiers** — Optional third-party servers (open DNS resolvers, NTP servers, Memcached servers) that reflect and amplify attack traffic. The attacker spoofs the victim's IP and sends a small request to the reflector, which responds with a much larger payload to the victim.

**Attack Flow:**
$$\text{Botmaster} \xrightarrow{\text{Command}} \text{C\&C} \xrightarrow{\text{Relay}} \text{Bots} \xrightarrow{\text{Direct/Spoofed Flood}} \text{Target}$$

**Why botnets are powerful:**
- Distributed sources make **IP-based filtering ineffective**
- Each bot generates only low traffic, hiding the attacker's location
- Traffic appears "legitimate" (real source IPs, real protocols)

---

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | Volume-Based | Protocol-Based | Application-Layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | L3 / L4 | L3 / L4 | L7 |
| **Target Resource** | Network bandwidth | Server state/connection tables | Web app resources (CPU, DB) |
| **Packet Rate Metric** | Gbps | Mpps | RPS / QPS |
| **Example Attacks** | UDP Flood, ICMP Flood, DNS Amplification | SYN Flood, Ping of Death, Smurf | HTTP Flood, Slowloris, DNS Query Flood |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Low (traffic spikes) | Medium | High (mimics legit traffic) |
| **Specific Countermeasure** | Traffic scrubbing / Anycast | SYN Cookies / Rate Limiting | WAF / CAPTCHA / CDN |

**Specific Countermeasures:**

1. **Volume-Based → Traffic Scrubbing via Cloud-based DDoS Protection (e.g., Cloudflare, AWS Shield).** All incoming traffic is redirected to a scrubbing center where malicious packets are filtered using signature and anomaly detection before clean traffic is forwarded to the origin server.

2. **Protocol-Based → SYN Cookies (RFC 4987).** Instead of storing the full TCP connection state upon receiving a SYN, the server encodes state into the SYN-ACK sequence number. Only when the final ACK arrives is the connection materialized, eliminating half-open connection memory exhaustion.

3. **Application-Layer → Web Application Firewall (WAF) with CAPTCHA and JavaScript challenges.** A WAF inspects HTTP headers, cookies, and request patterns to distinguish bots from humans. Slowloris is mitigated by enforcing minimum transfer rates and connection timeouts.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain the SYN Flood attack. Show how it exploits the TCP three-way handshake. How does the SYN Cookie defense mitigate it? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss any four DDoS countermeasures in detail, classifying them into prevention, detection, and response categories. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — SYN Flood with TCP Handshake Exploitation:**

**Normal TCP 3-Way Handshake:**

1. Client → Server: **SYN** (Seq=x)
2. Server → Client: **SYN-ACK** (Seq=y, Ack=x+1)
3. Client → Server: **ACK** (Ack=y+1) → **Connection Established**

**SYN Flood Attack Sequence:**

1. Attacker Bot → Server: **SYN** (Seq=x, **Spoofed Source IP**)
2. Server → Spoofed IP: **SYN-ACK** (waits for ACK — never arrives)
3. Server stores half-open connection in backlog (consumes memory)
4. Attacker repeats with **N different spoofed IPs**
5. Server backlog **fills up** → **legitimate SYNs rejected** → DoS

**Mathematical Analysis:**
If the server backlog = B and attacker sends R SYNs/sec with timeout T:

$$N_{half\text{-}open} = R \times T$$

When $N_{half\text{-}open} \geq B$, the buffer overflows and legitimate users are denied.

**SYN Cookie Defense (RFC 4987):**

Instead of allocating memory for each SYN, the server:
1. Computes a **cookie = Hash(SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort, Seq, Time)** and embeds it in the SYN-ACK sequence number.
2. **Does NOT store any state** in the backlog.
3. Upon receiving the final ACK, the server **recomputes the cookie** from the ACK value minus 1 and validates it cryptographically.
4. Only then does it allocate a full TCP control block (TCB).

This eliminates the half-open connection memory attack vector entirely.

---

**Part (b) — DDoS Countermeasures Classified:**

#### **Prevention Category (Proactive Defense)**

**1. Ingress Filtering (BCP 38 / RFC 2827):**
ISPs configure edge routers to drop packets whose source IP does not belong to the customer's allocated prefix. This blocks IP spoofing, which is the foundation of amplification and Smurf attacks. **Limitation:** Requires global ISP cooperation.

**2. Rate Limiting:**
Routers and firewalls enforce per-source-IP packet/byte rate limits. For example, a router may cap any single IP at 100 SYN/sec. **Limitation:** Distributed attacks with thousands of IPs can still exceed total capacity.

#### **Detection Category (Identifying Attacks in Progress)**

**3. Anomaly-Based Intrusion Detection Systems:**
Tools like **Suricata** or **Zeek** build statistical baselines of normal traffic (e.g., 1,000 SYNs/sec). When traffic exceeds 3σ above baseline, an alert is raised. Machine learning models can improve detection of low-and-slow attacks like Slowloris.

**4. Signature-Based IDS:**
Uses known attack fingerprints (e.g., specific UDP payloads of Mirai botnet) to match against incoming traffic. Fast and accurate for known attacks but useless against zero-day variants.

#### **Response Category (Active Mitigation)**

**5. Blackholing / Sinkholing:**
Routes all traffic to the victim's IP into a **null interface** (`null0` route). The attack is dropped, but so is legitimate traffic. Used as a last resort when traffic exceeds link capacity.

**6. Traffic Scrubbing Centers:**
Specialized DDoS mitigation services (Akamai Prolexic, Cloudflare Magic Transit) that absorb traffic, analyze it using heuristics, and forward only clean traffic to the origin via a **GRE tunnel** or **BGP route advertisement**.

**7. Anycast Routing:**
The victim's IP is advertised from multiple geographically distributed data centers. Attack traffic is split across many locations, diluting its concentration. **Best for:** Large-scale DNS, CDN providers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DoS and DDoS** — Always emphasize the **distributed** nature; mentioning the botnet is mandatory.
> 2. **Not categorizing by OSI layer** — Examiners explicitly look for "Layer 3/4 vs Layer 7" classification in 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Missing diagrams** — A 7-mark question without a diagram typically caps your score at 5/7. Always include a Mermaid/hand-drawn flow.
> 4. **Countermeasure without target mapping** — Don't list generic countermeasures; explicitly map each countermeasure to the attack type it addresses.
> 5. **Ignoring math** — Numerical questions on amplification factor, SYN cookie memory savings, or bandwidth calculations appear regularly. Practice the formulas.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation):**

- ✅ **DoS ≠ DDoS** — Distributed source via botnet is the defining feature.
- ✅ **Three Categories** — Volume-Based (L3/L4 bandwidth), Protocol-Based (L4 state), Application-Layer (L7 app resources).
- ✅ **SYN Flood** — Most cited protocol attack; mitigation = SYN Cookies (stateless).
- ✅ **Amplification Factor** — Ratio of response size to request size; Memcached = **51,200×** (highest known).
- ✅ **Botnet Lifecycle** — Recruitment → C&C → Attack → Retreat.
- ✅ **Four Defense Phases** — Prevention, Detection, Response, Recovery.
- ✅ **BCP 38** — Standard for ingress filtering to prevent IP spoofing.
- ✅ **Scrubbing Centers** — Most effective modern response mechanism.
- ✅ **Anycast Routing** — Geographic traffic dilution.
- ✅ **Real-World Examples** — Mirai botnet (2016 Dyn attack), GitHub 1.35 Tbps attack (2018).
- ✅ **OSI Layer Mapping** — L3 (IP), L4 (TCP/UDP), L7 (HTTP/DNS) — always cite.
- ✅ **Tools to Remember** — LOIC, Mirai, Slowloris, hping3, Snort, Suricata.
- ✅ **Numerical Formulas** — $N_{half\text{-}open} = R \times T$, $\text{AF} = \frac{Response}{Request}$, $B_{attack} = \text{AF} \times B_{attacker}$.
